stories.history_text_short: Chinese abelia is native to central and southern China. R. Brown described it in 1818. One of the first species in its genus to reach Western gardens, introduced by Robert Fortune in 1844, it blooms continuously from midsummer through autumn with fragrant white flowers that attract pollinators late in the season.

Mexican abelia, from the highlands of central Mexico, was described by Decaisne in 1846 based on collections by the French traveler Galeotti. Its long tubular deep-rose flowers and evergreen habit distinguish it from Asian abelias; grown mainly in mild West Coast gardens.

Fragrant abelia or Korean abelia is native to Korea. T.H. Chung described it in 1919. Unlike most abelias which have faint scent, this species produces powerfully fragrant pink-white tubular flowers in spring—one of the most cold-hardy and sweetly fragrant spring-blooming shrubs available.

Single-flowered abelia, native to central China, was described by Robert Brown in 1838 from Robert Fortune's Chinese collections. It is one of the parents (with A. chinensis) of the widely grown hybrid A. x grandiflora. It remains a connoisseur's shrub in Western gardens.

Abelia x grandiflora arose at the Rovelli nursery on Lake Maggiore, Italy, around 1886 as a seedling cross between Abelia chinensis and A. uniflora, two East Asian species. Edward André described it in 1886 in the Revue Horticole.

Okra is native to northeastern Africa (probably Ethiopia) and was carried across the Atlantic by the Atlantic slave trade. Its name comes from the Igbo "okwuru," and it forms the foundation of African diaspora cuisines from Louisiana gumbo to Brazilian caruru.

Musk mallow, or ambrette, is a South and Southeast Asian hibiscus relative whose musky-scented seeds have perfumed luxury fragrances since antiquity. Compact ornamental varieties have lately become popular tropical bedding and container subjects worldwide.

White fir is native to western North America. Gordon described it in 1858 from California material. Long blue-green needles and resin-blistered bark distinguish it. Heat- and drought-tolerant unusual among firs, it is a popular landscape conifer and Christmas tree across the US.

Fraser fir is endemic to high peaks of the southern Appalachians. Described by Poiret in 1810, named for Scottish plant collector John Fraser. Decimated in the wild by the invasive balsam woolly adelgid since the 1950s, it remains the most popular premium Christmas tree in the eastern United States.

Nordmann fir (Caucasian fir) is native to the western Caucasus and northeastern Turkey. Spach described it in 1841, named for Finnish biologist Alexander von Nordmann who collected it. Its lustrous dark green needles, symmetrical form, and excellent needle retention have made it Europe's dominant premium Christmas tree.

Velvetleaf is an Asian plant introduced to North America in colonial times as a fiber crop. Named for the ancient Greek botanist Theophrastus, it was grown for twine and paper fiber but escaped to become one of the worst Midwestern US corn and soybean weeds.

Field maple or hedge maple is native to Europe and western Asia. Cultivated since Roman times for hedgerow boundaries, Linnaeus described it in 1753. Its fine-grained wood was the preferred material for medieval wooden mazers (drinking bowls) and harps.

Vine maple is native to the Pacific Coast from British Columbia to northern California. Named by Thomas Nuttall in 1838, Indigenous peoples along the coast used its pliable stems for basketry, bows, and drum frames, and it provides stunning autumn color in regional forests.

Paperbark maple is native to central China. Discovered by Augustine Henry around 1888 and introduced by Ernest Wilson in 1901, its spectacular exfoliating cinnamon-colored papery bark made it one of the most prized specimen trees in Western horticulture.

Full moon maple is native to Japan and the southern Kuril Islands. Described by Thunberg in 1784, its deeply lobed rounded leaves earned the common name. Cultivated in Japanese gardens for centuries, it complements Acer palmatum as a choice specimen for shaded borders.

Acer negundo has the broadest native range of any North American maple, extending across most of the continent from coast to coast. Linnaeus described it in 1753. Unique among maples, it has compound pinnate leaves with 3-9 leaflets rather than simple lobed leaves—the only North American.

Acer palmatum is native to Japan, Korea, and parts of China. It has been cultivated in Japan for over a thousand years and valued in gardens since at least the Heian period, but the formal horticultural tradition of naming and propagating cultivars ("momiji" in Japanese) emerged in the Edo period.

Norway maple is native to central and eastern Europe and was widely planted across North American cities in the 20th century as a tough urban street tree. It has now become a serious invasive in northeastern US forests, where its dense shade prevents native seedling regeneration.

Sycamore maple is native to central Europe and western Asia. Cultivated since Roman times, it spread widely across Britain after medieval introduction. The timber has been the preferred wood for violin backs since the 16th century, including Stradivari instruments.

Red maple is native to eastern North America and is one of the continent's most abundant native trees. Its sap makes maple syrup (though less sugar-rich than sugar maple), and it is uniquely and severely toxic to horses — wilted or dried leaves have caused lethal hemolytic anemia.

Sugar maple is the source of maple syrup and maple sugar — a practice learned by European colonists from Indigenous peoples including Algonquian, Ojibwe, and Iroquois tribes. It is the national tree of Canada, whose red-leaf flag features a stylized maple leaf adopted in 1965.

Balkan yarrow, native to rocky slopes of southeastern Europe, was described by Sibthorp and Smith in the late 1700s during their Greek botanical expedition. It has become a key parent in modern bright-yellow yarrow hybrids such as 'Moonshine' and 'Coronation Gold'.

Fernleaf yarrow is native to the Caucasus and Central Asia. Introduced to European gardens in the 19th century, Alan Bloom's 1937 cultivar 'Gold Plate' standardized it as the premier tall yellow border yarrow used globally.

Yarrow is named for the Greek hero Achilles, who reputedly used it to stop the bleeding of his soldiers' wounds at the Siege of Troy. Used as a wound herb from the Neolithic onward — yarrow pollen was found with the 60,000-year-old Neanderthal burial at Shanidar.

Sneezewort is native to Europe and western Asia. The common name and specific epithet ptarmica come from Greek meaning to cause sneezing, referencing historical use of dried leaves as snuff. The double cultivar 'The Pearl' from the 1880s remains popular.

Achillea tomentosa grows on dry, rocky slopes and alpine meadows from Spain through the Alps to the Balkans. Linnaeus described it in Species Plantarum (1753). Like other yarrows, its fern-like silvery leaves were stuffed into battlefield wounds — a use reflected in the genus name.

Vanilla leaf is a delicate Pacific Northwest forest groundcover whose dried foliage emits a sweet vanilla-hay scent. Indigenous peoples hung bundles of the dried leaves as insect repellent in dwellings, and settlers adopted the practice in cabins and outbuildings.

Chinese monkshood is highly toxic and has been used in traditional Chinese medicine as fu zi for over 2000 years, carefully processed to reduce toxicity. Its alkaloids rank among the most poisonous of any garden plant.

Sweet flag is a wetland plant with disputed native range — possibly Asian and introduced worldwide, or truly circumboreal. Its sweet-spicy aromatic rhizome has been used medicinally and ceremonially across Eurasia and North America for over 2,000 years; in North America many Indigenous peoples including the Cree called it "wiikenh" and considered it sacred medicine.

Kiwifruit is native to central China ("mihoutao" — monkey peach). New Zealand fruit farmer Isaac Esby Hayward Wright and nurseryman Alexander Allison developed the Hayward cultivar in the 1930s, and 1950s rebranding as "kiwifruit" replaced the original name "Chinese gooseberry."

Variegated kiwi vine is native to the Russian Far East, Korea, China, and Japan. Maximowicz described it in 1857. Male plants develop dramatic pink-and-white splashed leaves in spring—a feature rare in deciduous vines. Small fragrant white flowers and occasional small edible kiwi fruits follow on female plants.

Desert rose is native to Sahel Africa and the Arabian Peninsula, where indigenous peoples used its toxic latex to poison arrow and spear tips for hunting. Intensive 20th- and 21st-century Thai and Taiwanese breeding transformed it into a spectacular collector's flowering caudex plant.

The Delta Maidenhair Fern is native to tropical South America. Its delicate black-stemmed fronds made it a signature Victorian conservatory plant; its reputation for being temperamental indoors is equally long-standing. Non-toxic to pets.

Tree aeonium is native to Morocco and the Canary Islands. Collected by 18th-century European expeditions and popularized by British Victorian succulent enthusiasts, it became iconic of Mediterranean and California dry gardens through the 20th-century drought-tolerant landscape movement.

'Zwartkop' is the black-leaved cultivar of Aeonium arboreum, selected in the late 20th century and popularized by Australian and Dutch nurseries. Its name means 'black head' in Dutch. It became iconic in 1990s-2010s 'dark plant' garden design.

Aesculus flava (formerly A. octandra) grows in rich cove forests of the southern Appalachians, with its best development in North Carolina and Tennessee. Scottish-English botanist William Aiton, director of Kew Gardens, formally described it in 1789 in Hortus Kewensis.

Ohio buckeye is native to the Midwest. Described by Carl Ludwig Willdenow in 1809, it is the state tree of Ohio (adopted 1953) and namesake of The Ohio State University's athletic teams. Indigenous peoples used ground seeds to stun fish after careful preparation.

Red buckeye is native to the southeastern US. Described by Linnaeus in 1753, its brilliant red 6-10 inch flower panicles in spring make it hugely attractive to ruby-throated hummingbirds, whose migration northward aligns with its bloom period.

Florida leucothoe, now placed in Agarista, is the same graceful evergreen shrub as the Leucothoe populifolia of older floras. Its taxonomic reassignment in 1984 separated it from the mountain-laurel relatives, though gardeners still know it by the traditional name.

Anise hyssop is a native of northern prairies, long used by Cree and Ojibwe peoples as a cough tea and ceremonial incense. European botanists described it in the 1780s. It remains a top pollinator and culinary herb in modern gardens.

Sunset hyssop is a southwestern US and Mexican native of rocky hillsides, described by Edward Greene in 1881. Apache and Tarahumara herbalists used related Agastaches for digestion; modern gardens value its orange-rose flowers and hummingbird appeal.

Agave americana is native to Mexico and adjacent parts of Arizona, New Mexico, and Texas. It has been cultivated by Indigenous Mesoamerican peoples for at least 7,000 years, providing strong sisal-like fiber for rope, cloth, sandals, and paper (amatl).

Fox tail agave is a central Mexican species unusual among agaves for lacking terminal spines. Its soft architectural rosettes and dramatic arching flower stalks made it the defining agave of mid-20th century California and Mediterranean landscape modernism.

Smooth agave or dwarf century plant is a cultivated agave of uncertain wild origin, possibly native to Mexico. Jacobi described it in 1867 from cultivated material. Forms 60-90 cm symmetrical rosettes of smooth blue-green leaves. 'Variegata' with yellow-margined leaves is widely grown.

Twin-flowered agave is a western Mexican endemic. Tagliabue described it as Littaea geminiflora in 1816; Ker Gawler transferred it to Agave in 1817. Forms dense 60-90 cm rosettes of 100-200 very narrow dark green flexible leaves resembling a pincushion. Paired flowers give species name.

Thorn-crested agave or lechuguilla-relative is a Mexican and Texas native. Schiede described it in 1829. Forms clumping 40-70 cm rosettes of dark green leaves with prominent central pale stripe and sharp marginal teeth. 'Quadricolor' variegated cultivar is widely grown.

Whale's tongue agave is a northeastern Mexican endemic. Described by Greg Starr and José Villarreal in 2002, one of the most recently named ornamental agaves. Forms 90-120 cm symmetrical rosettes of broad cupped powder-blue leaves resembling a whale's tongue. Hardy to 10°F.

Queen Victoria agave is native to the Chihuahuan Desert, named in 1875 to honor the British queen. Its perfectly symmetrical small rosette and white-lined dark green leaves have made it one of the most-collected agaves — and among the most illegally harvested.

Octopus agave is a Mexican native of the Sierra Madre Occidental. Berger described it in 1913, naming it for French seed house Vilmorin. Forms 1-1.2 meter rosettes of arching unarmed twisted gray-green leaves resembling octopus tentacles. Produces prolific bulbils along flowering stalk.

Weber agave is a Mexican species long known only in cultivation. Cels ex J.F. Poisson described it in 1901, honoring German naturalist Friedrich Weber. Forms large 1.2-1.8 meter symmetrical rosettes of smooth broad blue-green leaves with few marginal teeth. Used historically for mezcal.

Flossflower is a Mexican and Central American annual in the daisy family bearing fluffy powder-blue, lavender, or white flower clusters. Named for English physician William Houstoun, it has been a staple of American bedding schemes since the 19th century.

Aglaonema commutatum is native to the Philippines and Sulawesi, but the genus as a whole has been cultivated across East Asia for at least a millennium. In Chinese tradition Aglaonema plants are associated with prosperity and long life and are common gifts at business openings and lunar new year.

Painted Chinese evergreen is an Indonesian (Sumatra and Nias) native. Kunth described it in 1841 as Aglaonema pictum. Forms compact clumps of oval leaves with camouflage-like dark green, pale green, and silver-green patches. 'Tricolor' with three-tone patches is highly collected.

Aglaonema rotundum is a compact evergreen aroid in the family Araceae endemic to the rainforests of northern Sumatra, Indonesia, particularly around the Toba region. English botanist Nicholas Edward Brown formally described the species in 1914 based on specimens introduced to European horticulture.

Corncockle is native to Europe and western Asia. For millennia a common grain-field weed of European wheat and rye, whose toxic seeds contaminated bread flour and caused chronic "corncockle poisoning" in rural populations. Modern grain-cleaning technology has effectively eliminated the species from commercial agriculture, and it is now endangered in its native range.

Ailanthus altissima, tree of heaven, is a fast-growing deciduous tree native to China introduced to Europe in 1751 and America in 1784 as an ornamental. It now ranks among the worst invasive trees in temperate regions, spreading aggressively from seed and root suckers and releasing allelopathic chemicals that suppress competing plants.

Geneva bugle or blue bugle is a European native described by Linnaeus in Species Plantarum 1753. Forms clumping rosettes without stolons and produces erect 15-25 cm blue flower spikes. Named for Geneva, Switzerland. Non-invasive alternative to Ajuga reptans.

Pyramidal bugle is a European native in the mint family. Linnaeus described it in Species Plantarum 1753. Forms tight rosettes with blue flower spikes resembling a pyramid. 'Metallica Crispa' is a popular cultivar with crinkled bronze-purple foliage.

Bugleweed is native to Europe, western Asia, and North Africa. Its spikes of blue flowers and low carpeting evergreen habit made it a traditional European medicinal and ornamental plant since medieval times. Dark-foliage cultivars "Atropurpurea" and "Black Scallop" made it a staple of contemporary mixed groundcover plantings.

Chocolate vine or five-leaf akebia is native to Japan, Korea, and China. Decaisne described it in 1839. Its chocolate-purple spring flowers with vanilla-chocolate-spice fragrance and bizarre sausage-shaped purple fruits are exotic curiosities. Unfortunately, the species has become invasive in the eastern United States.

Albizia julibrissin, mimosa or silk tree, is a deciduous tree native from Iran through central Asia to Korea and Japan. André Michaux brought it to his Charleston, South Carolina garden in 1785, and by 1800 it had spread through southern US horticulture. Beloved for its pink silky powder-puff flowers, it now escapes into roadsides and old fields.

Alcea rosea (long known as Althaea rosea) is probably native to southwestern China, where it has been cultivated for centuries as "shu kui" (蜀葵, "Sichuan mallow"). The prevailing etymology holds that it was introduced to the Middle East and then to Europe during or shortly before the Crusades.

'Nigra' is the oldest black hollyhock cultivar in continuous commerce, documented in European gardens since the 17th century. Thomas Jefferson grew it at Monticello. It has survived primarily through seed-saving cottage gardeners over 400 years.

Golden trumpet is native to tropical South America, particularly the Guiana Shield and northern Brazil. The species epithet "cathartica" reflects its historical use as a purgative — all parts of the plant contain cathartic compounds that cause severe diarrhea and are not safe for self-medication.

Alliaria petiolata, garlic mustard, is a biennial native to Europe and western Asia introduced to North America in the 1860s as a culinary herb. Its garlic-scented leaves were used in medieval European cooking, but in North American forests it releases allelopathic chemicals that disrupt mycorrhizal fungi essential to native tree seedlings.

The onion is one of humanity's oldest cultivated crops, documented in Egypt at least 5,000 years ago. Pyramid workers received onion-garlic-radish rations, Egyptian priests considered them sacred, and Alexander the Great fed his armies onions to enhance aggression and stamina.

Allium christophii (syn. A. albopilosum) is native to dry stony slopes of eastern Turkey, northern Iran, and Turkmenistan. It was named in honor of a 19th-century German consul named Christoph. Russian and English bulb dealers introduced it to Western gardens around 1900.

Welsh onion or scallion is an East Asian cultigen unknown in the wild. Linnaeus described it in Species Plantarum 1753. Forms perennial clumps of hollow tubular green leaves used as scallions. Cultivated in East Asia for over 2000 years as a cornerstone vegetable.

Turkestan onion is native to the Karatau mountains of Kazakhstan. Described by Regel in 1875, its unique broad paired blue-gray leaves and 4-inch pale pink flower globes made it one of the most distinctive ornamental alliums for rock gardens.

The leek is the national emblem of Wales, worn on hats at the 640 CE Battle of Heathfield to distinguish Welsh from Saxon soldiers according to legend. Ancient Greek and Egyptian cultivation predates written history, and the leek is still celebrated on St. David's Day each March 1.

Garlic is a cultivated species unknown in the wild, originating in central Asia and cultivated for over 7,000 years. From Pharaonic Egyptian rations to Roman soldier's bread to Mediterranean folk medicine, it is among the oldest and most culturally universal herb-medicines.

Chives are the smallest cultivated Allium, native to Eurasia and North America. Used by the ancient Chinese as early as 3000 BCE, they reached medieval European gardens through Roman introduction and remain the most delicate onion-family herb, essential to French fines herbes.

Tumbleweed onion is native to the eastern Mediterranean from Israel through Syria and Turkey to Libya. German botanist Joseph Gerhard Zuccarini described it in 1843, honoring naturalist Gotthilf Heinrich von Schubert. Its starburst flower heads up to 18 inches across are the largest of any allium.

Drumstick allium is native across Europe and the Mediterranean to western Asia. Described by Linnaeus in 1753, its distinctive egg-shaped flower heads transitioning from green to maroon-purple made it a favorite of naturalistic gardeners including Piet Oudolf.

Allium tuberosum, commonly called garlic chives, Chinese chives, or jiucai in Mandarin, is a clumping perennial in the onion subfamily of the asparagus family (Amaryllidaceae) native to a broad area of East Asia including northern and central China, Mongolia, and the Russian Far East, though long c.

Ramsons or wild garlic is a European native bulbous perennial. Linnaeus described it in Species Plantarum 1753. Forms enormous spring carpets of broad green leaves with white starry flower umbels in European deciduous woodlands. Widely foraged and culinarily prized for garlicky flavor.

Despite its name, this plant is not from the Amazon — it is a hybrid of two Southeast Asian Alocasia species created in a Miami nursery in the 1950s. The name 'amazonica' was a marketing invention. Strongly toxic to pets due to calcium oxalate.

Giant taro or elephant ear taro is a widespread Austronesian cultigen. Linnaeus described it as Arum macrorrhizon in 1753; G. Don transferred it to Alocasia in 1839. Forms massive plants with 1-meter arrow-shaped leaves on thick stems. Long cultivated across Pacific Islands as starchy food source.

'Polly' is a dwarf cultivar of the horticultural hybrid Alocasia × amazonica (believed to be A. longiloba × A. sanderiana) rather than a species. Widely marketed as African mask plant despite Southeast Asian parentage. Compact arrow-shaped glossy black-green leaves with white venation.

Black velvet alocasia or 'Black Velvet' is a Borneo endemic. A. Hay described it in 1998. Dwarf species with small rounded heart-shaped leaves that are extraordinarily dark velvet-black on upper surface with silvery-white veins and purple undersides. One of the most striking small aroids.

Sander's alocasia or kris plant is a critically endangered endemic of Mindanao Island, Philippines. W. Bull described it in 1894 honoring Sander & Co. nursery. Distinctive arrow-shaped glossy deep green leaves with silvery-white venation and scalloped margins resembling a kris dagger.

Native to a handful of Philippine islands, Alocasia zebrina is famous for its tiger-striped petioles. Protected from wild collection in the Philippines since the late 20th century, most plants in the trade come from Dutch tissue culture.

The Lace Aloe is native to South Africa and Lesotho. Recently reclassified as Aristaloe aristata (sole species of its genus), it is small enough for indoor cultivation and more cold-tolerant than most aloes.

Short-leaved aloe is a South African dwarf species from the Western Cape lowlands. Its compact clumping rosettes and dramatic seasonal color shift to red-orange under sun stress have made it a mainstay of California and Mediterranean succulent gardens.

Soap aloe or zebra aloe is a southern African native, one of the most widespread wild aloes. Allioni described it in 1773. Forms 30-45 cm rosettes of triangular green leaves with prominent pale H-shaped spots. Coral-pink to orange flat-topped flower heads. Widely naturalized in Mediterranean climates.

Aloe plicatilis, the fan aloe, is one of the most distinctive aloes—a slow-growing branching small tree endemic to winter-rainfall sandstone slopes of the Western Cape of South Africa from the Franschhoek Mountains to the Elandskloof.

Spiral aloe is endemic to a tiny area of the Lesotho highlands. Its perfect five-rank spiral rosette — clockwise or counterclockwise — has made it one of the world's most coveted aloes, and over-collection has driven it to Critically Endangered status under CITES Appendix I.

Hardy aloe or coral aloe is a South African montane species. Haworth described it in 1825. Now often classified as Aloiampelos striatula following Grace et al. 2013. Forms sprawling 1-2 meter shrubby stems with slender green leaves and yellow flower spikes. One of the hardiest aloes.

One of humanity's longest-cultivated medicinal plants, Aloe vera appears in Sumerian tablets from around 2100 BCE and the Egyptian Papyrus Ebers (~1550 BCE), describing its use for burns and wounds. Best evidence points to Arabian Peninsula origin, though it now exists almost entirely as a cultigen with uncertain wild range.

Lemon verbena is a South American shrub carried to Europe by Spanish colonists in the 17th century. Its intense fresh-lemon fragrance — stronger than any other lemon-scented herb — made it central to French and Spanish herbal liqueurs, teas, and perfumery.

Whitebrush, or beebrush, is an aromatic shrub of the Chihuahuan Desert and Texas brush country whose tiny white flowers exude an intense vanilla-almond fragrance. It is a premier honey plant and wildlife browse across its range.

Shell ginger is native to East Asia (southern China through Japan's Ryukyu Islands). In Okinawan culture it is called "getto" (月桃) and used to wrap traditional "muchi" rice-cake confections. Okinawan longevity researchers have studied its leaf-wrapping tradition as one of several local dietary factors associated with Okinawa's historical status as a "blue zone" of exceptional longevity.

Ligtu lily is native to Chile, where its edible starchy roots were a traditional Mapuche food. The Ligtu hybrids developed in 1920s-30s England by Harold Bowles and others became the foundation of modern Peruvian lily cut flower breeding.

Love-lies-bleeding is likely native to the Andes of Peru, Bolivia, and northern Argentina. Domesticated over 4,000 years ago and a staple grain of the Inca Empire. The plant produces extraordinary dramatic drooping 30-60 cm tassel-like chenille-textured flower spikes in deep crimson-red, which gave it its evocative Victorian common name.

Amaranthus hybridus is native to tropical and subtropical America and is a key ancestor or close relative of the cultivated grain amaranths (A. caudatus, A. cruentus, A. hypochondriacus). Linnaeus described it in Species Plantarum (1753).

Amaranthus palmeri is native to the arid southwestern US and northern Mexico. Sereno Watson of Harvard described it in 1877, naming it for the botanist Edward Palmer who collected extensively across the Southwest.

Amaranthus retroflexus is native to central North America, especially the central plains and northern Mexico, and has spread globally since Columbian contact. Linnaeus described it in Species Plantarum (1753).

Spiny amaranth is a pantropical summer-annual native to the American tropics. Linnaeus described it in 1753. Unlike other pigweeds, it bears sharp paired spines at leaf bases, making mechanical weeding painful. It is a major tropical vegetable called 'chaulai' in India.

Joseph's coat (also called fountain plant or tampala) is native to tropical Asia — India, Sri Lanka, and Southeast Asia. Grown for spectacular multicolored top foliage — mature plants display flaming rosettes of yellow, orange, red, and green leaves. Young leaves widely eaten as a cooked green in India, Southeast Asia, and China; sold as "een choy," "tampala," or "red amaranth."

Ambrosia artemisiifolia, common ragweed, is an annual native to North America whose wind-blown pollen is a leading cause of hay fever. Once a minor prairie plant, it spread aggressively with agriculture and now grows on disturbed soils worldwide, especially in Europe and East Asia where it is an invasive weed.

Ambrosia trifida, giant ragweed, is a towering annual native to North American floodplains that can reach four meters in a season. Archaeological evidence shows Indigenous peoples cultivated it for its oily seeds before maize arrived. Today it is a major agricultural weed and a significant contributor to late-summer hay fever.

Allegheny serviceberry is native to eastern North America. Described by Nathaniel Lord Britton and Joseph Nelson Rose in 1903, it produces early white spring flowers, sweet edible dark-purple fruit, and exceptional red-orange autumn foliage, making it a top small native tree.

Apple serviceberry is a hybrid (A. arborea x A. laevis) widely grown in North American landscapes. Its white spring flowers bloom when shadbush runs were historically returning up East Coast rivers, giving the genus its common names "shadbush," "serviceberry," and "Juneberry."

Ammi majus, bishop's weed or bullwort, has a 3000-year medicinal history. Ancient Egyptians used its seeds to treat vitiligo, documented in the Ebers Papyrus. Modern pharmacology isolated psoralens from it in the 1940s for photochemotherapy.

Porcelain berry is native to East Asia. Trautvetter described it in 1884. Introduced to North America in the 1870s as a decorative vine for its spectacular speckled blue-to-purple berries, it is now a serious invasive species across the eastern United States and should not be planted.

Amsonia hubrichtii occurs naturally only along rocky streambeds in the Ouachita Mountains of central Arkansas and eastern Oklahoma. The self-taught naturalist Leslie Hubricht collected the first specimens in 1942, and Robert E.

Amsonia tabernaemontana grows in moist woodlands, streambanks, and meadow edges across the eastern and central United States. Thomas Walter described it in Flora Caroliniana (1788), naming the genus for John Amson, a Virginia physician.

Sand rose is a small South African succulent with purple-pink rosettes, cloaked in white cobweb-like hairs between leaves. Its ability to grow in almost any well-drained mix has made it a popular miniature collector's succulent.

Broomsedge bluestem is a widespread eastern North American native grass. Linnaeus described it in Species Plantarum 1753. Warm-season clumping grass reaching 60-120 cm, foliage turns bright coppery-orange in fall and persists through winter. Larval host for several native skippers.

Grecian windflower, native to southeast Europe and Turkey, was described by Schott and Kotschy in 1854. Its bright daisy-like flowers in shades of blue, pink, and white made it a 20th-century Dutch bulb-trade staple for drift plantings under deciduous trees.

The Poppy Anemone is native to the Mediterranean basin. In Christian legend its red flowers sprang from Christ's blood at the crucifixion, and in Greek myth from the blood of Adonis. Israel designated it the national flower in 2013.

Wood anemone, native across Europe, was described by Linnaeus in 1753. Long a symbol of spring in European folklore, it carpets ancient deciduous woodlands and has produced many prized British woodland-garden cultivars, including the double-flowered 'Vestal'.

Dill is native to the eastern Mediterranean and southwest Asia, cultivated for at least 5,000 years. Central to Scandinavian and Eastern European cuisine (gravlax, pickles, borscht), and used medicinally since Egyptian and Greek antiquity for digestion — the name "dill" comes from Old Norse "dilla," to soothe.

Garden angelica is a northern European native biennial in the carrot family. Linnaeus described it in Species Plantarum 1753. Enormous 2-meter plants with huge umbels of greenish-yellow flowers. Stems and roots used historically in liqueurs (Chartreuse, Bénédictine, gin) and as candied confection.

Korean angelica or purple parsnip is a Korean, Japanese, and Chinese native. Nakai described it in 1917. Striking ornamental with deep burgundy-purple domed flower umbels rising 1.5-2 meters tall in late summer. Roots (danggui) used in traditional Korean and Chinese medicine.

Summer snapdragon, or angelonia, is a heat-loving Mexican and Caribbean perennial grown as an annual for its tall spikes of orchid-like flowers in purple, pink, and white. Ball Seed's 2001 Serena series transformed it into a mainstream US summer bedding staple.

Anisacanthus quadrifidus, commonly called flame acanthus, Wright's desert honeysuckle, or hummingbird bush, is a deciduous shrub native to south Texas, the Edwards Plateau, and northern Mexico from Nuevo León to San Luis Potosí.

Dyer's chamomile is native to Europe and western Asia. Cultivated since medieval times for yellow wool dye extracted from its flowers, Linnaeus described it in 1753. It remains a cottage garden favorite for long summer bloom.

Native to the cloud forests of Colombia and Ecuador, the Flamingo Flower was introduced to Europe in 1876 by French botanist Édouard André. Its glossy red spathe is one of the longest-lasting cut flowers in commerce.

Endemic to a small limestone region of Chiapas, Mexico, Anthurium clarinervium is grown for its velvety heart-shaped leaves traced by bright white veins. It has been listed as a near-threatened species due to habitat loss.

Crystal anthurium is a Panamanian and Colombian rainforest epiphyte. Linden & André described it in 1873. Prized for enormous velvety heart-shaped dark green leaves with striking silvery-white pinstripe venation resembling crystalline facets. Slow-growing collector's foliage anthurium.

King anthurium is a Colombian rainforest epiphyte. M.T. Masters described it in 1876 honoring the Veitch Nursery of England. Produces enormous pendant corrugated strap-shaped leaves that can reach 1.5-2 meters long in maturity. One of the most dramatic large-leaved aroids.

Common snapdragon is native to the western Mediterranean — Spain, France, Portugal, Morocco. Cultivated since ancient Roman times, mentioned in 1st-century Pliny the Elder. The hinged "dragon mouth" flower requires bumblebee strength to pry open — a textbook example of specialized bee pollination.

Groundnut, or hopniss, is a North American twining legume whose starchy tubers were a staple food of many eastern Indigenous peoples. It reportedly sustained the Pilgrims through their first winters and was later studied as a potential domesticated crop.

Baby sun rose is a South African trailing succulent with heart-shaped leaves and small magenta daisy-like flowers. Now classified as Mesembryanthemum cordifolium, it is a Mediterranean and California coastal ground cover widely sold at garden centers.

Eastern red columbine is an eastern North American native described by Linnaeus in 1753. Iroquois, Menominee, and other tribes used it as a love charm and heart stimulant. It was one of the earliest American plants grown in European gardens after 1635.

Golden columbine is a native of rocky canyons in the southwestern US and northern Mexico, described by Asa Gray in 1873. Its long-spurred, bright yellow flowers made it a prized parent in the McKana Giants hybrid strain of the 1950s.

European columbine has been grown in gardens since the Middle Ages, appearing in medieval herbals and Flemish religious paintings as a symbol of the Holy Spirit. Its short-spurred violet flowers became the type for Linnaeus's genus Aquilegia and progenitor of many modern hybrids.

'Black Barlow' is a dark double-flowered columbine bred by William Barlow in 1970s England as part of the Barlow Series. It was the first widely distributed spurless double black columbine, now an icon of gothic and cottage garden design.

Monkey puzzle is native to the volcanic slopes of the Chilean-Argentinian Andes. Molina described it in 1782. Sacred to the Mapuche and Pehuenche peoples who call it pehuén and rely on its seeds as a staple food, the tree is Chile's national tree and is now endangered due to logging and fire.

Vine Hill manzanita is native to a single small area of Sonoma County, California — the Vine Hill Preserve north of Sebastopol, a tiny relict ecosystem maintained for this and two other rare endemic manzanitas. One of the rarest plants in California, but the cultivar "Howard McMinn" derived from the species is among the most widely-grown California-native garden manzanitas.

Hooker's manzanita is native to the California coast. G. Don described it in 1834, naming it for William Jackson Hooker. Smooth reddish bark, urn-shaped pink-white flowers, and drought tolerance make it a signature shrub of California native gardens. Several subspecies occur on coastal barrens including the rare Monterey manzanita.

Common manzanita is native to the foothills of the Sierra Nevada and North Coast Ranges of California. One of about 60 California-native Arctostaphylos species defining California chaparral, it features smooth mahogany-red bark peeling in thin curling sheets and small pink-white urn-shaped flowers.

Bearberry ("kinnikinnick" from Algonquian languages) is a circumboreal groundcover native across cold-temperate and subarctic North America, Europe, and Asia. Leaves used by Indigenous North American peoples as a ceremonial smoking mixture and as a traditional urinary-tract medicine for over 1,000 years — the medicinal arbutin glycoside later validated by European pharmaceutical research.

Cape weed or plain treasure flower is native to South Africa. Levyns published the combination in 1942. Yellow daisy-like flowers on low silvery foliage carpet hillsides in spring. Widely planted as drought-tolerant groundcover in California and Australia—but has become a serious invasive weed in both regions.

Arctotis stoechadifolia, commonly called white arctotis, African daisy, or Cape gold, is an herbaceous perennial native to coastal dunes and fynbos of the Western Cape of South Africa. German botanist Berthold Karl Seemann described the species in the early 19th century, and it reached.

Marguerite daisy is a shrubby daisy of the Canary Islands grown since Victorian times as a conservatory specimen and now as a cool-season bedding favorite. Danish breeders revived it in the 1980s with the globally successful Butterfly and Madeira series.

Dutchman's pipe is native to the Appalachian Mountains. Lamarck described it in 1783. Pipe-shaped green-and-purple flowers hidden beneath giant heart-shaped leaves resemble meerschaum tobacco pipes and trap pollinating flies. Host plant for the pipevine swallowtail butterfly.

Purple chokeberry is a natural hybrid between red chokeberry (A. arbutifolia) and black chokeberry (A. melanocarpa) from eastern North America. Its dark purple fruit and brilliant red fall color combine traits of both parents in a vigorous garden shrub.

Wormwood or grand wormwood is a European and Eurasian native. Linnaeus described it in Species Plantarum 1753. Silvery aromatic perennial 60-120 cm tall with deeply divided silver-green leaves. Principal flavoring and traditionally the hallucinogen of absinthe liqueur (thujone).

Artemisia annua has been used in Chinese medicine (qinghao) for over 2000 years. Scientist Tu Youyou isolated artemisinin from it in 1972, winning the 2015 Nobel Prize. Artemisinin combination therapy is now the WHO first-line antimalarial treatment, credited with saving millions of lives.

Tarragon exists in two distinct cultivated forms: sterile French tarragon (propagated only by cuttings, central to béarnaise and French cuisine) and seed-grown Russian tarragon (stronger, less aromatic). The genus Artemisia honors the Greek goddess of the hunt, Artemis.

White sage or western mugwort is a western North American native. Nuttall described it in 1818. Silver-leaved rhizomatous perennial 45-90 cm tall, important in Indigenous ceremonies of western North America. 'Silver King' and 'Valerie Finnis' are popular ornamental cultivars.

Common mugwort is a Eurasian native widely naturalized. Linnaeus described it in Species Plantarum 1753. Tall 1-2 meter perennial with dark green leaves silver beneath. Used medicinally since antiquity, in moxibustion in Chinese/Japanese medicine, and as a bitter flavoring. Major allergen.

Goatsbeard is native to northern temperate forests across Europe, Asia, and North America. Described by Linnaeus as Spiraea aruncus in 1753, its massive creamy-white plumes made it a popular woodland garden plant since the 19th century.

Swamp milkweed is native to wetland and streamside habitats throughout North America. A major host plant for monarch butterfly caterpillars and a key nectar source for monarchs, swallowtails, and many native bees, it is one of the most horticulturally useful native milkweeds for garden and rain-garden use — unlike spreading common milkweed, it forms tidy clumps.

Asclepias tuberosa is native to eastern and central North America — from southern Ontario and New England south to Florida and west to Texas and Arizona. It produces flat-topped clusters of vivid orange (occasionally yellow or red) small five-pointed flowers on 45-75 cm stems through summer.

Pawpaw is the largest native fruit of temperate North America. Indigenous peoples cultivated and traded it for millennia, and Lewis and Clark recorded surviving on pawpaws in 1806. Modern breeders at Kentucky State University maintain the species' national germplasm repository.

The Foxtail and Sprenger forms of this South African species are among the most popular florist and houseplant 'ferns' — again, not true ferns but asparagus relatives. Red-black berries are moderately toxic to pets.

Asparagus is native to Europe and western Asia and was cultivated as a delicacy since Egyptian and Greek antiquity. Roman Emperor Augustus's saying "velocius quam asparagi cocuntur" ("faster than you can cook asparagus") reflected its reputation as a fast-cooking luxury.

The Asparagus Fern is not a fern at all but a flowering climber in the asparagus family, native to southern Africa. A staple florist's 'green' used in buttonhole bouquets since the late 19th century. Mildly toxic to pets.

Aspidistra elatior was introduced to Europe from Japan in 1822 and became the iconic 'parlor palm' of Victorian homes, celebrated for tolerating gas-lit, coal-dust-filled interiors. George Orwell's 1936 novel 'Keep the Aspidistra Flying' used the plant as a symbol of middle-class respectability.

The Bird's Nest Fern is native to tropical east Africa, Asia, and Australia, where it grows as a rainforest epiphyte. A traditional food in Taiwan and parts of the Pacific, its young fronds are stir-fried as a delicacy.

Aster alpinus has the broadest natural range of any aster, occurring in alpine and subalpine meadows from the Pyrenees and Alps across Asia to the Rocky Mountains in a circumboreal distribution. Linnaeus described it in Species Plantarum in 1753.

Italian aster is the only European aster species, ranging from France to Ukraine. Documented by Virgil in the Georgics as a cattle remedy, it carries the oldest garden history of any aster and remains the type species of the now-split genus Aster.

Aster novae-angliae grows wild across meadows and moist prairies of eastern and central North America. Several Indigenous nations, including the Iroquois and Ojibwe, burned the roots as ceremonial smoke and used decoctions for fevers and skin ailments.

Aster novi-belgii is native to moist ground along the Atlantic coast of North America from Newfoundland to Georgia. Specimens reached England by 1710 via Mark Catesby and Peter Collinson's trans-Atlantic plant exchange.

Frikart's aster is a 1918 hybrid of Aster amellus and A. thomsonii developed by Swiss nurseryman Carl Ludwig Frikart in Stäfa. The cultivar 'Mönch' is one of the most acclaimed perennials of the 20th century, bridging European and Himalayan asters.

Chinese astilbe is native to China, Korea, and parts of Russia. Introduced to European horticulture in the late 19th century, it became parent to many popular cultivars including the drought-tolerant 'Pumila' and the tall fragrant 'Purpurlanze'.

Japanese astilbe was introduced to Europe in 1837 by Philipp Franz von Siebold. It became the primary parent of early 20th-century hybrid astilbes bred by Georg Arends, transforming European shade gardens with the Astilbe x arendsii group.

Red astilbe is a Chinese and Japanese shade perennial whose fluffy red-pink plume flowers brighten moist woodland gardens. George Arends of Wuppertal bred many Astilbe x arendsii hybrids from this species and its cousins, now standards of shade gardens worldwide.

Star astilbe is a dwarf Japanese species endemic to Honshu cliffs, described by Tomitaro Makino in 1902. Its delicate star-shaped flowers and compact habit distinguish it from other astilbes and made it popular for rock gardens and small shade spaces.

Thunberg's astilbe is a graceful Japanese woodland perennial with arching white flower panicles. Named for 18th-century Swedish physician-botanist Carl Peter Thunberg, it contributed essential genetics to the German Arends hybrids that dominate modern shade gardens.

Astilbe x arendsii is a hybrid group created by German nurseryman Georg Arends between 1902 and 1955, crossing Asian species to produce the plume-flowered shade perennials that transformed 20th-century European and American shade gardening.

Shieldleaf is a monotypic genus native to northeast China and North Korea. Collected by Russian botanist Carl Maximowicz in the 1860s, it was moved from Rodgersia to its own genus in 1973 by Austrian botanist Engler, prized for massive round leaves.

Athyrium filix-femina is a circumboreal fern, native across temperate Europe, Asia, and North America — one of the most widely distributed fern species in the Northern Hemisphere. The pairing of common and scientific names with Dryopteris filix-mas (male fern) goes back to medieval.

Atropa belladonna is one of the most notorious poisons of European history. The name 'beautiful lady' refers to Renaissance women using berry juice to dilate their pupils. In modern medicine, atropine extracted from the plant saves lives in cardiac arrest and nerve agent poisoning.

Chinese aucuba is native to southern China, Taiwan, Laos, and Vietnam. Bentham described it in 1861. Similar to the Japanese aucuba (A. japonica) but more tender with glossier, narrower leaves. Valued as a shade-tolerant tropical-looking evergreen in mild Southeast US and Mediterranean gardens.

Japanese aucuba ("aoki") is native to Japan, Korea, and China. Introduced to English horticulture in 1783 as a female-only plant, it remained non-fruiting for 78 years until a male plant was finally imported from Japan in 1861 by Robert Fortune — after which the "variegata" gold-spotted cultivar produced long-prized bright red berries. A foundational Victorian shade-garden shrub.

Flame azalea (now Rhododendron calendulaceum) is native to the Appalachian mountains. Michaux described it in 1803, the epithet meaning resembling marigold for the vivid orange-yellow flowers. William Bartram famously described seeing a hillside 'set on fire' with bloom in 1776—it remains one of the most spectacular native flowering shrubs.

Azalea flammea is a historical name, usually treated as a synonym of the flame azalea Rhododendron calendulaceum, a vivid orange-flowered deciduous shrub of the Appalachians. The name reflects 18th- and 19th-century taxonomy before Azalea was merged into Rhododendron.

Japanese azalea is an old horticultural name covering several evergreen azalea species and hybrids (principally Rhododendron indicum, R. kiusianum, and their hybrids) cultivated in Japan for centuries. They are the foundational parents of the Kurume and Satsuki azalea groups that dominate warm-climate azalea plantings worldwide.

Swamp azalea (now correctly Rhododendron viscosum) is native to the eastern United States. Linnaeus described it in 1753. Blooming in midsummer after most azaleas finish, its sticky-glandular white to pink flowers carry an intense clove-spice fragrance that has inspired generations of breeders.

Coyote brush is a dioecious shrub of the California Floristic Province. A.P. de Candolle described it in 1836. The prostrate form 'Pigeon Point' and 'Twin Peaks' are widely used as drought-tolerant groundcovers. Dioecious—male plants preferred for landscape use to avoid cottony wind-blown seed.

Hedge bamboo is native to southern China and Taiwan, where it has been cultivated for more than a millennium as a hedging and ornamental bamboo. A clumping species, it has been widely planted across the southern US, the Caribbean, and warm-temperate gardens worldwide for privacy screens and formal hedging.

Oldham's bamboo (Chinese "lǜ zhú" 绿竹) is native to southeastern China and Taiwan. A large clumping (non-running) bamboo reaching 12-20 meters tall with dense foliage — the most widely planted large clumping bamboo in subtropical North America, valued as a privacy screen because it stays where planted, unlike aggressive running bamboos.

Bambusa vulgaris is one of the most widely distributed and cultivated bamboos in the tropics and subtropics worldwide. Its precise wild origin is debated — molecular evidence suggests a likely origin in southern China, Indochina, or Malesia, but the species has been carried, cultivated.

White wild indigo is a prairie and open-woods native of eastern North America, first described by Linnaeus in 1753. Indigenous peoples used root decoctions for toothache; it is now a popular drought-tolerant, deer-resistant perennial with chalk-white flower spikes.

Blue false indigo is native to the central and eastern United States. Used in colonial and early American textile industry as a substitute for true indigo (Indigofera tinctoria) when imported indigo was unavailable or expensive, though its dye yield was much inferior. Selected as the 2010 Perennial Plant Association "Plant of the Year."

Yellow wild indigo is a native eastern North American legume once used by colonial Americans as a poor-man's dye substitute for true indigo. Cherokee and other tribes used its roots medicinally. The species name 'tinctoria' refers to this dye use, recorded by the 1700s.

Ponytail palm is native to the semi-desert of eastern Mexico and is neither a palm nor a tree, despite appearances — it is a caudiciform succulent in the asparagus family. Its elephant-foot swollen base stores water, and individual plants can live several centuries.

Bolivian begonia is native to the cloud-forest slopes of the Andes in Bolivia and northern Argentina. Discovered by Richard Pearce collecting for Veitch Nursery in 1864 — one of the founding parent species of modern tuberous begonia hybrids. Produces pendulous bright-red bell-shaped flowers, making it particularly suited to hanging-basket cultivation.

Hardy begonia is native to China and Japan. Described by Dryander in 1791, it is the only truly hardy begonia (to zone 6), grown in European gardens since the early 19th century for its pink winged flowers and red-veined leaves that glow when backlit.

The Polka Dot Begonia is native to southeast Brazil's Atlantic rainforest. Obscure in commerce for decades, a 2016 Instagram post by interior designer Ursula Wesselingh is widely credited with making it one of the decade's fastest-rising houseplants.

The Rex Begonia was introduced to Europe in 1858 from an Assam shipment in which a single exotic specimen was hidden among orchids. Its discovery sparked an immediate breeding craze that produced thousands of cultivars over the next 150 years.

Begonia cucullata (the correct name for what horticulturists call B. semperflorens) is native to Brazil and adjacent parts of South America, growing in the understory of humid forests. It reached European cultivation around 1821 via Berlin Botanic Garden.

Begonia x tuberhybrida is a complex hybrid group developed from multiple tuberous Begonia species native to the Andes Mountains of South America. The foundational breeding work was done by John Seden, chief hybridizer at the James Veitch and Sons Nursery of Chelsea, London.

English lawn daisy, native to Europe, is the type species of the aster family name Bellis. Linnaeus described it in 1753. The common name 'daisy' comes from Old English 'dæges-eage' (day's eye), because the flower heads open at dawn and close at dusk.

Berberis thunbergii, Japanese barberry, is a compact spiny deciduous shrub native to Japan introduced to the United States in 1875 via the Arnold Arboretum. Popular for its tidy habit and red autumn foliage, it has escaped into eastern forests where it forms dense thickets that harbour blacklegged ticks carrying Lyme disease.

Heart-leaved bergenia, from Siberia and Mongolia, was named in 1804 for German botanist Karl August von Bergen. Its leaves were used in Siberian folk tea ('Mongolian tea'). It reached European gardens by the early 19th century as an evergreen shade perennial.

Chocolate flower is a Southwestern perennial whose yellow daisies release a distinct cocoa fragrance in morning air. It honors Jean Louis Berlandier, a Belgian-French naturalist who explored Texas and northeastern Mexico in the early 19th century.

Beetroot, chard, sugar beet, and fodder beet are all the same species. Sugar beet was developed in 18th-century Prussia by Andreas Marggraf and commercialized after Napoleon's British-blockade response broke the Caribbean sugar monopoly — launching European sugar independence.

Paper birch is native to northern North America and was fundamental to Indigenous Algonquian, Ojibwe, and Cree peoples — bark formed birchbark canoes, wigwam coverings, scrolls for sacred drawings (wiigwaasabak), and cooking containers. Its waterproof bark made continental water-travel possible before European contact.

Silver birch is the national tree of Finland, Sweden, and Russia. Birch bark tar was used as one of the earliest known adhesives — Neanderthal-era European tools bear birch-tar glue — and birch sap is traditionally fermented into beer and wine across northern Eurasia.

Apache beggarticks (golden goddess) is native to Mexico and the southwestern United States. A trailing annual or tender perennial producing continuous small 2-3 cm bright yellow-to-orange daisy flowers from early summer through hard frost — one of the most reliable long-blooming "spillers" for mixed summer containers and hanging baskets.

Crossvine is native to the southeastern United States. Linnaeus described it in 1753. The epithet capreolata means with tendrils. Clusters of trumpet-shaped orange-red flowers with yellow throats in spring are magnets for returning hummingbirds. A non-invasive native alternative to trumpet creeper.

Deer fern is an evergreen fern of moist coniferous forests across western North America, northern Europe, and northern Asia. Indigenous peoples used it as emergency food and thirst quencher, and it remains a staple of shade garden design.

Borage is a Mediterranean annual whose starry blue flowers and cucumber-flavored leaves have been a cottage-garden staple for over a thousand years. The classical proverb "Ego borago gaudia semper ago" ("I, borage, always bring joy") captures its reputation as a cordial herb.

Lesser bougainvillea is a close relative of B. spectabilis, distinguished by smoother leaves, and is the parent of many modern horticultural cultivars. It is the official flower of multiple tropical cities including Naha (Okinawa), Shenzhen, and Tainan.

Bougainvillea was discovered in Brazil in 1768 by French explorer Philibert Commerson aboard the Bougainville circumnavigation — reportedly by Jeanne Baret, the first woman to sail around the world (disguised as his male valet). It became a defining plant of Mediterranean and tropical architecture.

Swan River daisy is a slender Western Australian annual bearing masses of small daisy flowers in blue, violet, pink, or white. Discovered along the Swan River in the 1830s, it quickly entered European horticulture as a delicate edging and container annual.

Mustard greens, brown mustard, or Indian mustard is a Eurasian cultigen derived from the hybrid of Brassica nigra × B. rapa. Linnaeus described it as Sinapis juncea in 1753; Czernajew transferred it to Brassica in 1859. One of the world's major oilseed and leafy vegetable species.

Kale and collard greens are both Brassica oleracea var. acephala, the non-heading leafy forms of wild cabbage. de Candolle described the variety in 1821. Cultivated for at least 2000 years. Includes curly kale, lacinato/Tuscan/dinosaur kale, Russian kale, and Southern US collards.

Cauliflower was developed in the eastern Mediterranean — probably Cyprus — during the medieval period from broccoli-like ancestors. Mark Twain called it "nothing but cabbage with a college education." Its white color is preserved by tying leaves over the developing head to exclude sun.

Cabbage is one of many cultivars of Brassica oleracea — the species also gives us broccoli, kale, cauliflower, Brussels sprouts, and kohlrabi. European domestication dates to ancient Greek times, and the tight-head cabbage form dominated medieval peasant cuisine across northern Europe.

Kohlrabi or German turnip is a variety of Brassica oleracea with swollen stem base grown as a vegetable. L.H. Bailey described it in 1922. Likely developed in northern Europe in the 15th-16th centuries. Flavor similar to broccoli stem with crisp turnip texture. Purple and green forms available.

Brassica oleracea var. italica (broccoli) was developed in Italy, almost certainly during the Etruscan and Roman era, from the wild cabbage ancestor. The name derives from the Italian "broccolo" meaning "little branch" or "little sprout," the diminutive of "brocco" (stem).

Napa or Chinese cabbage was developed in northern China during the 15th century Ming dynasty from crosses between bok choy (B. rapa subsp. chinensis) and other B. rapa forms. It is the primary cabbage of Chinese, Korean, and Japanese cuisine — and the backbone of kimchi.

Cheatgrass, native to Eurasia, was introduced accidentally to the US with packing material and contaminated seed in the 1800s. By 1930 it had transformed tens of millions of acres of western sagebrush steppe into annual grassland dominated by summer fire cycles.

Broussonetia papyrifera, paper mulberry, is a fast-growing deciduous tree native to East Asia whose inner bark has been beaten into tapa cloth and paper for millennia. Polynesian voyagers carried it across the Pacific. Introduced to America in the 1700s, it now colonizes southeastern roadsides with aggressive root suckering and allergenic pollen.

American bush violet is a South American annual producing delicate star-shaped lavender-blue flowers through summer and autumn. It was one of the first plants Linnaeus used to illustrate his Species Plantarum in 1753, founding modern botanical nomenclature.

Bush violet (amethyst flower) is native to Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, and adjacent tropical Andean countries. A tender perennial grown as a shade-tolerant summer bedding annual producing small five-petaled flowers in intense violet-blue, purple, or pure white — one of the few reliably blue-flowered shade plants.

Sticky amethyst flower is a South American annual bearing brilliant violet-blue tubular flowers with white eyes. The genus honors 18th-century Swedish bishop-botanist Johan Browallius, a friend and defender of Linnaeus during the early days of binomial nomenclature.

Angel's trumpet is native to the Brazilian Atlantic Forest. Now classified as "Extinct in the Wild" by the IUCN — all known plants are cultivated. Its massive, intensely fragrant, trumpet-shaped pendant flowers make it a popular ornamental, but every part is highly toxic (tropane alkaloids) and it has a long history of indigenous hallucinogenic ritual use and modern poisonings.

Brunnera macrophylla grows naturally in damp deciduous forests of the Caucasus, northern Turkey, and western Siberia. The species was first described as Anchusa myosotidiflora in 1801 and later transferred to the genus Brunnera by Johnston in 1924; the genus itself was named in 1812 by the German-R.

Buddleja alternifolia grows on dry rocky slopes and riverbanks of Gansu, Qinghai, Shaanxi, and Tibet in northwestern China. The English plant collector Reginald Farrer introduced it in 1914 after seeing it flowering along the Min River, describing it as a 'gracious small-leaved willow.

Buddleja davidii is native to central and southwestern China, particularly in the upper Yangtze river system in Sichuan and Hubei, where it colonizes disturbed rocky slopes and riverbanks. French Lazarist missionary and naturalist Père Armand David (after whom the species is named) first collected.

Orange ball tree, native to Chile and Argentina, was introduced to Europe in 1774. Its distinctive globular orange flower heads inspired its common name. Mapuche peoples used the leaves as 'palqui' for wound healing. It became the first Buddleja grown in British gardens.

Lindley's butterfly bush, native to China, was introduced to England in 1844 by Robert Fortune and named for botanist John Lindley. Unlike the more common B. davidii, it has gracefully arching branches of dark purple flowers and has become invasive in the US Southeast.

Littleleaf boxwood is native to Japan, Korea, and Taiwan. Siebold and Zuccarini described it in 1845. Smaller-leaved and more cold-hardy than English box (B. sempervirens), it is the parent of hardy cultivars 'Winter Gem' and 'Green Pillow' that have replaced English box in cold-winter landscapes.

Common box is native to southern Europe, North Africa, and western Asia, and has been cultivated as the foundational hedging and topiary plant of European gardens since antiquity. Its exceptionally fine, hard, dense wood was used for woodcut printing blocks, chess pieces, and wind-instrument parts. Boxwood blight has devastated hedges since the 1990s.

Korean boxwood is native to Korea and northern China. T.T. Yu and P.C. Chia clarified the taxonomy. Formerly classified as B. microphylla var. koreana, it is the most cold-hardy boxwood and the ancestor of many American-bred cold-climate cultivars—particularly the Sheridan Nurseries' 'Green' series.

Native to tropical South America, Caladiums were cultivated ornamentally by indigenous Amazonians before European contact. Victorian breeders produced hundreds of colorful cultivars; today Lake Placid, Florida, supplies over 95% of the world's tubers.

Bush grass is a Eurasian native widespread across temperate Europe and Asia. Linnaeus described it as Arundo arundinacea in 1753; Roth transferred it to Calamagrostis in 1789. Forms 60-120 cm clumps with feathery purple-tinged plumes in summer. Tolerates shade better than most ornamental grasses.

Calamagrostis brachytricha, commonly called Korean feather reed grass or diamond grass, is a cool-season clumping perennial grass in the family Poaceae native to a broad range across Korea, Japan, northern China, southeastern Siberia, and into the Russian Far East, growing in moist forest edges and.

Calandrinia spectabilis (sometimes sold as Cistanthe grandiflora) is a succulent perennial in the purslane family native to the coastal deserts and rocky slopes of central and northern Chile. The plant forms a low mound of fleshy blue-grey paddle-shaped leaves, from which tall wiry stems.

Rattlesnake plant is a Brazilian native now classified as Goeppertia insignis following Borchsenius et al. 2012. Described by Linden ex Körnicke in 1862. Elongated lance-shaped wavy-margined green leaves patterned with alternating small and large dark green ovals resembling snake skin.

The Peacock Plant is native to eastern Brazil. Named in 1875 after the Belgian horticulturist Jacob Makoy, its leaves bear a pattern of alternating light and dark ovals resembling a peacock's tail. Non-toxic to pets.

Native to the Bolivian rainforest understory, Calathea orbifolia was a specialist collector's plant until the late 2010s, when social media demand propelled it into the mainstream houseplant trade. Recently reclassified into the genus Goeppertia.

Pinstripe Calathea is a Colombian and Venezuelan rainforest native now classified as Goeppertia ornata. Linden described it in 1848. Elliptical dark glossy green leaves with characteristic thin pink or cream pinstripes running perpendicular to midrib. Young leaves have brighter pink stripes.

Rose-painted Calathea is a Brazilian rainforest native now classified as Goeppertia roseopicta. Linden described it in 1866. Rounded dark green leaves with characteristic pink-to-cream 'painted' feathered arc and pink-red midrib. Parent of many modern Calathea cultivars.

Zebra plant is a Brazilian rainforest native now classified as Goeppertia zebrina. Sims described it as Maranta zebrina in 1815; Lindley transferred it to Calathea in 1828. Distinctive velvety green leaves boldly striped with alternating dark and light green bars resembling zebra markings.

Field marigold is a small annual native across the Mediterranean and widely naturalized as a cornfield weed. Medieval herbalists used it much as its larger cousin Calendula officinalis, though today it survives mostly as a wildflower in restored cornfield habitats.

Pot marigold has been cultivated in Mediterranean Europe since antiquity, used by Greeks and Romans as culinary coloring and by medieval herbalists for wound healing. Nicholas Culpeper praised it in 1653, and during both World Wars its tincture was applied to battlefield wounds to reduce infection.

Seaside petunia is a low-growing annual to short-lived perennial of the Americas with small magenta to violet flowers. It is one of the wild species that fed into the breeding of modern Calibrachoa Million Bells hybrids now ubiquitous in container gardens.

Calibrachoa, widely sold as Million Bells or Superbells, is a group of hybrids derived from South American species. Japanese breeder Suntory Flowers released the first commercial hybrids in 1992, revolutionizing container gardening with nonstop miniature petunia flowers.

American beautyberry is a southeastern United States shrub famous for its clusters of vivid magenta fall berries. Indigenous peoples used crushed leaves as insect repellent, a use recently validated by USDA chemistry research on its repellent compounds.

Bodinier's beautyberry, from central and western China, was described by Léveillé in 1911, honoring French missionary collector Émile-Marie Bodinier. Introduced by E.H. Wilson around 1907, its cultivar 'Profusion' became one of the most popular fall-fruiting shrubs worldwide.

Purple beautyberry is an East Asian shrub cultivated since antiquity in Chinese and Japanese gardens for its arching branches laden with small violet berries. It entered Western horticulture in the 19th century and remains a popular landscape shrub for autumn color.

Giraldi's beautyberry is a central Chinese shrub described by Hesse in 1904, honoring the Italian Franciscan missionary Giuseppe Giraldi, who collected plants in Shaanxi in the 1890s. It is now the most widely grown hardy beautyberry, prized for intense violet fruit.

Japanese beautyberry, native to Japan, Korea, and eastern China, was described by Thunberg in 1784. Long cultivated in Japanese gardens under the name 'murasaki-shikibu' (honoring the Heian author), it entered Western horticulture in the 1840s.

Incense cedar is native to the Sierra Nevada, Cascades, and Klamath Mountains of California, Oregon, and adjacent Nevada and Baja California. The aromatic softwood is the primary commercial source of wood for American wooden pencils — the vast majority of the world's wooden pencils have been made from incense cedar since the late 19th century.

Superb mariposa lily is endemic to California. Similar to C. venustus but distinguished by typically larger white-to-cream flowers with distinctive crescent-shaped dark markings at the base of each petal above a yellow nectary. Widespread across California grasslands and foothill slopes.

Butterfly mariposa lily is endemic to California. Spring-blooming bulb producing 2-4 large cup-shaped 5-7 cm flowers with distinctive dramatic dark markings ("eye spots") at the base of each petal — in pink, lavender, white, yellow, or red with combination markings. One of roughly 70 species of Calochortus, a largely California-centered genus.

Caltha palustris is a circumboreal wetland perennial native across temperate and arctic Europe, Asia, and North America — from British Isles and European Russia east through Siberia to Kamchatka, and across North America from Alaska and Labrador south to the Carolinas and along the Rocky Mountains.

Calycanthus chinensis is endemic to a small area of northwestern Zhejiang Province in eastern China. Chinese botanists W.C. Cheng and S.Y. Chang formally described the species in 1964 in the Acta Phytotaxonomica Sinica.

Sundrops, or square-bud primrose, is a low-growing perennial of Texas and the southern Great Plains honoring Scottish naturalist Thomas Drummond. Its lemon-yellow evening primrose flowers bloom profusely on tough dry calcareous soils.

Hedge bindweed is a vigorous perennial vine native across the Northern Hemisphere, clambering over hedges and fences to two metres with large white trumpet flowers. Its deep rhizomes make it a persistent garden weed, but its nectar-rich blooms attract hawk moths and bumblebees.

Cusick's camas is native to northeastern Oregon and adjacent Idaho. Named for early Oregon botanist William Conklin Cusick, its 2-3 foot spires of pale blue stars made it a popular naturalizing bulb for meadows and woodland edges across temperate gardens.

Large camas, native to the Pacific Northwest from British Columbia to California, was described by Sereno Watson in 1885, honoring German horticulturist Max Leichtlin. Its edible bulbs were a staple starch for Indigenous peoples; Lewis and Clark recorded eating them in 1806.

Common camas is the species whose bulbs were the most important cultivated root food of Pacific Northwest Indigenous peoples. Pursh described it in 1814, adapting the Nez Perce name 'qém'es'. Vast camas prairies once stretched across Oregon, Washington, and Idaho.

Japanese camellia is native to Japan, Korea, and China, cultivated in Japanese and Chinese gardens for more than a thousand years. It reached Europe in the 18th century and the American South in the 19th, becoming the iconic "winter rose" of Southern coastal gardens and the Portuguese estates of the Minho.

Reticulate camellia is native to Yunnan Province, southwestern China. Lindley described it in 1827. Cultivated in Dali, Yunnan, for over a thousand years—some temple specimens exceed 500 years old—its enormous flowers (up to 15 cm across) are the largest in the genus and have inspired modern giant-flowered breeding.

Sasanqua camellia is native to southern Japan. Thunberg described it in 1784. Flowering in autumn and winter months, its oil-rich seeds (sazanka abura) have been pressed in Japan for over a thousand years for cooking, cosmetics, and samurai sword maintenance—a tradition continuing to this day.

The tea plant is native to southwestern China, northeast India, and Southeast Asia. Tea drinking is documented in China from at least the Han dynasty and became a national industry by the Tang (8th century). British industrial tea cultivation in Assam and Darjeeling (1830s onward), built on stolen Chinese techniques obtained by Robert Fortune, made tea a global commodity.

Campanula carpatica grows wild in the limestone cliffs and alpine meadows of the Carpathian range spanning Romania, Slovakia, Ukraine, and Poland. It was introduced to English gardens around 1774 and praised by William Robinson in The English Flower Garden (1883) as one of the finest.

Campanula medium is native to southern France, northern Italy, and adjacent Mediterranean regions. It entered English gardens by the 16th century and is named for the bells worn by pilgrims traveling to the shrine of Thomas Becket at Canterbury Cathedral — the association traced to the plant's.

Trumpet vine is native to the southeastern United States. Its aggressive climbing, adhesive aerial rootlets, and orange-red trumpet flowers beloved by hummingbirds make it both a popular native ornamental and a notorious suckering nuisance that can persist for years after removal.

Indian shot (also called canna lily) is native to the Americas — from the southern US through Mexico, Central America, the Caribbean, and into tropical South America. Despite the species name "indica" it is not from India — the name reflects Columbus-era confusion between the Americas and "the Indies."

Garden canna is the ornamental hybrid complex combining Canna indica with several other Canna species, bred intensively by French, Italian, and American breeders from the 1860s onward. The "Crozy" hybrids from 1860s-1880s France produced the first showy garden cannas and remain the foundation of most modern cultivars.

Shepherd's purse is a Eurasian annual that has become one of the world's most common arable weeds. Its distinctive heart-shaped seedpods resemble a medieval shepherd's leather pouch. It has been used in European and East Asian medicine as a hemostatic for over 2000 years.

Capsicum annuum was first domesticated in central Mexico at least 6,000 years ago — archaeological evidence from Tehuacán Valley in Puebla records Capsicum use and cultivation. It was a staple of Aztec and Maya cuisine.

Ají chile or Peruvian ají is a South American native domesticated in Peru and Bolivia around 2500 BCE. Linnaeus described it in 1767. One of the five domesticated Capsicum species. Tall 1.5-2 meter plants producing long slender pods with fruity, citrusy heat. 'Lemon Drop' is popular cultivar.

Despite the misleading name, habaneros and Scotch bonnets are native to the Amazon basin — not China. The species contains the world's hottest peppers, including the Carolina Reaper (over 2 million Scoville units), and is a defining ingredient of Caribbean and South American cuisines.

Rocoto or manzano chile is a highland Andean native, the least commercially known of the five domesticated Capsicums. Ruiz & Pavón described it in 1799. Cold-tolerant perennial unique for purple flowers and black seeds. Thick apple-shaped fruits with intense heat. Cultivated at 1500-3500 m elevation.

Cardamine hirsuta is native to Europe and western Asia. Linnaeus described it in Species Plantarum (1753). The genus name derives from Greek 'kardamon' — its peppery flavor linking it to cress — and the species is part of the Brassicaceae family with the characteristic spicy mustard-oil.

Carduus nutans, musk or nodding thistle, is a biennial composite native to Europe, North Africa, and western Asia. First recorded in the eastern United States around 1853, probably from ballast soil, it spread westward through contaminated hay and crop seed and by the mid-twentieth century infested.

New Zealand hair sedge is a New Zealand endemic. Berggren described it in 1880. Forms fine-textured cascading 30-45 cm mounds of slender hair-like olive-brown to bronze leaves. Popular cultivars 'Frosted Curls', 'Amazon Mist', 'Bronco', and 'Bronze Form'.

Tufted sedge is a European and western Asian wetland native. Allioni described it in 1785. Grows as large water-side clumps to 90 cm. The gold-variegated cultivar 'Aurea' (Bowles' Golden sedge), selected by E.A. Bowles before 1955, is widely cultivated for brilliant spring foliage.

Gray's sedge or morningstar sedge is an eastern North American native. J. Carey described it in 1847 honoring Asa Gray. Named for its distinctive spherical seed heads resembling medieval morningstar maces. Wet woodland sedge that reaches 60-90 cm with attractive architectural fruit.

Japanese sedge is native to Japan, where it grows as an understory evergreen sedge. Its arching fountains of variegated leaves made its cultivars — particularly "Ice Dance" and "Silver Sceptre" — staple shade groundcovers of late-20th-century American landscape design.

Oshima sedge is a Japanese endemic of Izu Islands south of Tokyo. Nakai described it in 1915. Forms low evergreen mounds of glossy green leaves. 'Evergold' (synonym 'Everillo' for golden form) variegated cultivar is one of the best-selling ornamental sedges worldwide.

Creeping broad-leaved sedge is an East Asian woodland sedge of China, Japan, Korea, and Russian Far East. Hance described it in 1873. Forms slowly spreading mats of broad 2-3 cm wide bamboo-like leaves. Variegated 'Variegata', 'Banana Boat', and 'Lemon Zest' are popular cultivars.

American hornbeam or musclewood is native to eastern North America. Described by Thomas Walter in 1788, its distinctive sinewy muscled-looking trunk gave rise to the common name. Its extremely hard heavy wood was used for tool handles, levers, and wedges.

Spreading bluebeard is a herbaceous perennial native to China, Japan, and Korea, described by Maximowicz in 1872. Unlike the shrubby hybrid Caryopterises, it forms tall clumps with curiously shaped violet flowers that smell of cat litter when crushed.

Caryopteris incana grows on dry hillsides and open forests across eastern China, Taiwan, Korea, and Japan. Friedrich Miquel described it in 1865. In Chinese traditional medicine (as lan xiang cao) the aerial parts were used to treat coughs, bronchitis, and rheumatism.

Caryopteris x clandonensis originated around 1930 at Clandon Park in Surrey, England, where horticulturist Arthur Simmonds crossed the Chinese C. incana with the Mongolian C. mongholica. The hybrid combined the larger, denser inflorescences of C.

Madagascar periwinkle is native to Madagascar, distributed globally as an ornamental in the 18th–19th centuries. In the 1950s–60s Canadian and American researchers isolated vincristine and vinblastine from its leaves — chemotherapy drugs that transformed childhood leukemia and Hodgkin's lymphoma.

New Jersey tea was brewed by American colonists from dried leaves as a Chinese tea substitute during the British tea boycott of 1773-1775. It was called 'Liberty Tea.' Indigenous peoples used the red roots medicinally. A nitrogen-fixing native shrub still valued today in pollinator gardens.

Point Reyes ceanothus is native to the outer Coast Ranges of northern California. J.T. Howell described it in 1938. A tough, low-growing evergreen groundcover with glossy holly-like leaves and deep violet-blue flower clusters in spring—one of the best California lilacs for prostrate coastal-sage groundcover use.

Carmel creeper is a prostrate variety of Ceanothus griseus from the Carmel Highlands of coastal California. Howard McMinn described the variety horizontalis in 1942. 'Yankee Point' and 'Diamond Heights' are widely planted cultivars forming dense 60 cm tall mats of glossy evergreen leaves with blue spring flowers.

Santa Barbara ceanothus is native to a very restricted area of coastal Santa Barbara County, California. Distinguished by the deeply impressed veins of its tiny dark-green leaves — the leaves appear almost quilted in texture. Classified as a vulnerable rare native plant, it is also the parent of the widely-grown garden cultivar "Julia Phelps."

Maritime ceanothus is a rare prostrate shrub endemic to coastal bluffs of San Luis Obispo County, California. Howard McMinn described it in 1942. 'Valley Violet' and 'Point Sierra' are cultivated selections. Tolerates heavy clay and coastal conditions better than most Ceanothus.

Blueblossom is native to the California coast from Southern Oregon to Santa Barbara County. One of the most widely grown California-native ornamental shrubs, producing clouds of tiny sky-blue flowers in spring that draw crowds of native bees and butterflies. The parent of many popular "wild lilac" garden cultivars including "Skylark" and "Victoria."

Atlas cedar is native to the Atlas Mountains of Morocco and Algeria. Manetti formally named it in 1844. Its aromatic wood was used in ancient Egyptian mummification and temple construction.
