The blue-needled cultivar 'Glauca' and its weeping form 'Glauca Pendula' are iconic specimen trees in Western gardens.

Deodar cedar is native to the western Himalaya (Afghanistan, Pakistan, India, Nepal, and western Tibet). Its name derives from Sanskrit "devadāru" — "wood of the gods" — and it has been used for Hindu and Buddhist temple construction for more than 3,000 years. It is the national tree of Pakistan.

Cedrus libani grows naturally on mountain slopes in Lebanon, western Syria, and the Taurus Mountains of southern Turkey, typically between 1300 and 3000 meters. French botanist Achille Richard formally described it in 1823.

Oriental bittersweet is a woody climbing vine native to East Asia, introduced to North America in 1860 as an ornamental. Its orange-red autumn fruits are attractive but vines strangle and topple mature trees, and the species hybridizes with and genetically swamps the native American bittersweet.

American bittersweet is native to eastern and central North America. Linnaeus described it in 1753. Its orange-capsuled yellow-to-scarlet fruits split open in autumn and have been used in Americana decor for generations. Increasingly rare due to hybridization with invasive Oriental bittersweet (C. orbiculatus).

Plumed and crested celosias originate from tropical Asia and Africa, cultivated for centuries for edible leaves and vivid inflorescences. The species name argentea, silvery, is misleading; gardeners know the plant for its intense red, orange, yellow, and pink plumes.

Coxcomb, the crested cultivar group of Celosia argentea, has been cultivated since antiquity in Asia and Africa for its brain-like crinkled flower heads. The bizarre form has inspired gardeners from Ming China to 20th-century American cut flower farmers.

Wheat celosia, the Spicata Group of Celosia argentea, produces narrow upright flower spikes resembling miniature wheat heads in pink, magenta, or silver. Its more elegant form suits naturalistic and cutting garden use alongside plumed and crested celosias.

Common hackberry is native to central and eastern North America. Linnaeus described it in 1753. Prized for extraordinary tolerance of urban conditions, drought, and alkaline soils, it has become a frequent street tree replacement for ash and elm in disease-ravaged cities.

Cornflower (also called bachelor's button) is native to Europe. Historically a common grain-field weed, dramatic 20th-century declines due to herbicides have made it rare in its native range, though it is still widely grown from seed. A cultural symbol of remembrance in Germany, Austria, and France — the bleuet de France honors WWI soldiers.

Centaurea moschata, the sweet sultan or royal sweet sultan, now often treated as Amberboa moschata, is an upright annual native to the eastern Mediterranean, Turkey, and Iran. The plant was cultivated in Ottoman imperial gardens and reached western Europe via Constantinople around 1620.

Centaurea solstitialis, yellow starthistle, is a deep-taprooted annual composite native to the Mediterranean basin and western Asia. It arrived in California before 1860 in contaminated alfalfa seed shipped from Chile, spread rapidly through Gold Rush livestock movements, and now infests.

Centaurea stoebe (often split as C. stoebe subsp. micranthos), spotted knapweed, is a biennial or short-lived perennial composite native to eastern Europe, the Balkans, and western Asia. First recorded in North America around 1893 as a contaminant of Central Asian alfalfa seed.

Buttonbush is native to eastern and central North America and Cuba. Linnaeus described it in 1753. Its spherical clusters of fragrant white flowers attract over 20 butterfly species, native bees, and hummingbirds. A classic wetland plant used by indigenous peoples for bark medicines.

Japanese plum yew is native to Japan, Korea, and eastern China. Koch named it in 1873 after the Earl of Harrington who first cultivated it in Britain. Resembling yew but with larger olive-shaped seed drupes, it is shade- and deer-tolerant, making it a valuable landscape alternative to Taxus in the southeastern United States.

Common mouse-ear chickweed is a Eurasian perennial naturalized worldwide. Baumgarten described it in 1816 from Transylvanian mountain springs — fontanum meaning 'of springs'. It is a ubiquitous lawn weed whose fuzzy grayish leaves resemble mouse ears.

Chinese plumbago is native to western China. Stapf described it in 1914, naming it for the English horticulturist Ellen Willmott who sponsored E.H. Wilson's expeditions. Its sky-blue phlox-like flowers cover the compact shrub from August through October, when few other blue-flowered plants bloom.

Eastern redbud is native to eastern and central North America and produces distinctive pink-purple pea-like flowers directly on bare branches in early spring, before leafing. It is the state tree of Oklahoma. The flowers are edible, with a mildly sweet, pea-like flavor, and have long been added to salads and baked goods.

Chinese redbud is native to central and southern China. Linnaeus's son described it in 1781. Cultivated in Chinese gardens for over 1000 years, its rose-pink flowers clustering along bare branches made it a symbol of brotherhood in classical Chinese poetry.

Western redbud is native to California, southern Nevada, Utah, Arizona, and New Mexico. A classic California-native small ornamental tree producing masses of hot-magenta pea-shaped flowers in early spring on bare branches, followed by heart-shaped leaves and decorative reddish seed pods. Branches were traditionally used by California Indigenous basketweavers for red-colored basket warp.

Judas tree is native to the eastern Mediterranean and southern Europe. It produces rose-pink flowers directly on bare branches in early spring. The common name stems from a Christian legend that Judas Iscariot hanged himself from such a tree, after which its formerly white flowers turned pink with shame.

Peruvian apple cactus is ironically not native to Peru — the original specimen described in 1753 was of unclear South American origin. The plant is widely cultivated as a fast-growing columnar cactus for landscaping and a fruiting crop (koubo) in Israel.

Native to the Mediterranean, Cerinthe major has been grown since antiquity as a nectar-rich forage for honeybees—the genus name derives from Greek keros (wax) and anthos (flower). Pliny the Elder mentioned it in Naturalis Historia as a bee plant.

Chinese flowering quince is native to central and southern China. Schneider formalized it in 1906. Larger and more tree-like than C. speciosa or C. japonica, it bears the biggest fruits of the genus (up to 15 cm)—traditionally used in Chinese medicine (mu gua) for rheumatic pain and digestion.

Japanese quince is a low-growing native of Japan, described by Thunberg in 1784 and transferred to Chaenomeles by Lindley in 1822. Its brilliant spring flowers and small yellow fruit rich in pectin and vitamin C made it a beloved cottage-garden ornamental.

Chinese flowering quince, native to China, has been cultivated for over two millennia as 'mugua' in traditional Chinese medicine for arthritis and digestion. Introduced to the West in 1796, it became a beloved Victorian shrub and major parent of modern hybrids.

Sawara cypress is native to Japan. Siebold and Zuccarini described it in 1842. Planted for over a thousand years around Japanese temples and as one of the Five Sacred Trees of Kiso, its rot-resistant wood has been used for casks, bath tubs, and musical instruments. Many ornamental cultivars are popular worldwide.

The Parlor Palm is native to the rainforests of southern Mexico and Guatemala. One of the defining Victorian and Edwardian parlor plants, it tolerates gaslight, low humidity, and low light better than any other palm of its era.

Roman chamomile is an ancient European meadow herb used by Egyptians as an offering to the sun god Ra and by medieval Europeans for calming teas. Beatrix Potter's Peter Rabbit was given chamomile tea after his garden raid — a reference to deep English folk-medicine tradition.

Slender spike grass or slender woodoats is a southeastern US woodland native. Linnaeus described it as Uniola laxa in 1753; H.O. Yates transferred it to Chasmanthium in 1966. Finer, narrower and more shade-tolerant than the better-known Chasmanthium latifolium. Natural woodland grass.

White turtlehead is native to the eastern and central United States and Canada. The sole native host plant for the Baltimore checkerspot butterfly (Euphydryas phaeton), Maryland's state insect — making this otherwise modest wetland wildflower a keystone species for conservation of the Baltimore checkerspot.

Lamb's quarters is a cool-season annual likely native to Europe or western Asia, now globally naturalized. Linnaeus described it in 1753. It has been eaten as a nutritious spinach-like vegetable worldwide for millennia, and its related species quinoa was a staple Andean crop.

Forbes's glory-of-the-snow, native to western Turkey, was described by J.G. Baker in 1879, honoring the English clergyman-botanist James Forbes. Its blue star flowers with white centers made it one of the most widely planted spring bulbs, naturalizing freely in cold climates.

Glory-of-the-snow is native to the mountains of western Turkey. Discovered by Swiss botanist Pierre Edmond Boissier in 1842 and named for his wife Lucile, its brilliant blue star flowers emerge through late-winter snow, becoming a beloved early-spring bulb.

Sardian glory-of-the-snow, from the mountains of western Turkey around ancient Sardis, was described by William Barr and J.D. Hooker in 1878. Deeper blue than its cousin C. forbesii, it was introduced to Kew from Anatolian expeditions and spread globally via Dutch bulb houses.

Chitalpa is a bigeneric hybrid between American Chilopsis linearis and Catalpa bignonioides created in 1964 at the Uzbek Academy of Sciences in Tashkent. It combines desert tolerance from Chilopsis with Catalpa's larger flowers, becoming a popular tree for arid Southwest US gardens.

The Spider Plant is native to coastal southern Africa and has been a Victorian-era houseplant staple for over 150 years. Famous for producing plantlets on runners, it is among the most forgiving indoor plants and is non-toxic to pets.

Damianita is a compact aromatic subshrub of the Chihuahuan Desert, blanketed in bright yellow daisy flowers from spring through fall. Its intense resinous foliage and drought tolerance make it a mainstay of Texas and southwestern xeriscape design.

Wild Indian Chrysanthemum is the primary wild parent of the cultivated florist's mum. Native to China, Korea, and Japan, its small yellow flowers are the traditional source of Chinese medicinal chrysanthemum tea (juhua cha).

The Florist's Chrysanthemum has been cultivated in China for over 2,500 years. One of the Four Gentlemen of Chinese art (plum, orchid, bamboo, chrysanthemum), it is the imperial seal of Japan and a symbol of autumn across East Asia.

Garden chrysanthemum (Chinese "jú huā" 菊花, Japanese "kiku") is a cultivated hybrid derived from Chinese wild species, cultivated for over 2,500 years. One of the "Four Gentlemen" of Chinese painting and the Imperial Seal of Japan — the Chrysanthemum Throne — since the 12th century. The national flower of Japan and one of the most commercially grown cut flowers worldwide.

Chrysogonum virginianum, commonly called green-and-gold or goldenstar, is a low-growing perennial wildflower in the aster family (Asteraceae) native to open woodlands and forest edges of the eastern United States from Pennsylvania and Ohio south through the Appalachians to Georgia.

Endive and escarole are both Cichorium endivia. Linnaeus described it in Species Plantarum 1753. Annual or biennial salad chicory native to the eastern Mediterranean. Var. crispum is curly endive/frisée, var. latifolium is broad-leaved escarole. Slightly bitter winter salad greens.

Common chicory is native to Europe and western Asia, widely naturalized. Linnaeus described it in Species Plantarum, 1753. A 60–150 cm perennial with pure blue ray flowers opening only in morning. Cultivated for coffee-substitute roots, radicchio, endive, Belgian witloof, and forage.

Cinchona officinalis bark is the source of quinine, the first effective antimalarial drug. Introduced to Europe by Jesuits in the 1630s as 'Jesuit's bark.' The 19th-century British and Dutch plantation systems in India and Java made quinine globally affordable and helped enable European colonization of tropical regions.

Cirsium arvense, Canada thistle, is a deep-rooted perennial native to Europe and western Asia, despite its misleading common name. It spreads aggressively by creeping rhizomes and is one of the world's worst agricultural weeds, listed as noxious in most Canadian provinces and many US states. Its nectar-rich flowers attract pollinators.

Cirsium vulgare, bull thistle or spear thistle, is a spiny biennial native to Europe, western Asia, and North Africa. Its fierce spines and tall flowering stalks make it a familiar weed of pastures and roadsides worldwide. The flowers are rich in nectar and are a favorite of bumblebees and goldfinches, and the seeds feed many songbirds.

The watermelon is native to northeastern Africa, with the Sudanese Kordofan melon identified in 2021 as its likely wild ancestor. Cultivated in Egypt for at least 4,000 years, it appears in tomb paintings and was placed in King Tutankhamun's burial chamber for the afterlife.

The lemon is a cultivated hybrid (citron × sour orange) that probably originated in northeast India, northern Burma, or China. It became essential to 18th-century British naval medicine as a treatment for scurvy, giving rise to the nickname 'limey'.

Mandarin is a true citrus species (not hybrid) native to southern China and northeast India, one of the three ancestral citrus species along with pomelo and citron. Its name comes from the Mandarin Chinese officials who reportedly wore robes in the fruit's orange color.

The sweet orange was developed in China as a cross between pomelo and mandarin, reaching Europe via Arab traders in the 10th century (bitter orange) and Portuguese traders in the 15th century (sweet orange). Brazil is now by far the largest global producer — about one-third of world supply.

Citrus × microcarpa is a natural hybrid of the kumquat (Citrus japonica) and the mandarin orange (Citrus reticulata), most likely originating in southern China and brought to the Philippines in prehistory.

Citrus x paradisi is a surprisingly recent hybrid, first documented in Barbados around 1750 as "the forbidden fruit." It is a natural hybrid between sweet orange (Citrus sinensis) and pomelo (Citrus maxima).

Farewell-to-spring (godetia) is native to western North America from British Columbia to central California. Meriwether Lewis collected it in 1806 during the Lewis & Clark expedition. A spring annual with cup-shaped pink-to-lavender flowers, often with a deeper central blotch, of western grasslands.

Ragged-robin, or pink fairies, is a slender Pacific Northwest annual with delicate three-lobed pink petals. Meriwether Lewis collected it at the Lolo Trail in 1806, and Thomas Nuttall named the genus Clarkia for Captain William Clark.

Elegant clarkia (also called mountain garland) is endemic to California. A distinctive spring-blooming annual with four-petaled flowers on slender wands whose petals have narrow "claws" (stalks) supporting rounded blades above — like tiny dancing figures. Widely grown from seed in California-native wildflower meadow plantings.

Silver torch cactus is a Bolivian high-Andean species. Heese described it as Cereus strausii in 1907 honoring German cactophile Leopold Strauss; Backeberg transferred to Cleistocactus in 1934. Forms clumping columns 1-3 meters tall densely covered in white bristles, producing deep carmine-red tubular flowers.

Alpine clematis is native to mountains of Europe and northern Asia. Miller described it as Atragene alpina in 1768, transferred to Clematis by P. Miller. Nodding blue-violet bell-shaped flowers open in spring on the previous year's wood. One of the easiest, hardiest, and most refined clematis for small gardens and cool climates.

Armand's clematis is native to central and southern China and northern Myanmar. Franchet described it in 1885, naming it for Père Armand David, the French missionary-naturalist who collected it. One of the few evergreen clematis, its powerfully fragrant white star flowers in early spring have made it a mainstay of mild-winter pergolas.

Mountain clematis was collected in the western Himalaya by British plant hunter Lady Amherst in 1831 and introduced to British gardens, where its spring cascade of pink and white flowers made it one of the most widely grown large-flowered climbers of the 20th century.

Clematis terniflora occurs naturally in Japan, Korea, Taiwan, and eastern China. Swiss botanist Augustin Pyramus de Candolle described the species in 1818. The epithet terniflora means three-flowered in reference to groups of three flowers on side branches.

Virgin's bower is native to eastern North America. Linnaeus described it in 1753. The common name references the curly silver-white fluffy seedheads remaining after bloom. A native alternative to the invasive C. terniflora, it supports native pollinators and is an important host for the Large Lace Border moth.

Clematis viticella is native to southern and southeastern Europe including Italy, the Balkans, and Turkey. It was introduced to British horticulture around 1569 by John Gerard, the herbalist, from Italy — one of the earliest exotic clematis species grown in England.

Spider flower is a tall South American annual famous for its long-stamened airy flower clusters that bloom at the top of stems while seedpods elongate below. Swiss botanist Emile Hassler, who collected extensively in Paraguay, lent his name to the species.

Rocky Mountain beeplant is a tall native of western North America that Indigenous peoples used as potherb and as a boiled-down black paint pigment for pueblo pottery. It remains an important restoration and pollinator plant across the Great Plains and Southwest.

Mountain pepperbush is a southern Appalachian endemic, described by Michaux in 1803. Taller than summersweet and distinguished by exfoliating cinnamon bark, it was collected in the Great Smokies by early American botanists and is valued as a specimen woodland shrub.

Summersweet is an eastern North American wetland native described by Linnaeus in 1753. Colonial settlers brewed the bark for skin wounds. Its intensely fragrant late-summer white flower spikes and tolerance of wet soils make it a top pollinator shrub.

Japanese clethra is native to Japan, Korea, and eastern China. Siebold and Zuccarini described it in 1839. Tall narrow racemes of fragrant white flowers in midsummer and spectacular exfoliating cinnamon bark on mature stems make it one of the finest small flowering trees for the woodland garden.

Japanese cleyera or sakaki is native to Japan, Taiwan, Korea, and southern China. Thunberg described it in 1783. In Japan it is called sakaki (榊) and is central to Shinto ritual—sacred branches are offered at shrines and household altars, and it is planted around Shinto shrine precincts throughout the country.

The Garden Croton is native to Malaysia, Indonesia, and the western Pacific. It has been cultivated in Pacific island cultures for centuries as a hedge and ceremonial plant; hundreds of vivid cultivars now exist.

Autumn crocus, native to Europe, was described by Linnaeus in 1753. Though resembling a crocus, it is in the lily family. Its alkaloid colchicine, used since ancient Egypt for gout, remains a modern pharmaceutical and a key tool in plant chromosome research.

Coleus, or painted nettle, is a Southeast Asian tropical perennial prized for kaleidoscopic foliage rather than flowers. Victorian Britain saw a coleus mania, and Ball Horticultural's 21st-century sun-tolerant introductions brought a second global renaissance.

Taro is one of humanity's oldest cultivated food plants, domesticated in tropical Asia over 9,000 years ago. The starchy corm feeds hundreds of millions globally; raw leaves and corms are toxic due to calcium oxalate.

Giant elephant ear is native to Southeast Asia, from Bangladesh and southern China through Indochina to Java. Its arrow-shaped leaves up to 1.5 m long make it one of the most dramatic foliage plants of subtropical gardens. Petioles are eaten as a vegetable in Vietnamese and Thai cuisine ("bạc hà").

Rocket larkspur is native to the Mediterranean basin. The annual relative of perennial Delphinium, it is widely grown for its spire-like flower stalks in blue, purple, pink, and white. All parts are toxic — larkspur alkaloids cause significant livestock poisoning in the western US, particularly in young cattle on spring rangeland.

Forking larkspur is a European annual in the buttercup family whose spurred blue-violet flowers colored medieval fields of rye and wheat. Now rare in many European countries because of herbicide-intensive farming, it remains a classic cottage and meadow annual.

Lily-of-the-valley is a Eurasian woodland perennial woven into European folk tradition — Norse myth, the French "Muguet du 1er mai," and royal weddings including Kate Middleton's. All parts are highly toxic, and its fragrance is one of the most imitated in perfumery.

Convolvulus arvensis, field bindweed, is a deep-rooted perennial native to Europe and western Asia whose twining stems and pink-striped white funnel flowers are beautiful but extremely invasive. Its taproot can reach six metres deep, regenerating from tiny fragments, and it is rated among the worst agricultural weeds worldwide.

Large-flowered tickseed is native to the southeastern US and adjacent Mexico. Described by botanist Stephen Elliott in 1821, it became a foundational garden coreopsis and parent of many modern hybrids since the 1990s.

Lance-leaved tickseed is native to the central and southeastern United States. The state wildflower of Florida and Mississippi, its bright yellow daisy flowers and easy-care habit have made it one of the most widely used native wildflowers in North American highway seed mixes and meadow plantings.

Pink tickseed is a rare wetland coreopsis native to coastal plain ponds from Nova Scotia to Georgia. Described by Thomas Nuttall in 1818, it is globally imperiled in the wild but popularized in gardens by the cultivar 'American Dream' in the 1980s.

Threadleaf coreopsis is native to the eastern US, described by Linnaeus in 1753. The cultivar 'Moonbeam' revolutionized American perennial gardens when it won Perennial Plant of the Year in 1992 for its soft yellow flowers and heat tolerance.

Coriandrum sativum is probably native to the eastern Mediterranean or southwest Asia. Seeds have been recovered from the Nahal Hemar cave in Israel dating to approximately 6000 BCE, making it one of the earliest-cultivated spices.

Pagoda dogwood is native to eastern North America from Newfoundland to Georgia. Described by Linnaeus's son in 1782, its distinctive tiered horizontal branching inspired the pagoda common name and made it a signature tree of American native woodland plantings.

Giant dogwood or wedding cake tree is native to China, Japan, Korea, and the Himalayas. Described by Hemsley in 1909, its dramatically tiered horizontal branching earned its elegant common name. The variegated 'Variegata' is among the most iconic specimen trees.

Flowering dogwood is native to eastern North America and is the state tree or flower of several states including Virginia, Missouri, and North Carolina. Its bracts (often called flowers) open in early spring. Since the 1970s, dogwood anthracnose (Discula destructiva) has devastated wild populations in the Appalachian and Northeast forests.

Kousa dogwood is native to East Asia (Japan, Korea, China) and blooms 2-3 weeks later than North America's C. florida. Introduced to Western horticulture in the late 1800s, it is now widely grown as a resistant replacement for the anthracnose-damaged native flowering dogwood.

Cornus mas ranges across southern Europe and western Asia. Its hard dense wood has been used since antiquity for tool handles, spears, and bows—Greek mythology held that the javelin of Romulus, founder of Rome, took root as a cornel tree on the Palatine Hill.

Pacific dogwood is native to the Pacific Northwest. David Douglas collected it in the 1820s, and Thomas Nuttall described it in 1840. Its 6-inch creamy-white bracts are among the largest of any dogwood, and it is the state flower and tree symbol of British Columbia.

Pampas grass is native to the grassland pampas of Argentina, Uruguay, Chile, and southern Brazil. Its huge silvery plumes made it one of the defining Victorian ornamental grasses, but it has become a serious invasive along the California coast, South Africa, New Zealand, Australia, and southern Spain.

Buttercup winter hazel is native to Japan and Taiwan. Siebold and Zuccarini described it in 1835. One of the earliest shrubs to bloom in spring, its soft yellow bell-flower chains open before the leaves, filling the late-winter garden with a delicate cowslip-honey fragrance.

Cosmos is native to Mexico. Spanish priests in 16th-century missions named it Cosmos (Greek for 'ordered beautiful world') for the neat symmetry of its petals. It has naturalized across the world's warm regions as a self-seeding meadow annual.

Sulphur cosmos (yellow cosmos) is native to Mexico and Central America. Widely naturalized across Africa, South Asia, and Southeast Asia where it is now a familiar roadside weed. The intense orange-to-yellow flowers produce natural yellow dye widely used in traditional Asian textile craft.

Crepe ginger or spiral ginger is native to South and Southeast Asia. Not a true ginger but a member of Costaceae, its stems grow in a distinctive spiral and crepe-papery white flowers emerge from red cone-like bracts. Used for over 2,000 years in Ayurvedic and South Asian medicine as keukand.

Pig's ear is a South African succulent widely used in Cape folk medicine for warts, corns, and earache. Introduced to European cultivation in the 17th century, it has a long horticultural pedigree and one of the more severe toxicity profiles among common garden succulents.

Silver jade plant or silver dollar plant is a South African succulent. Miller described it in 1768. Forms thick tree-like 1-3 meter shrub with round blue-gray coin-shaped leaves edged in red. Star-shaped pink-white flowers in winter. Close relative of Crassula ovata (money tree).

The Jade Plant is native to South Africa and Mozambique, where it has been used medicinally by Khoi and Xhosa peoples. In East Asian cultures it is associated with prosperity and given as a housewarming gift.

Crassula perforata, commonly called string of buttons or necklace vine, is a succulent shrublet in the stonecrop family (Crassulaceae) native to rocky slopes and cliffs of the Eastern Cape and KwaZulu-Natal of South Africa.

Miniature pine tree is a widespread southern African succulent. Linnaeus described it in Species Plantarum 1753. Forms upright shrubby 60-90 cm stems with whorls of narrow awl-shaped green leaves giving a small conifer appearance. Small white star-shaped flowers in late spring.

Common hawthorn is native to Europe, northwest Africa, and western Asia, and has been used as a pastoral hedging plant across the British Isles and continental Europe for over a thousand years. It carries deep folklore — in Irish and British tradition, a lone hawthorn in a field is a "fairy tree" that must not be cut.

Powell's crinum is a 19th-century hybrid of Crinum bulbispermum and C. moorei developed in the 1880s by Charles Baden Powell. Its large pink trumpets on 3-foot stems and cold hardiness exceeding most crinums made it the most widely grown garden crinum.

Golden montbretia is native to southern and tropical Africa. Introduced to European horticulture in the 1840s, it became parent of the famous garden hybrid Crocosmia x crocosmiiflora and later the iconic red cultivar 'Lucifer' bred by Alan Bloom in 1966.

Saffron is the dried stigma of this autumn-flowering crocus. The world's most expensive spice by weight, it takes 150,000 flowers to produce one kilogram. Cultivated since at least 1500 BCE in Bronze Age Crete.

The Spring Crocus (Dutch Crocus) is native to Alpine meadows of central and southern Europe. Breeding has turned it into one of the earliest and most reliable spring bulbs across temperate gardens. Moderately toxic to pets.

An obsolete name for Codiaeum variegatum (Garden Croton). The plant has nothing to do with the true Croton genus despite the persistent common name. Native to Malaysia, western Pacific, and northern Australia; mildly toxic due to latex.

Japanese cedar ("sugi" 杉) is the national tree of Japan. Native to Japan and parts of China, it forms the vast commercial softwood-timber plantations that cover roughly 20% of Japanese forested land. The thousand-year-old Jomon Sugi tree on Yakushima Island is a UNESCO World Heritage centerpiece and one of the oldest trees in Asia.

Named after the legendary Brazilian landscape architect Roberto Burle Marx, this species from eastern Brazil has strikingly banded silver-and-green leaves. Like its close relatives in Marantaceae, it performs nightly leaf folding and is considered non-toxic to pets.

The cucumber originates in the Himalayan foothills of India and was domesticated at least 3,000 years ago. Carried west by Greek and Roman traders, it was cherished by Roman Emperor Tiberius who had year-round supply from early greenhouses — possibly the earliest documented greenhouse cultivation.

Cushaw pumpkin or silver-seed gourd is a Mexican and Central American native. C. Huber described it in 1867. One of the five domesticated Cucurbita species. Large vining pumpkin with distinctive striped pear-shaped fruits and large silver-edged seeds. Staple of traditional Mexican cuisine.

Cucurbita maxima originates in the lowlands of what is now Argentina, Bolivia, Uruguay, and Chile, with domestication around 4,000 years ago. It differs from the Mexican C. pepo lineage and includes cultivars such as Hubbard, Atlantic Giant, Kabocha (Japanese kabocha squash), Turban, Jarrahdale.

Butternut squash and many tropical pumpkins are C. moschata, native to Mexico and Central America. Its heat tolerance made it the dominant pumpkin across the American South, the Caribbean, and tropical Africa, displacing the other cultivated Cucurbita in these regions.

Cucurbita pepo has one of the longest cultivation histories of any American crop. Archaeological evidence from Oaxaca, Mexico (Guilá Naquitz Cave) places domestication at 8,000-10,000 years ago, predating maize and beans in the archaeological record.

Cuphea hyssopifolia, commonly called Mexican heather, false heather, or Hawaiian heather, is an evergreen subshrub native to Mexico and Guatemala. Spanish botanist Mariano Lagasca described the species in 1821, the epithet hyssopifolia alluding to its foliage resembling hyssop.

Cigar plant is a Mexican and Jamaican subshrub whose slender orange-red tubular flowers with black-and-white tips resemble lit cigars. Introduced to English greenhouses in the 1840s, it remains a popular Victorian-style hummingbird and butterfly plant today.

Bat-faced cuphea is a Mexican subshrub whose flowers feature two vivid red upper petals suggesting bat ears above a dark purple face. Colombian and American breeders developed the widely grown Tiny Mice and FloriGlory series for modern container gardens.

Arizona cypress is native to the southwestern United States and northern Mexico. Greene described it in 1882. Exceptionally heat- and drought-tolerant, it is widely planted in hot dry regions and across the Mediterranean as a Christmas tree alternative. The blue-foliaged cultivar 'Blue Ice' is popular worldwide.

Italian cypress is native to the eastern Mediterranean and has been the iconic columnar tree of Mediterranean funerary and sacred landscapes since antiquity — planted around Greek and Roman temples, in Persian gardens, and in Tuscan cemeteries. Van Gogh's painted cypresses of Arles and Saint-Rémy made the form a central image of Western art.

Turmeric is native to South Asia and has been cultivated in India for at least 4,000 years. Sacred in Hindu ritual, central to curry powder, and the source of curcumin — a pigment and potential anti-inflammatory compound that has generated thousands of clinical studies and an estimated $1 billion global supplement market.

Australian tree fern is native to eastern Australia, where it grows as a fast-growing tree fern to 12 meters tall in subtropical rainforest. Widely planted in frost-free mild-winter gardens of California, New Zealand, Mediterranean Europe, and Hawaii, where it has also become invasive in Hawaiian native forests.

Cyclamen persicum is native to a band from Greece and the Aegean Islands through Turkey, Lebanon, Israel, and Jordan, where it grows on rocky hillsides and in light woodland. It is one of the most important winter-flowering gift plants in the global horticultural trade.

Staghorn cholla is a Sonoran Desert native. Engelmann ex J.M. Coulter described it in 1896. Forms 1-2 meter branching shrubs with slender cylindrical green stems and unusual variable flower colors ranging from yellow through orange to red, bronze, and dark purple in the same population.

Noble orchid (Chinese "chūn lán" 春兰, Japanese "shunran") is native to China, Japan, Korea, and Taiwan. The "orchid" of classical Chinese literature — one of the "Four Gentlemen" of Chinese brush painting, praised by Confucius as embodying the virtue of the refined scholar. Cultivated in China and Japan for over 2,000 years, prized for form and subtle fragrance rather than showy color.

Lemongrass is a tropical Asian grass central to Thai, Vietnamese, and Indonesian cuisines. Commercial citral production from its essential oil made it a major industrial crop in 20th-century India and Madagascar, supplying the perfume, food-flavoring, and vitamin A synthesis industries.

Black swallow-wort is a twining perennial native to the western Mediterranean, introduced to Massachusetts before 1867 as an ornamental. Its milkweed-family flowers trick monarchs into laying eggs on foliage the caterpillars cannot digest, an ecological trap in invaded northeastern fields.

The globe artichoke is a Mediterranean thistle domesticated in Roman or Arab-era North Africa. Catherine de Medici scandalized 16th-century French court by eating them — considered aphrodisiac and improper for women. Modern cultivation centers on Italy, Egypt, Spain, and California.

Umbrella sedge (also called umbrella palm) is native to Madagascar. Often confused with its large African relative Cyperus papyrus, it is a much smaller, more manageable pond-margin and houseplant sedge, widely grown in containers and small water gardens worldwide.

Yellow nutsedge, believed native to Eurasia and Africa, produces edible tubers called 'tiger nuts' or chufa. Cultivated in ancient Egypt and now in Spain where they are used to make horchata, it is simultaneously a traditional food and one of the world's worst agricultural weeds.

Umbrella papyrus is native to eastern Africa (Madagascar, Kenya, Tanzania, Mozambique). Often sold interchangeably with the smaller Cyperus alternifolius, it is a larger-scaled umbrella sedge more resembling scaled-down papyrus, widely used in container water gardens and as a tropical-look pondside plant.

Papyrus is native to tropical Africa, particularly the Nile delta and tropical African wetlands. Ancient Egyptians processed its pith into papyrus paper — the writing substrate of pharaonic Egypt, classical Greece and Rome, and the early Mediterranean Christian church. The English word "paper" descends from "papyrus."

Purple nutsedge is ranked by weed scientists as the world's worst weed. Native to Africa, southern Europe, and South Asia, it has spread through 90+ countries. Yet in Ayurveda and TCM ('Xiang Fu Zi'), its aromatic tubers are a traditional medicine for women's health.

Cyrilla racemiflora, commonly called swamp titi, leatherwood, or ironwood, is a semi-evergreen shrub or small tree native to acidic bogs, streamheads, and pocosins from Virginia through Florida and west to Texas, with disjunct populations in the Caribbean and South America.

Southern dahlia is a species native to southern Mexico, Guatemala, and Honduras, described by William Sherff in 1947. It grows as a shrubby perennial with small single flowers and is one of the less-known wild dahlias, rarely grown outside specialist collections.

Scarlet dahlia is native to the highlands of Mexico and Guatemala. Aztec gardens cultivated dahlias before Spanish contact; this species reached Europe in 1789 and became a principal ancestor of modern garden dahlia cultivars.

Tree dahlia is native to highland forests of Mexico and Central America. Reaching 20 feet tall on bamboo-like canes, it blooms in late autumn with hanging lavender-pink flowers. Introduced to Europe in the 1860s, it remains a dramatic feature plant in mild climates.

Merck's dahlia, from the mountains of central Mexico, was described by Lehmann in 1839 and introduced to European gardens by the Hamburg nurseryman Heinrich Merck. Unlike showy hybrid dahlias, it bears single lavender-pink daisy-like flowers on tall airy stems.

The Dahlia is the national flower of Mexico, where Aztecs cultivated it for the edible tubers (cocoxochitl) before European contact. Spanish botanists sent tubers to Madrid in 1789, starting a 230-year European breeding tradition.

Garland daphne or rose daphne is native to the mountains of central and southern Europe. Linnaeus described it in 1753. A low-growing evergreen shrub beloved for its intensely fragrant pink flower clusters in late spring, it has been cherished in rock gardens since the 18th century despite being notoriously difficult to establish.

February daphne or mezereon is native to Europe and western Asia. Linnaeus described it in 1753. One of the earliest-blooming shrubs, its fragrant purple-pink flowers open on bare stems before the leaves. Bark has been used in traditional medicine since ancient times—but is extremely toxic.

Winter daphne is native to China, later introduced to Japan where it has been cultivated for centuries. Thunberg described it in 1784. The intensely fragrant late-winter flowers—one of the sweetest scents in horticulture—have made it a beloved cold-season shrub despite its reputation for sudden unexplained death in cultivation.

Dasylirion wheeleri is native to arid slopes of Arizona, New Mexico, Chihuahua, and Sonora. It was described in 1878 and named for geologist George Wheeler, leader of an 1870s US survey of the American Southwest.

Datura stramonium — known as jimsonweed, thornapple, or devil's trumpet — is likely native to Central America (Mexico, Central American highlands), though it has naturalized so thoroughly across temperate and tropical regions worldwide that its original range is debated.

Datura wrightii, sacred datura, is a night-blooming perennial native to the American Southwest and northern Mexico. Indigenous peoples used it ceremonially and medicinally, though all parts are dangerously toxic. Gardeners grow it for its huge white trumpet flowers that open at dusk and release a heavy, sweet perfume.

Daucus carota has two subspecies: wild D. carota subsp. carota (Queen Anne's Lace) is native to most of temperate Europe and Asia and is a widespread roadside weed. The cultivated carrot (D. carota subsp.

Dove tree or handkerchief tree was discovered by French missionary Père Armand David in 1869 in China. Ernest Wilson made two famous expeditions (1899, 1903) to introduce it, nearly dying on the 1903 trip. Its huge white bracts fluttering in wind resemble doves or handkerchiefs.

Hardy ice plant is native to the grasslands of South Africa's Drakensberg and Lesotho highlands, at elevations up to 2,500 meters. Unusually cold-tolerant for an African succulent (hardy to zone 5), it has become a widely planted drought-tolerant flowering groundcover in the western US, Mediterranean Europe, and South Africa's own xeriscape industry.

Candle larkspur is a tall perennial of Eurasian mountain meadows, parent of the English border Delphinium. Nineteenth-century British breeders, notably Kelway's and later Blackmore & Langdon, developed the towering blue hybrid spires that defined the Edwardian herbaceous border.

Noble dendrobium is native to the Himalayas, southern China, and Southeast Asia. One of the "51 fundamental herbs" of traditional Chinese medicine ("shí hú" 石斛), used for over 2,000 years. Produces fragrant 5-8 cm pink-and-white flowers along bare pseudobulbs in late winter — one of the most popular orchids for home cultivation worldwide.

Fuzzy deutzia, native to Japan, has been cultivated for centuries under the name 'utsugi'. Described by Siebold and Zuccarini in 1835, it was used in Japanese carpentry, fencing, and rural woodworks. Its wood is still used for traditional sake measures (masu).

Slender deutzia is a small arching shrub native to Japan, introduced to Western horticulture by Philipp Franz von Siebold in 1840. Its clouds of pure-white star-shaped flowers in late spring, combined with tolerance of shade and cold, made it a staple of 19th- and 20th-century cottage and shrub-border planting.

Purple deutzia, from Yunnan in southwestern China, was described by Rehder in 1912. Collected by French missionary botanist Jean Marie Delavay in the 1880s, it contributed purple-pink coloring to Lemoine's famous deutzia hybrids.

Fuzzy deutzia is native to Japan and China. Thunberg described it in 1780. The epithet scabra (rough) refers to the sandpaper-textured leaves once used in Japan to polish wooden objects. Tall arching sprays of white to pink-tinged bell flowers in early summer made it a Victorian garden staple.

Hybrid deutzia is a group of garden crosses produced mainly by the French nursery Lemoine of Nancy in the late 19th and early 20th centuries, combining species such as D. longifolia, D. purpurascens, and D. discolor for larger, pinker flowers.

Sweet William is native to southern Europe. The common name's origin is debated — possibly William the Conqueror, possibly William of Orange, possibly William Shakespeare. A cottage garden staple for four centuries.

The Carnation has been cultivated for over 2,000 years, appearing in ancient Greek literature as a garland flower. In 20th-century labor and political history, red carnations became a global symbol of May Day (Labour Day) and socialist movements.

China pink is native to northern China, Korea, Mongolia, and the Russian Far East. Introduced to European horticulture in the 1710s via the French Jesuit missionary-botanists in Beijing. One of the foundation parents of modern bedding dianthus, producing flat-faced 3-4 cm flowers in vivid pink, red, white, and striking bicolored eye patterns.

Maiden pink is native across much of Europe and western Asia. A small creeping mat-forming dianthus producing masses of small 1.5-2 cm single pink-to-magenta flowers from late spring through summer — widely used as a groundcover in rock gardens, at stepping-stone edges, and at the front of sunny perennial borders.

Cheddar pink is a European alpine dianthus native to limestone cliffs in the Cheddar Gorge of England, where it is a rigorously protected rarity. Continental populations extend through France to central Europe, and garden cultivars remain rock garden classics.

Yellow hardy dianthus is a Balkan pink native to serpentine slopes in Bosnia and Herzegovina, notable as the only consistently yellow-flowered species in its genus. Garden cultivars such as Yellow Harmony remain the benchmark yellow for dianthus beds.

Cottage pink is native to central and eastern Europe. The foundational species of the English "pink" — an important cottage-garden plant since Tudor times — and the source of many old heritage cultivars including "Mrs. Sinkins" (1868) and "Sops-in-Wine," some of which are still grown 200+ years after their introduction.

Diascia barberae, commonly called twinspur or Barber's twinspur, is a low-growing tender perennial native to moist cliffs and stream banks of KwaZulu-Natal, South Africa. English botanist Joseph Dalton Hooker described the species in 1870, honoring Mary Elizabeth Barber.

Dutchman's breeches is a spring ephemeral native to eastern North American woodlands. Described by Linnaeus as Fumaria cucullaria in 1753, its distinctive pantaloon-shaped white flowers emerge with snowdrops and disappear by summer, making it an iconic spring wildflower.

Pacific bleeding heart is native to the Pacific Northwest from British Columbia to California. David Douglas collected it in the 1820s. It has parented modern repeat-blooming hybrids like 'Luxuriant' and 'King of Hearts' that extended the bleeding heart season.

Dicentra spectabilis (recently reclassified as Lamprocapnos spectabilis by molecular systematics, though horticulture retains the old name) is native to Siberia, Heilongjiang, Liaoning, and other northern parts of China, Korea, and northern Japan.

Kidney weed is native to Australia and New Zealand. From the 1950s through the 1970s it was marketed as a no-mow green-leaved "lawn" across California and the US Southwest, but heavy water needs and weed problems caused its decline. Related species are now popular as hanging-basket plants.

Soft tree fern is native to the cool-temperate rainforests of eastern Australia, Tasmania, and New Zealand. Despite the species epithet "antarctica" it does not grow in Antarctica — the name refers to its far-southern distribution. Historically exported in large numbers from Tasmania as landscape specimens; export is now restricted.

Dumb Cane earned its English name from temporary speech loss caused by chewing its sap, a reaction enslaved people in the 18th-century Caribbean were reportedly punished with. Native to the West Indies and tropical South America, it remains one of the most severely oral-irritating common houseplants.

Foxglove is a Eurasian biennial whose leaves yielded the cardiac glycoside digitalis — the mainstay heart-failure drug for two centuries. English physician William Withering systematized its medical use in 1785 after learning the recipe from a Shropshire folk healer, founding modern cardiac pharmacology.

Smooth crabgrass, native to Eurasia, is one of the most common US summer-annual lawn weeds along with its cousin D. sanguinalis. Schreber described it in 1771. It is distinguished by smooth, nearly hairless leaves and shorter growth habit.

Large crabgrass, native to Europe and western Asia, has spread globally as one of the world's worst lawn and agricultural weeds. Its small grains were actually cultivated as the cereal 'Manna grass' in parts of central and eastern Europe until the early 20th century.

Cape marigold, or Namaqualand daisy, is a South African spring annual producing carpets of vibrant orange daisies across its native semi-arid habitats. It is the iconic wildflower of the Cape west coast flower season and a classic cool-season bedding annual.

Sticky monkeyflower (formerly Mimulus aurantiacus) is native to California and southwestern Oregon. A shrubby perennial with sticky resinous dark-green leaves and bright orange-to-yellow tubular flowers from spring through early summer — one of the most important hummingbird plants of California coastal chaparral.

Diplotaxis tenuifolia, commonly called wild rocket, perennial wall rocket, or sand rocket, is a perennial herb in the cabbage family (Brassicaceae) native to the Mediterranean basin, temperate Europe (including much of Britain), North Africa, and southwestern Asia, typically growing on.

Distictis buccinatoria (now transferred by some authorities to Amphilophium buccinatorium based on molecular data) is native to central and southern Mexico. Austrian botanist Nikolaus Joseph von Jacquin originally described the species in 1804 as Bignonia buccinatoria.

Livingstone daisy (ice plant) is native to coastal South Africa. A small spreading cool-season annual succulent producing masses of brilliantly iridescent daisy-form flowers in vivid pinks, oranges, yellows, reds, and magentas that close at night and open only in bright sunlight.

The Corn Plant is native to tropical Africa and named for the intense sweet fragrance of its nocturnal flowers. Grown as a houseplant since the 19th century, its cultivar 'Massangeana' was central to the early 21st-century 'Lucky Bamboo of Cuba' Stachybotrys concern later debunked.

The Madagascar Dragon Tree is endemic to Madagascar. Its slender stems and tufts of red-edged leaves resemble miniature palms and became a 1970s and 1980s design staple. Like other Dracaenas, it contains saponins mildly toxic to pets.

Song of India or pleomele is a Madagascar, Mozambique, and Indian Ocean islands native. Lamarck described it in 1786. Forms 3-5 meter slow-growing tree with whorls of strap-shaped glossy green leaves. Variegated 'Song of India' with yellow-green stripes is a major commercial houseplant.

Dracaena sanderiana is native to Central Africa and is not actually a bamboo. Marketed globally as 'lucky bamboo' beginning in the 1990s through feng shui branding, it became one of the most-sold houseplants worldwide, with the number of stalks carrying specific symbolic meanings.

Snake plant or mother-in-law's tongue is a tropical West African native, formerly Sansevieria trifasciata. Prain described it in 1903; the 2018 Takawira-Nyenya revision merged Sansevieria into Dracaena. Upright sword-shaped leaves with cross-banded pattern. One of the most popular houseplants worldwide.

Drosanthemum hispidum, dew flower or rosy dewplant, is a trailing succulent native to the Cape region of South Africa, forming low mats of hairy-looking succulent leaves covered in spring with small sparkling pink daisy-like flowers. Glistening crystalline surface cells give the foliage the characteristic dewy ice-plant appearance, and the species is widely planted in Mediterranean and California gardens.

Male fern is native across Europe, North Africa, and temperate Asia. Its dried rhizome was one of the most important anthelmintic (tapeworm-expelling) drugs in European and later American pharmacy from antiquity through the mid-20th century — listed in the US Pharmacopoeia until 1965, when safer synthetic drugs replaced it.

Dudleya brittonii is native to a relatively restricted area of coastal northwestern Baja California, Mexico, from roughly Ensenada south to around El Rosario, growing on steep coastal rocky cliffs within about 20 km of the Pacific Ocean.

Dudleya pulverulenta is native to rocky cliffs, steep road-cuts, and canyon walls of Southern California — from Santa Barbara County south through Ventura, Los Angeles, Orange, Riverside, and San Diego Counties — and into northern Baja California, Mexico.

Silver carpet or dymondia is a South African endemic in the daisy family. Compton described it in 1953. Forms extremely flat 2-3 cm silver mats with tiny yellow flowers. Prized drought-tolerant lawn substitute in California and Mediterranean climates. Monotypic genus.

The Areca Palm is native to Madagascar, where it is critically endangered in the wild despite being one of the most mass-produced palms on earth. Nearly all commercial plants originate from seed farms in India, Malaysia, and Central America.

Native to rocky highlands of northeast Mexico, Echeveria agavoides produces pointed sharp-tipped leaves that give it the species name ('agave-like'). Red-edged forms like 'Lipstick' and 'Ebony' are widely traded among collectors.

The Mexican Snowball is native to rocky cliffs in Hidalgo state, Mexico. Described in 1905, it became one of the defining rosette succulents of 20th-century horticulture and has parented many of today's most popular Echeveria hybrids.

Ghost echeveria is a Mexican species from the limestone hills of Nuevo León, described in 1958. Its chalky lilac-pink rosettes made it a late 20th-century succulent icon, central to the social-media succulent boom of the 2010s.

Pulido's echeveria was described in 1977 from Puebla, Mexico, named for botanical collector Reyes Pulido Flores. Its chubby blue-green leaves edged in crimson and bright yellow spring flowers have made it a favorite of small-rosette succulent growers and Korean hybridizers.

Echeveria setosa is native to the highlands of Puebla and Veracruz in Mexico, growing at 2,000-3,000 meters. It was described by Rose and Purpus in 1910 and long grown as a distinctive "fuzzy" echeveria — the species name "setosa" means "bristly.

Narrow-leaf coneflower is the original "Echinacea" of Plains tribe medicine — used by Lakota, Cheyenne, Pawnee, and Comanche for snakebite, toothache, and infections. It was the Echinacea species of the 19th-century Meyer's Blood Purifier patent medicine and entered European phytomedicine before E. purpurea.

Echinacea laevigata occupies thin limestone and diabase soils in open woodlands of the southeastern Appalachians. It was long confused with E. purpurea until Cronquist recognized it as distinct in 1945.

Echinacea pallida is native to the tallgrass prairie and oak-savanna regions of the central United States, from Illinois and Iowa south to Texas. Like its showier cousin E. purpurea and the narrower-ranged E.

Purple coneflower is native to eastern and central North America, where Lakota, Cheyenne, and Comanche peoples used its roots for snakebite, toothache, and infections. German researcher Gerhard Madaus brought it to Europe in 1938; it became a best-selling herbal supplement in the late 20th century.

Golden barrel cactus is critically endangered in the wild: the original population near Zimapán, Mexico was largely submerged by the 1995 Zimapán Dam. Ironically, it is one of the most propagated decorative cacti on Earth, with millions of cultivated specimens.

Barnyard grass is a cosmopolitan summer-annual grass likely native to Eurasia. One of the world's worst weeds of rice, it has co-evolved with cultivated rice over millennia as a crop mimic. Its seeds were eaten in prehistoric Japan before rice domestication.

Easter lily cactus is a South American cactus. Link described it as Cereus oxygonus in 1827; Zuccarini transferred it to Echinopsis in 1837. Forms clumping globose green stems with 14 prominent ribs and produces enormous night-blooming pink-white fragrant trumpet flowers.

Water hyacinth is native to the Amazon basin of South America. Introduced worldwide as an ornamental for its attractive purple flowers, it has become one of the world's worst invasive aquatic weeds — a single plant can produce 140 million daughter plants in a year, clogging lakes, rivers, and reservoirs throughout the tropics. Now taxonomically placed in genus Pontederia as P. crassipes.

Elaeagnus umbellata, autumn olive, is a nitrogen-fixing deciduous shrub native to East Asia introduced to the United States in 1830 and widely promoted after 1940 for wildlife habitat and erosion control. Birds spread its abundant red berries, and the species now colonizes eastern roadsides and old fields, altering soil nitrogen balance.

Green cardamom is native to the rainforests of southwestern India (Kerala's Cardamom Hills) and Sri Lanka. The third most expensive spice by weight (after saffron and vanilla), it is foundational to Indian, Arab, and Scandinavian cuisines — Sweden alone consumes a disproportionate share.

Goosegrass is a warm-season annual grass native to Africa and southern Asia, now a global weed of paved surfaces, driveways, and summer lawns. Its wiry flattened stems and white-ribbed leaf bases distinguish it as one of the toughest herbicide-resistant lawn weeds.

Engelmann daisy is a tough yellow-flowered perennial of the US Great Plains named for 19th-century German-American botanist George Engelmann. It is a dependable wildflower of Texas roadsides and a staple of native restoration seed mixes.

Enkianthus campanulatus is native to the mountainous regions of Japan (Hokkaido, Honshu, Shikoku, Kyushu). Dutch botanist Friedrich Anton Wilhelm Miquel formally described it in 1863. The genus Enkianthus was established by the French botanist Joao de Loureiro in 1790.

Ethiopian banana or enset is native to the highlands of East Africa and is the staple carbohydrate crop of Ethiopia's Southern Nations region, feeding more than 20 million people. Unlike true bananas, the fruit is inedible; food comes from fermented starch scraped from the corm and pseudostem.

Ephedra sinica has been used in Chinese medicine as ma huang for 5000+ years for asthma and fever. Ephedrine was isolated from it in 1885. It became the first mass-produced asthma drug and remains the biosynthetic source of pseudoephedrine and related bronchodilators.

California fuchsia (hummingbird trumpet) is native to California, southern Oregon, Nevada, and southwestern Idaho. Blooms in late summer and early autumn with tubular bright-scarlet flowers — the single most important late-summer and fall hummingbird nectar plant of California-native horticulture, critical fuel for fall migrating hummingbirds.

Queen of the night (also called Dutchman's pipe cactus) is native to tropical Mexico and Central America. A night-blooming epiphytic cactus, its enormous fragrant white flowers open for a single night — often attended by gathered families in Chinese, Indian, Sri Lankan, and Filipino cultural traditions as an auspicious event.

Native to the Solomon Islands, it was popularized worldwide after NASA's 1989 Clean Air Study named it a top air-purifying houseplant. In much of Asia it is called the 'Money Plant' and associated with prosperity.

Dragon tail plant is a widespread tropical Asian aroid, the wild progenitor of Epipremnum aureum (pothos). Linnaeus described it as Pothos pinnatus in 1753; Engler transferred it to Epipremnum in 1908. Adult leaves develop distinctive pinnate splits. Variegated 'Cebu Blue' is popular cultivar.

Equisetum hyemale is a spore-reproducing vascular plant in the Equisetaceae family — the sole modern family of an extremely ancient plant lineage. Equisetophytes (horsetails and their extinct relatives) dominated Carboniferous swamp forests 350-300 million years ago alongside tree-sized lycophytes.

Elliott's lovegrass is a southeastern US coastal native. S. Watson described it in 1890 honoring Stephen Elliott. Smaller than Eragrostis spectabilis with softer silvery-blue-green foliage and airy silver-tan panicles. 'Wind Dancer' is a popular cool-season-growing cultivar.

Purple lovegrass is a widespread native of eastern and central North America. Pursh described it as Poa spectabilis in 1814; Steudel transferred it to Eragrostis. Low tufted grass producing ethereal reddish-purple cloud-like panicles that turn entire meadows pink in late summer.

Eremurus robustus grows on rocky mountain slopes of the Tian Shan and Pamir-Alai ranges in Tajikistan, Kyrgyzstan, and neighboring Central Asian countries. Eduard August von Regel of the Imperial Botanical Garden of St.

Canadian horseweed, native to North America, was described by Linnaeus in 1753. It spread to Europe in the 17th century, possibly via a stuffed bird in Paris, and is now the worst-resistance weed of US no-till agriculture — the first weed confirmed resistant to glyphosate in 2000.

Santa Cruz Island buckwheat is native to only three California Channel Islands — Santa Cruz, Santa Rosa, and Anacapa. A rare narrow-endemic native but relatively vigorous in garden cultivation, where it forms a 1-1.5 meter woody-trunked subshrub with flat clusters of pink-tinged white flowers through summer and fall. A particularly useful California-native honey and pollinator plant for coastal gardens.

California buckwheat is native to coastal and inland Southern California and northern Baja California. One of the most important native pollinator and honey plants of Southern California — summer-blooming creamy-white flower clusters support native bees, butterflies, beetles, and wasps, and produce a prized regional "buckwheat honey" from commercial beekeepers.

Arugula or garden rocket is a Mediterranean and western Asian native. Miller described it in 1768 based on pre-Linnaean material. Annual peppery-flavored salad brassica cultivated since Ancient Greek and Roman times. Modern salad-bowl resurgence since the 1990s. Now often classified as Eruca vesicaria subsp. sativa.

Dog's-tooth violet is native to European mountain woodlands. Linnaeus described it in 1753. The name refers to the toothlike white corms. Its mottled leaves and pink reflexed flowers made it a classic European woodland bulb known since medieval times.

Giant white fawn lily is a spring ephemeral of Pacific Northwest woodlands and oak savannas, producing elegant nodding cream flowers above mottled leaves. Indigenous peoples roasted the deep bulbs as food, and settlers admired the species as a rival to eastern dogtooth violets.

Pink fawn lily, or mahogany fawn lily, is a spring ephemeral of moist Pacific Northwest forests and streamsides. Its deep pink recurved petals and mottled leaves make it one of the most striking members of its genus and a coveted woodland garden subject.

California poppy is native to California, Oregon, and northwestern Mexico. Named in 1816 for Johann Friedrich Eschscholtz, surgeon-naturalist on the Russian Rurik expedition. The state flower of California since 1903 — Californians celebrate California Poppy Day on April 6, and "Super Bloom" years produce vast orange hillside displays that draw millions of visitors.

Torch ginger is native to the rainforests of insular Southeast Asia (Indonesia, Malaysia, and the Philippines) and Melanesia. Its immense torch-shaped pink-red inflorescence — often over 20 cm long on a 1-meter stalk — is an iconic image of Southeast Asian tropical gardens. The young flower buds are a key ingredient of Malaysian laksa and several Indonesian dishes.

Two-color pineapple lily, native to grasslands of eastern South Africa and Lesotho, was described by J.G. Baker in 1878. Its pineapple-crown inflorescence and pale green flowers edged in purple made it a popular Victorian curiosity that remains a striking summer perennial.

Pineapple lily is native to southern Africa. Described by William Aiton in 1789, its pineapple-like flower spikes crowned with a leafy tuft have made it a favorite subtropical bulb that has become increasingly popular in temperate summer borders since the 1990s.

Euonymus alatus, burning bush or winged spindle, is a deciduous shrub native to East Asia introduced to the United States in 1860 for its spectacular scarlet autumn foliage. Birds disperse the orange-red fruits into forests, where the shrub now forms dense thickets across the Northeast that suppress native understory plants.

Strawberry bush or hearts-a-bustin' is native to the eastern United States. Linnaeus described it in 1753. Its extraordinary warty pink-red capsules splitting open to reveal orange-red seeds in autumn give it the folksy name hearts-a-bustin'-with-love. A favorite browse of white-tailed deer that limits it in many areas.

Wintercreeper is native to eastern Asia. Turczaninow described it in 1863 and named it for Robert Fortune, the Scottish plant collector who introduced it to the West. Vigorous evergreen groundcover with many variegated cultivars—but highly invasive in the eastern United States and discouraged from new plantings.

Japanese spindle is native to Japan, Korea, and China. Thunberg described it in 1780. A tough evergreen shrub widely used for hedging and topiary in mild coastal climates. Known in Japan as masaki (柾) and planted since ancient times around homes and shrines for year-round greenery.

Sweet-scented Joe Pye weed is native to eastern and central North America. The common name honors a Native American (possibly Mohican) herbalist named Joseph Shauquethqueat (anglicized "Joe Pye") who reportedly used the plant to cure a typhus epidemic in colonial New England. Recent taxonomic work moves it to the genus Eutrochium as E. purpureum.

Mottled spurge or dragon bones is an Indian and Sri Lankan native. Haworth described it in 1812. Forms 3-5 meter candelabriform shrub with 3-4 angled stems marked with pale mottling. 'Cristata' crested form and 'White Ghost' are highly prized ornamental sports. Toxic milky sap.

Spotted spurge is a warm-season annual native to eastern North America, described by Linnaeus in 1753. A low prostrate mat plant of sidewalks, driveways, and summer lawns, it bleeds sticky white latex sap when broken — a skin irritant that typifies the genus.

Crown of thorns is endemic to Madagascar and was reputedly introduced to the Middle East in antiquity. Christian tradition connects it to the crown placed on Christ's head at the Crucifixion, and it remains a spring-flowering potted plant of religious symbolic significance.

Baseball plant is a critically endangered succulent endemic to a tiny area of South Africa's Great Karoo. Over-collection by late 19th- and 20th-century horticulturists nearly exterminated it in the wild, making it one of the first succulents protected under CITES Appendix I.

Prostrate spurge is a warm-season annual native to the American tropics, now spread worldwide as a weed of paving cracks and summer lawns. Willdenow described it in 1800. Its sticky white latex sap is a skin irritant typical of Euphorbia, though less potent than E. maculata.

Poinsettia is native to Mexico and Guatemala. Its transformation into the iconic American Christmas plant began with Joel Roberts Poinsett, first US Minister to Mexico, who brought cuttings back in 1828. The modern commercial poinsettia industry was built by the Ecke family of California, who dominated production from the 1900s through 2008.

Pencil Cactus — not actually a cactus — is a tree-sized succulent euphorbia native to arid Africa. Its caustic milky latex is severely toxic, causing blindness on eye contact and being investigated as a potential biofuel feedstock.

African milk tree or cathedral cactus is a Central African native of uncertain wild origin. Miller described it in 1768. Forms upright 2-3 meter three-angled green stems with small red-tinged leaves along the ridges. 'Rubra' with red-purple stems is a popular cultivar.

Lisianthus, or prairie gentian, is a Great Plains native producing rose-like flowers with subtle color. Japanese breeders in the 1930s and 1960s transformed the wild species into one of the world's top cut flowers, sold in florist shops worldwide.

Giraldi's pearlbush is a central Chinese shrub described by Hesse in 1902, honoring the Italian Franciscan missionary Giuseppe Giraldi. Introduced to Western gardens by E.H. Wilson around 1907, it produces profuse white flowers opening from round pearl-like buds.

Common pearlbush is native to central and eastern China. Lindley described it as Amelanchier racemosa in 1858; Rehder transferred it to Exochorda. Pure white flowers in upright racemes open in April-May, opening from perfectly round pearl-like buds that give the genus its common name.

American beech is native to eastern North America. Described by Joseph Ehrhart in 1788, it forms majestic smooth-barked trees that can live 300+ years. Beechnuts were an essential Indigenous food and supported the now-extinct passenger pigeon in enormous seasonal concentrations.

European beech produces the densest, most uniform canopy shade of any European forest tree, creating the characteristic shaded silent "cathedral forest" beneath mature stands. Its hard wood gives its English name to the "book" (Germanic "boke" meant both beech and writing tablet) and to "Buchenwald."

Fallopia japonica (syn. Reynoutria japonica, Polygonum cuspidatum), Japanese knotweed, is a rhizomatous perennial native to Japan, China, Korea, and Taiwan. Philipp Franz von Siebold collected plants from volcanic slopes near Nagasaki and sold them through his Leiden nursery from 1850.

Umbrella bamboo was collected in central China by Ernest Wilson in 1907 and named for his daughter Muriel. Unlike running Phyllostachys, Fargesia is clumping and non-spreading, the preferred bamboo for cold-climate gardens. All cultivated plants flowered simultaneously worldwide in the 1990s.

Japanese aralia ("yatsude" 八手) is native to Japan and southern Korea. Its huge glossy tropical-looking palmate leaves make it one of the most dramatic hardy shade plants in temperate horticulture — a Victorian and Edwardian favorite that gives almost-tropical effect in cool British, Irish, and American coastal gardens without actually requiring tropical conditions.

Blue marguerite, or blue daisy, is a South African subshrub with true-blue daisy flowers surrounding bright yellow centers. Introduced to European and North American horticulture in the 19th century, it remains a favorite of Mediterranean climate gardens worldwide.

Blue barrel cactus is native to central Mexico's limestone hills and is distinguished by its blue-glaucous body and straw-yellow spines. Introduced to European horticulture in the 19th century, it remains a collector's cactus valued for its symmetrical architectural form.

Blue fescue is native to southern France and likely wider western Europe. Its dense clumps of fine blue-silver leaves made it one of the first ornamental grasses to gain mainstream landscape acceptance in the US in the 1980s-1990s, when Wolfgang Oehme and James van Sweden's "New American Garden" movement popularized ornamental grasses.

'Audrey' is a trade name for Ficus benghalensis, the Indian banyan, selected as a houseplant alternative to Ficus lyrata. Linnaeus described F. benghalensis in 1753. The cultivar name 'Audrey' became popular after 2015 when the plant entered the Western indoor-plant market.

Ficus benjamina is native from India and Nepal through Southeast Asia to the Philippines and northern Australia, where it grows into a large spreading evergreen with drooping branchlets that give it its English name.

Ficus carica has extraordinarily ancient cultivation history. Archaeological evidence from Gilgal I in the Jordan Valley, where carbonized parthenocarpic (seedless, propagated from cuttings) figs were dated to 9400 BCE, suggests fig was the first tree domesticated — predating cereal domestication b.

Ficus elastica was the world's primary source of natural rubber through the mid-1800s, tapped commercially in Assam, Burma, and Malaya before the South American Hevea brasiliensis — easier to plant in neat rows — displaced it on plantations in Ceylon and Malaya by the 1890s.

Ficus lyrata grows naturally in the lowland tropical rainforests of western Africa, from Cameroon to Sierra Leone, where it typically begins life as an epiphyte in the crown of a host tree before sending roots to the ground and eventually strangling its host — a growth habit called hemi-epiphytism.

The Indian Laurel Fig is native to tropical Asia, ranging from India through Southeast Asia to northern Australia. A major urban street tree across subtropical cities and one of the most popular bonsai subjects worldwide.

The Creeping Fig is native to East Asia. Widely grown to soften walls in gardens from the Mediterranean to Japan, its mature fruits are the basis of a traditional Taiwanese jelly (愛玉 àiyù), made from pectin released by the seeds.

Triangle fig or sweetheart fig is a southern African native. Warburg described it in 1894. Small tree-like fig with distinctive triangular wedge-shaped leaves that appear to have been snipped off at the tips. Variegated 'Variegata' with cream-edged leaves is the main horticultural form.

The Nerve Plant is native to the lowland rainforests of Peru, Colombia, and Ecuador. A mid-century terrarium staple, it is famous for dramatically wilting when thirsty and reviving within minutes of watering — a behavior that earned it the nickname 'drama queen'.

Fennel is native to the Mediterranean and has been cultivated since ancient Egyptian and Greek times. Central to Italian cuisine (finocchio bulb, seeds in sausages), absinthe distillation, and Indian paan digestif tradition. The sweet bulb cultivar was selected in 17th-century Florence.

Border forsythia is a hybrid between two East Asian species (F. suspensa and F. viridissima) that originated in Germany around 1880. Its early-spring yellow bloom on bare branches — one of the first woody plants to flower in temperate gardens — has made it a ubiquitous ornamental across Europe and North America.

Dwarf fothergilla is native to the Atlantic and Gulf Coastal Plain. L. (Linnaeus) named the genus in 1774 to honor John Fothergill, the English physician-botanist; the species honors Scottish naturalist Alexander Garden. Bottlebrush white spring flowers and spectacular scarlet-orange fall color have made it a native garden favorite.

Large fothergilla is a rare Appalachian native described by John Loudon in 1838 and named for English Quaker physician John Fothergill. It bears fragrant white bottlebrush flowers before leaf-out and some of the most brilliant fall color of any North American shrub.

Fragaria x ananassa is a hybrid of two American Fragaria species that first crossed in Europe, not in the Americas. The Virginian strawberry (F. virginiana) had been brought to Europe from North America in the early 17th century.

Franklin tree was discovered by John and William Bartram in 1765 along Georgia's Altamaha River. Last seen in the wild in 1803, it is extinct in nature and survives only through descendants of Bartram's original cultivated stock—one of horticulture's most poignant stories.

European ash is the Yggdrasil of Norse mythology — the world tree connecting the nine realms. Its tough flexible wood shaped spear shafts, tool handles, and cart wheels across Europe for millennia. Emerald ash borer and ash dieback are now collapsing ash populations across Europe and North America.

Green ash is native to eastern and central North America. Described by Heinrich Marschall in 1785, it was once among America's most widely planted street trees. Since 2002, emerald ash borer has killed tens of millions of trees, making it functionally extinct in many regions.

Freesia is native to South Africa's Cape region. Named in 1866 after German physician Friedrich Freese, its intensely sweet fragrance made it a major 20th-century cut flower, particularly in Japanese and Dutch production.

California flannelbush is native to chaparral and oak woodland of California and southwestern Arizona. Named for John C. Frémont, the 19th-century American explorer and first California senator who collected the species during his 1846 expedition. Produces spectacular large bright-yellow flowers, but foliage hairs cause significant skin irritation to sensitive gardeners.

Crown imperial, native from Turkey through Iran to the Himalayas, was introduced to Vienna in the 1570s by Clusius via Constantinople. Its stately whorled orange bells atop crowned tufts of leaves made it a Renaissance-garden status symbol depicted in countless Dutch paintings.

Snake's head fritillary, native to European wet meadows, was described by Linnaeus in 1753. Its distinctive checkered purple-and-white bells earned it the Latin epithet 'meleagris' (guinea fowl). Once widespread, it is now a protected species in many UK meadows.

Michailovsky's fritillary is a small Turkish alpine discovered in 1904 and rediscovered in 1965 after being thought extinct. Its unique bicolor bronze-and-yellow bell flowers made it an instant hit in rock gardens when Dutch bulb firms began propagating it in the 1970s.

Fritillaria persica grows on rocky slopes and open woodlands from southern Turkey through northern Iraq and into western Iran and the Caucasus. Linnaeus described it in Species Plantarum (1753). It was grown in Persian palace gardens for centuries under the name 'lale-ye Farsi', and was introduced.

Fuchsia magellanica, called hardy fuchsia or Magellan fuchsia, is the most cold-tolerant fuchsia species, native to cool temperate forests and coastal bluffs of southern Chile and Argentina, including Tierra del Fuego.

Fuchsia triphylla, the honeysuckle fuchsia, is a tropical shrub native to Hispaniola in the Caribbean. French Franciscan botanist Charles Plumier collected the species in 1696 or 1697 and formally described it in his Nova Plantarum Americanarum Genera of 1703.

Garden fuchsia is a horticultural hybrid group derived primarily from South American Fuchsia magellanica and F. fulgens. British nurserymen produced the first crosses in the 1830s, spawning a Victorian fuchsia craze that established today's thousands of cultivars.

Indian blanket (also called firewheel) is native to the central and southern United States and Mexico. The state wildflower of Oklahoma, its dramatic concentric red-and-yellow daisy flowers carpet prairies and sandy soils from Texas to the Dakotas and are a staple of warm-season American wildflower meadow plantings.

Giant snowdrop is native to western Turkey and was described in 1875 by J.G. Baker, honoring the English collector Henry John Elwes who introduced it to Western cultivation. Larger and earlier-blooming than G. nivalis, it is a cornerstone of modern galanthophile collections.

Common snowdrop is a European native described by Linnaeus in 1753. Long used in folk medicine for headaches, its alkaloid galantamine is now a major pharmaceutical for Alzheimer's disease. Eastern European 'galanthomania' has driven cultivar collecting since the 1990s.

Galinsoga parviflora is native to the Peruvian Andes and parts of Central America. Spanish botanists Ruiz and Pavón collected it during their 1777-1788 South American expedition and brought living plants to the Royal Botanic Gardens of Madrid, where José Antonio Cavanilles described it in 1794.

Shaggy soldier, a sister species to Galinsoga parviflora, is native to Mexico and Central America. Ruiz and Pavón described it in 1798. It is distinguished from its relative by hairier stems and sticky glandular hairs. It is the more common of the two in North American gardens.

Gardenia jasminoides is native to southern China and cultivated throughout East Asia for at least a millennium. In China (where it is "zhizi," 栀子) the plant is grown as an ornamental, in traditional medicine for inflammation and anxiety.

Gasteria carinata is native to the Western Cape of South Africa, growing on rocky slopes under light shrub cover. It was introduced to European cultivation in the late 17th century via Dutch East India Company ships stopping at the Cape of Good Hope.

Gazania krebsiana is a low-growing perennial in the aster family (Asteraceae) native to a wide range in southern and eastern Africa from South Africa, Lesotho, and Swaziland north through Zimbabwe, Zambia, Tanzania, and Ethiopia.

Gazania linearis, the strap-leaved gazania or treasure flower, is a clump-forming evergreen perennial native to grasslands and rocky slopes of eastern and northeastern South Africa and Lesotho. Named by English botanist William Aiton in 1789, the species differs from coastal G.

Treasure flower is a South African coastal perennial bearing large daisy flowers in blazing tropical colors that close at night. Introduced to Europe in 1755, it became a signature of Mediterranean and California coastal bedding and slope cover schemes.

Carolina jessamine is native to the southeastern United States and Central America. Linnaeus described it as Bignonia sempervirens in 1753; Aiton transferred it to Gelsemium in 1789.
