The state flower of South Carolina, its fragrant yellow trumpet flowers herald southern spring—but all parts are extremely toxic.

Himalayan cranesbill is native to the mountain meadows of Sikkim, Nepal, and Tibet. Collected by 19th-century plant hunters, it became parent of the hugely popular 'Rozanne' cultivar developed by Donald and Rozanne Waterer and named RHS Plant of the Centenary in 2013.

Bigroot geranium is native to the Balkans and southern Europe. Bulgarian peasants distilled essential oil called zdravetz from the aromatic rhizomes for traditional perfumery and religious ceremonies, a trade that continues today.

Geranium phaeum grows naturally in mountain woodlands and meadows of central Europe from the Pyrenees to the Balkans. Linnaeus described it in 1753 with the epithet phaeum meaning dark or dusky in Greek, referencing the unusually somber near-black flowers.

Meadow cranesbill is native to Europe and northern Asia. Described by Linnaeus in 1753, its blue-flowered natural variations and cultivars including 'Mrs. Kendall Clark' and 'Plenum Violaceum' have made it a cornerstone of English cottage and meadow gardens since the 19th century.

Geranium sanguineum, the true cranesbill geranium (not to be confused with tender South African Pelargonium), is native across Europe to the Caucasus and western Siberia, growing on limestone grasslands, coastal dunes, and open woods.

Gerbera jamesonii is native to a narrow area of South Africa's Mpumalanga (formerly eastern Transvaal) province, where Scottish gold prospector Robert Jameson discovered it in 1884 and sent specimens to the Durban Botanic Gardens.

Geum chiloense grows in moist grasslands and forest edges of south-central Chile, including Chiloé Island after which it is named. The Italian-French botanist Giovanni Battista Balbis described it in 1804.

Geum coccineum is native to montane meadows in the Balkan Peninsula and northern Greece. English botanist John Sibthorp collected specimens during his 1780s expeditions documenting classical Greek flora for the Flora Graeca.

Water avens is a northern-hemisphere wetland perennial described by Linnaeus in 1753. Medieval Europeans called it 'Herb Bennet' and used the clove-scented roots as a chocolate-flavored tonic. It remains a shade-tolerant bog-garden plant.

Prairie smoke is native to North American prairies and alpine regions. Collected by Lewis and Clark on the 1804-06 expedition, its feathery pink seed heads inspired its common name. Used medicinally by the Blackfoot and other Plains tribes for eye ailments.

Ginkgo is a "living fossil" — the sole surviving species of a lineage stretching back 270 million years to the Permian. Extinct in the wild for most of recent geological history and preserved in Chinese and Japanese Buddhist temple courtyards, it was reintroduced to the wider world in the 17th-18th centuries. Six ginkgos survived the Hiroshima atomic bombing of 1945 and still live today.

Field gladiolus, native to Mediterranean Europe, was described by Linnaeus in 1753. It has grown wild in wheat fields since Roman times and was used medicinally by Pliny the Elder. The subspecies byzantinus is a classic cottage-garden hardy gladiolus.

Parrot gladiolus, native across much of Africa and Madagascar, was described by Van Geel in 1828. Its vivid orange-red flowers served as a primary parent for the modern summer-flowering hybrid gladiolus, bred in Europe beginning in the 1840s.

Dwarf gladiolus is a hybrid group derived from South African species including G. tristis and G. cardinalis, bred in 20th-century Netherlands. Its small size and earlier bloom filled a niche between species gladiolus and modern hybrid sword lilies.

Evening-flower gladiolus, native to the South African Cape, was described by Linnaeus in 1760. Its creamy yellow flowers open at dusk and release an intense clove-and-honey scent, a rare trait in the genus. It was one of the earliest Cape gladioli cultivated in Europe.

Gladiolus × gandavensis is a hybrid group developed in 1837 by Belgian horticulturist Hermann Beddinghaus and Ghent nurseryman Louis Van Houtte, using four South African species (G. oppositiflorus, G.

Glechoma hederacea is native to Europe and Asia. Linnaeus described it as Nepeta hederacea in 1753; Schulting transferred it to Glechoma in 1810. Anglo-Saxon and medieval European herbalists called it alehoof or tunhoof because it was added to ale as a bittering and preserving agent.

Japanese honey locust is native to Japan and Korea, with a related form in China. Miquel described it in 1867. The long twisted pods and branched trunk thorns resemble the American honey locust. The pods have been used as a soap substitute in East Asia thanks to their saponin content.

Warszewicz's calathea is native to Nicaragua, Costa Rica, and Panama. Mathieu described it as Maranta warszewiczii in 1852, honoring Polish collector Józef Warszewicz; Körnicke moved it to Calathea in 1862; Borchsenius et al. to Goeppertia in 2012. Velvety dark leaves with cream-yellow bracts.

Globe amaranth is a tropical American annual whose papery clover-like flower heads dry perfectly for everlasting arrangements. Cultivated since pre-Columbian times in the Americas, it became a staple of colonial and Victorian cutting gardens worldwide.

Rio Grande globe amaranth is a slender Mexican and Texan native bearing coral, orange, and red flower heads on airy stems. The species honors 19th-century German seedsman Friedrich Adolph Haage and is beloved for meadow-style and cut-flower plantings.

Loblolly bay is a graceful southeastern United States evergreen tree of swamps and pocosins, bearing white camellia-like flowers. Colonial botanist John Bartram collected it in the 1760s, and the genus honors his Scottish gardener colleague James Gordon.

Ghost plant is a Mexican succulent of obscure origin, despite its misleading scientific name. Its pale silvery rosettes root from fallen leaves with almost reckless ease, making it one of the most-shared succulents in the hobbyist trade worldwide.

'Debbie' is a popular intergeneric hybrid between Graptopetalum amethystinum and Echeveria, creating the nothogenus xGraptoveria. Raised by California breeder Dick Wright in the mid-20th century. Forms 15 cm rosettes of broad lavender-pink leaves that intensify in bright light.

Fred Ives is an intergeneric hybrid between Graptopetalum paraguayense and Echeveria gibbiflora, created in the mid-20th century and named for British nurseryman Fred Ives. Its large bronze-to-pink rosettes made it one of the most widely grown graptoverias in horticulture.

Guzmania lingulata is native to a broad tropical range from the West Indies and Central America through northern South America into Brazil, where it grows as an epiphyte in rainforest canopies. The genus is named after the 18th-century Spanish apothecary and naturalist Anastasio Guzmán.

Gymnocalycium mihanovichii is native to Paraguay and adjacent northern Argentina. It was first described by F. Pazout in 1941. The ubiquitous "moon cactus" or "ruby ball" sold in every supermarket and plant shop worldwide is actually a chlorophyll-deficient mutant — either red (lacking.

Gymnocladus chinensis occurs naturally in southern and central China including Sichuan, Yunnan, Guizhou, Hubei, Guangxi, Hunan, and Jiangxi provinces, typically in valley forests between 300 and 1500 meters. French botanist Henri Ernest Baillon described the species in 1875.

Japanese forest grass (urahagusa) is native to mountain cliffs of the Hakone region of Honshu, Japan. Its cascading mounds of arching foliage — especially the gold 'Aureola' cultivar selected in Japan — made it a defining shade ornamental grass. Named RHS Perennial Plant of the Year in 2009.

Hamamelis virginiana is the source of commercial witch hazel astringent, produced by Thomas Newton Dickinson in Connecticut since 1866. Indigenous peoples used it medicinally for centuries before. One of the very few native North American woody plants that blooms in late autumn.

The Zebra Plant is native to Eastern Cape, South Africa. Almost all plants sold commercially under this name are actually the related Haworthiopsis attenuata; true H. fasciata is rare and almost absent from the trade.

Widely traded (and mislabeled) as Haworthia fasciata, this South African succulent is the true Zebra Plant of commerce. Its white-banded leaves make it one of the most common succulents sold worldwide. Non-toxic to pets.

English ivy is native to most of Europe and western Asia. Sacred to Dionysus/Bacchus in Greco-Roman tradition, central to British midwinter evergreen traditions alongside holly, and a signature covering of English university buildings — hence the American "Ivy League." Now invasive in parts of the US Pacific Northwest and other temperate regions.

White ginger lily is native to the Himalayas and southwest China. Its intensely fragrant white butterfly-shaped flowers are the national flower of Cuba ("mariposa blanca"), where they became a symbol of women's freedom during the 19th-century Cuban wars of independence — women hid messages in the flowers to pass between fighters.

Helianthus annuus was domesticated by Indigenous peoples of eastern North America around 3000 BCE for its seeds, which were ground into meal, pressed for oil, and used for pigments, dyes, and ceremonial purposes.

Beach sunflower is a sprawling southeastern US coastal annual to perennial producing countless small yellow sunflowers over silvery-green foliage. It is a salt-tolerant dune stabilizer and pollinator plant throughout the Florida and Gulf Coast landscape.

Jerusalem artichoke, or sunchoke, is a North American perennial sunflower whose knobby tubers were cultivated by Indigenous peoples long before European contact. Samuel de Champlain encountered it in 1605 and carried tubers to France, where it spread across Europe as a reliable carbohydrate crop.

Licorice plant is native to South Africa. A spreading tender perennial grown worldwide as a foliage filler plant for container gardens and hanging baskets — prized for silvery-white felted trailing stems that soften and unify mixed summer container compositions.

Hanging lobster claw is native to Peru, Ecuador, Colombia, Venezuela, and Bolivia. Its dramatic pendant chains of red-and-yellow bracts resembling lobster claws made it one of the most iconic tropical cut flowers of late-20th-century floristry. The national flower of Bolivia.

Heliotrope was introduced to Europe from Peru in the 1750s by French naturalist Joseph de Jussieu. Victorian gardeners prized its intense vanilla-cherry pie fragrance for cut flowers and perfumery, making it a staple of scented borders.

Christmas rose is native to the Alps and Apennines, so named for its reputed ability to bloom at Christmas. Medieval legend tied it to the tears of a young shepherdess at the Nativity, and it appears in monastery gardens from the Middle Ages to the present.

Lenten rose is native to the Balkans, Greece, and Turkey, named for its bloom coinciding with Lent. Hybridized extensively since the 1980s by English breeders including Helen Ballard and Elizabeth Strangman, it became a flagship winter-flowering perennial of contemporary shade gardens.

Citron daylily is native to China and cultivated for its edible flower buds (jin zhen cai) for centuries. It opens at dusk with a strong citrus fragrance, making it important in Chinese cuisine and parent to many night-blooming hybrids.

The Orange Daylily is an ancient Chinese cultigen that has naturalized along roadsides across Europe and North America. Despite being called 'lily', it is not a true lily — and unlike true lilies, its flowers are edible and a traditional Chinese vegetable (黃花菜 huanghuacai).

The lemon daylily is native to China and Siberia and has been cultivated in Chinese gardens for over 2000 years. It was the first daylily species introduced to Europe, documented by Carolus Clusius in 1601, and later became a garden mainstay across America.

Dwarf daylily is a compact yellow-flowered species from northern China, Mongolia, Korea, and Siberia. Described by Philip Miller in 1768, it is one of the smallest daylily species and valued for rock gardens and front-of-border planting.

Seven-son flower was discovered by Ernest Wilson in 1907 in Hubei, China, but remained unknown outside China until 1980 when the Sino-American Botanical Expedition reintroduced it. Its fragrant late-summer flowers and persistent red sepals create two-season beauty.

Heracleum mantegazzianum, giant hogweed, is a monocarpic perennial in the carrot family native to the western Caucasus of Georgia and southern Russia, reaching five metres tall at flowering with white umbels fifty centimetres across.

Green carpet or rupturewort is a European native in the pink family. Linnaeus described it in Species Plantarum 1753. Forms extremely flat 2-5 cm moss-like mats of tiny bright green leaves. Historically used medicinally for hernia and kidney issues. Popular between-paver groundcover.

Hesperis matronalis, dame's rocket, is a short-lived perennial native to Europe and western Asia with fragrant four-petalled purple, pink, or white flowers that perfume the evening garden. Introduced to North America by colonial gardeners, it has escaped into woodland edges and roadsides where it is now considered invasive in several states.

Toyon (also called California holly or Christmas berry) is native to California, southwestern Oregon, and northern Baja California. Its bright red berries and holly-like leaves gave "Hollywood" its name — early 20th-century boosters coined the neighborhood name from the abundant toyon ("California holly") in the surrounding Hollywood Hills. Official native plant of the City of Los Angeles since 2012.

American alumroot is native to eastern North America. Linnaeus described it in 1753 as the type species of the genus, which honors German physician Johann Heinrich von Heucher. Indigenous tribes used its astringent roots medicinally.

Roundleaf alumroot is a western North American native producing pale green bell-shaped flowers on wiry spikes. It contributed genetics to many modern heuchera hybrids and remains a tough native for rock and wildflower gardens of the Pacific Northwest.

Gooseberryleaf alumroot is a small rock-dwelling heuchera of the northern Rockies and eastern Cascades, with leaves resembling gooseberry foliage. Polyploid evolution in this species has been extensively studied by evolutionary biologists as a model of plant speciation.

Small-flowered alumroot is native to western North America from British Columbia to California. Collected by David Douglas in the 1820s, it became the parent of the famous purple-leaved cultivar 'Palace Purple' in the 1980s that transformed Heuchera breeding.

Heuchera sanguinea grows wild in rocky canyons and pine woods from northern Mexico to southern Arizona and New Mexico. It was introduced to European cultivation around 1882 and used throughout the late 19th and early 20th centuries for its coral-red flower sprays.

Heuchera villosa inhabits rocky outcrops, cliff faces, and shaded slopes across the southern Appalachians from Virginia to Georgia and west to Arkansas. French botanist André Michaux collected it during his American expeditions and described it in his 1803 Flora Boreali-Americana.

Coral bells is a hybrid complex developed in Europe by crossing eastern US Heuchera sanguinea with other species for showier flowers. It launched the modern heuchera flower-breeding tradition that gives us today's coral, red, and pink garden cultivars.

Chinese hibiscus is cultivated of unknown exact wild origin — probably a hybrid arising in East Asia and spread across Pacific and Indian Ocean cultures over a millennium. It is the national flower of Malaysia and the state flower of Hawaii, and the defining tropical garden plant worldwide.

Rose of Sharon is native to central and eastern Asia despite its species name suggesting Syria. The national flower of South Korea (Mugunghwa — "the flower that never withers"), it has been celebrated for over a millennium in Korean poetry, art, and national identity.

Himalayan sausage vine is native to the Himalayas and western China. Wallich described it in 1824. A close relative of Akebia, its fragrant white-to-purplish flowers and large sausage-shaped purple fruits are similar but more tender. The genus honors 19th-century Danish botanist Frederik Ludvig Holbøll.

Wall barley is a cool-season Eurasian annual grass, a wild relative of cultivated barley (H. vulgare). Linnaeus described it in 1753. Its barbed spikelets lodge dangerously in dogs and livestock, earning the common name 'foxtail' in western US parlance.

Fortune's hosta is an East Asian shade perennial introduced to Britain by Scottish plant explorer Robert Fortune in the 1840s. Many classic garden hostas trace lineage to this horticultural group, foundational to modern shade gardening.

Narrow-leaved plantain lily is an old Japanese garden hosta introduced to Europe by Carl Peter Thunberg in the 1780s. Long cultivated before being described botanically, it formed a foundation of 19th-century European hosta gardens.

Hosta montana grows in cool montane forests of Japan's main islands from Honshu north to Hokkaido. Russian botanist Carl Maximowicz published the name in 1867 based on specimens from Japanese mountain habitats.

Black-stemmed hosta is a Japanese species bearing tall stems of lavender flowers over striking dark petioles and cupped blue-green foliage. It is an important parent of many American-bred hosta cultivars with dark stems and heavy substance.

Fragrant plantain lily is the only strongly fragrant Hosta species, native to eastern China. Introduced to Europe via Paris in 1789, it parents nearly all of the scented-flowered hosta hybrids treasured in modern evening gardens.

Siebold's hosta was collected in Japan by Philipp Franz von Siebold in the 1830s and introduced to Europe through his Leiden nursery. Its massive blue-glaucous leaves made it the architectural centerpiece of 20th-century shade gardens.

Siebold's hosta is a Japanese species honoring Philipp Franz von Siebold, the 19th-century Dutch physician whose collections introduced many Japanese garden plants to Europe. Its white-edged variegated form remains a heritage cottage garden favorite.

Tokudama hosta is a Japanese garden selection with thick, rounded, deeply cupped blue-green leaves. Cultivated in Japan for centuries, it was formally described from cultivation and remains a key parent in breeding blue hostas.

Blue plantain lily, or bellflower hosta, is a Chinese species and one of the earliest hostas introduced to Europe. Its deep green leaves and purple bellflowers anchor classic cottage garden shade plantings to this day.

The Kentia Palm is endemic to a single tiny island — Lord Howe Island off eastern Australia. Seed export has been the island's main export industry for over 140 years, and the palm was a Victorian aristocratic status symbol.

Hoya carnosa is native to a broad sweep of eastern and southeastern Asia from southern China and India through the Malay Archipelago to Queensland, Australia, where it climbs as an epiphyte in humid forests.

Hindu rope plant or compacta hoya is a curled-leaf cultivar of Hoya carnosa, selected for its distinctive tightly-twisted and curled foliage. Traced to East Asian horticulture in the 20th century. Produces typical carnosa white-pink star flower umbels on the compact curled stems.

Hoya kerrii is native to Thailand, Laos, Cambodia, Vietnam, and southern China, where it climbs as a woody-stemmed epiphyte in tropical forest. It was described in 1911 and named after the British physician and botanist Arthur Francis George Kerr.

Linear-leaf hoya is a Himalayan native of Nepal, Bhutan, and northern India. Wallich ex D. Don described it in 1825. Unusual among Hoyas for its pendant hanging curtains of narrow needle-like green leaves covered in soft hairs. White starry flowers with lemon scent in autumn.

Hoya obovata is a climbing epiphytic vine in the milkweed family (Apocynaceae, subfamily Asclepiadoideae) native to a broad range across tropical Asia from northeastern India, Myanmar, and Thailand through peninsular Malaysia, Sumatra, Java, Fiji, and the Philippines.

Pubicalyx wax plant or Philippine wax vine is a Philippine endemic. Merrill described it in 1918. Vigorous climbing vine with thick dark green leaves often flecked with silver. Produces pendant ball-shaped umbels of starry waxy flowers in deep burgundy-red to near-black with fragrance.

Spanish bluebell is native to the Iberian Peninsula. Introduced to Britain in the 17th century, it has extensively hybridized with the British native H. non-scripta, threatening the genetic integrity of ancient English bluebell woods.

The Dutch Hyacinth is native to western Asia and the eastern Mediterranean. Ottoman Turkish cultivation produced hundreds of cultivars before European trade in the 16th century; Holland's Haarlem region has been the global production center since 1700.

Climbing hydrangea is native to Japan, Korea, and China. D. Don described it in 1825. The only self-clinging vine in the genus, it uses aerial rootlets to ascend tree trunks and walls. White lacecap flowers in early summer and exfoliating cinnamon bark on mature stems give year-round interest.

Smooth Hydrangea is native to the eastern United States, where indigenous Cherokee used its roots medicinally as a diuretic and for kidney stones. The 1960 cultivar 'Annabelle' made it a mainstream garden plant across North America.

Rough-leaved hydrangea is native to a wide arc from the Himalayas through China to Taiwan. D. Don described it in 1825. Its large velvety leaves, purple-centered lacecap flowers, and shrub-to-small-tree habit make it a late-summer star. Subsp. sargentiana, introduced by E.H. Wilson, is especially dramatic.

Himalayan hydrangea, native to the eastern Himalayas and western China, was described by David Don in 1825. It was introduced to English gardens via Kew in the 19th century and is valued as a cold-hardy, tree-like hydrangea with large lacecap flowers.

Bracted hydrangea, from Japan and Taiwan, was described by Siebold in 1829 from specimens collected during his Nagasaki residency. Valued in Japanese temple gardens for its unique flower buds enclosed in large bracts, it remains a specialty shrub in Western collections.

Hydrangea macrophylla is native to the coastal forests of Japan (Honshu, Shikoku, Kyushu) and parts of coastal China, where it grows in the humid shaded understory. It was introduced to European horticulture in 1790 by Joseph Banks from Kew Gardens.

Hydrangea paniculata is native to a broad temperate range across China, Korea, Japan, and the Russian Far East, where it grows as a large shrub or small tree in mountain forest. The earliest cultivar introduced to Western horticulture — 'Grandiflora', commonly called 'PeeGee' — reached.

Hydrangea petiolaris (most taxonomists now treat it as Hydrangea anomala subsp. petiolaris) is native to Japan (all main islands), Korea, the Russian Kuril Islands and Sakhalin, and Taiwan. German botanists Siebold and Zuccarini described it in 1840 in Flora Japonica.

Oakleaf Hydrangea is native to the southeastern United States. Distinctive for its oak-shaped leaves that turn crimson in autumn, giving it the rare feature of three seasons of ornamental value. Named Alabama's state wildflower.

Mountain hydrangea is native to mountains of Japan and Korea. Siebold and Zuccarini described it in 1840. Smaller and more refined than H. macrophylla, its leaves yield sweet tea (amacha) used in the Buddhist Hanamatsuri festival celebrating Buddha's birthday. The cultivar 'Bluebird' is a classic lacecap favorite.

Rose-of-Sharon or creeping St. John's wort is native to southeast Europe and southwest Asia. Linnaeus described it in 1767. A vigorous evergreen groundcover, its large yellow flowers with prominent bushy stamens bloom all summer—but the species has become invasive in parts of North America.

St. John's wort is a European herb named for the June 24 feast of St. John the Baptist when it flowers. Modern clinical trials show comparable efficacy to standard SSRIs for mild-to-moderate depression, though drug-interaction risks are significant and it is restricted in several countries.

False dandelion, native to Europe and Mediterranean regions, was introduced globally with human agriculture. Linnaeus described it in 1753. Unlike true dandelion, it has branched flower stems and hairy leaves. It is implicated in 'Australian stringhalt' muscle disease in horses.

Hyssop is an ancient Mediterranean herb mentioned repeatedly in the Hebrew Bible as a ritual purification plant — though biblical "hyssop" probably referred to Syrian oregano (Origanum syriacum), not this species. Central to Benedictine and Chartreuse liqueurs.

Ilex aquifolium is native to western and southern Europe, North Africa, and western Asia. The glossy spiny evergreen leaves and bright red berries that persist through winter made it sacred in pre-Christian European midwinter traditions: Romans exchanged holly boughs at Saturnalia.

Chinese holly is native to eastern China and Korea. Lindley described it in 1846. Distinctive rectangular leaves with prominent spines at the corners and bright red berries have made it a Southern US landscape staple. Cultivar 'Burford' (nearly spineless, self-fertile) is one of the most widely planted evergreen shrubs in the South.

Japanese holly is native to Japan, Korea, China, and Sakhalin. Its small dark evergreen leaves and tolerance of shearing have made it the standard boxwood substitute in 21st-century landscape planting as boxwood blight (Calonectria) devastates traditional Buxus hedging across Europe and North America.

Myrtle-leaf holly is a small evergreen tree of the southeastern United States coastal plain, found in wet pine savannas and pond margins. Indigenous peoples valued hollies for ceremonial black drink, and 20th-century horticulturists introduced narrow-leaved selections to landscape use.

American holly is native to eastern and southeastern North America. Aiton described it in 1789. The traditional North American Christmas holly, its white flowers and red winter berries were central to colonial holiday decoration. State tree of Delaware and a foundation coastal forest species.

Yaupon holly is native to the southeastern US and northeastern Mexico. Aiton described it in 1789. Its caffeine-rich leaves were the source of the black drink, a ceremonial purgative tea used by indigenous peoples of the Southeast—giving it the botanically unfortunate specific name. It is North America's only native caffeine-containing plant.

Meserve holly is a cold-hardy hybrid series bred by Kathleen Meserve on Long Island, New York in the 1950s-60s. Crossing English holly (I. aquifolium) with the ultra-hardy Prostrate holly (I. rugosa) produced the first blue hollies, whose 'Blue Princess' and 'Blue Prince' hybrid revolutionized cold-climate holly culture.

Florida anise or Florida star-anise is native to the southeastern United States. Ellis described it in 1770. Star-shaped maroon flowers in spring smell intensely fishy to some noses—but the crushed leaves smell of anise. Unrelated to the culinary Chinese star anise; this species is toxic and not used as spice.

Small-flowered anise tree is endemic to central Florida. Michaux described it in 1803. Though its flowers are small and inconspicuous compared to I. floridanum, the fragrant anise-scented glossy foliage and upright form make it one of the most useful evergreen shrubs for shaded southern gardens.

Garden balsam, or rose balsam, is a South and Southeast Asian annual cultivated for over 2000 years. Its bright camellia-like flowers earned it the cottage garden nickname Touch-me-not, from explosive seed capsules that launch seeds when brushed.

New Guinea impatiens is native to Papua New Guinea, the Solomon Islands, and nearby Pacific islands. Collected by USDA expedition in 1970 and developed into commercial ornamentals by Claude Hope of Linda Vista in Costa Rica — revolutionized the shade-bedding market of the 1980s with larger flowers and heat tolerance unavailable in traditional Impatiens walleriana.

Impatiens walleriana originates in the forests of eastern Africa from Kenya to Mozambique. It reached European cultivation in the late 19th century through collections made by Horace Waller, a British anti-slavery missionary associated with David Livingstone; Hooker named the species after him in 1.

Imperata cylindrica is native to an enormous range across tropical and subtropical Asia, Africa, southern Europe, and Australia, where it grows as a dominant tall rhizomatous grass of disturbed ground, burnt fields, and deforested tropical soils.

Ipomoea alba is native to a wide tropical and subtropical range in the Americas, from southern Florida and Mexico south through Central America and the Caribbean into northern South America, and has been cultivated and naturalized in warm climates worldwide.

Water spinach or ong choy is a tropical Asian aquatic morning-glory native. Forsskål described it in 1775. Fast-growing semi-aquatic vining vegetable with hollow stems and arrow-shaped leaves. Cornerstone of Southeast Asian cuisine. Federally noxious weed in the US.

The sweet potato originated in the Americas but reached Polynesia centuries before European contact — a distribution puzzle still debated. Carbonized sweet potato at Polynesian sites in the Cook Islands dates to 1000 CE, suggesting pre-Columbian trans-Pacific contact between South Americans and Polynesians.

Native to Mexico and tropical America, red morning glory naturalized across the American South by colonial times. Known for its scarlet hummingbird flowers and aggressive self-seeding, it remains a classic cottage-garden and hummingbird-garden vine.

Common morning glory is native to Mexico and Central America. Domesticated for ornamental use in pre-Columbian Mesoamerica and brought to Europe by Spanish colonists in the 16th century, its purple-blue trumpet flowers opening at dawn and closing by midday have made it one of the most universally cultivated annual climbers of temperate gardens worldwide.

Morning glory is native to Mexico and tropical Central America. Sacred to ancient Mesoamerican Mexica and Maya peoples — seeds contain lysergic acid amide alkaloids related to LSD and were used as ritual visionary sacraments. The cultivar "Heavenly Blue" is one of the most widely-grown climbing annuals worldwide.

Bloodleaf (also called chicken gizzard plant, Herbst's bloodleaf) is native to Brazil. Grown almost exclusively for its intensely colored foliage — the typical form has deep wine-red leaves with bright pink-red veining creating a dramatic almost-unnatural tropical color signature in bedding and container displays.

Iris cristata grows natively in rich deciduous forests and rocky slopes from Maryland through the Appalachians to Georgia and west to Missouri. Scottish-English botanist William Aiton, director of Kew Gardens, formally described the species in 1789 in his Hortus Kewensis.

Douglas iris is native to coastal California and southwestern Oregon. A clump-forming evergreen iris producing large 8-10 cm flowers in spring in colors ranging from white through cream, yellow, pink, lavender, purple, and deep blue. One of the foundational parents of the "Pacific Coast Hybrid" iris group.

Iris ensata is native to Japan, Korea, China, and the Russian Far East, where it grows in wet meadows and along stream margins. Cultivated in Japan under the name hanashobu since at least the 1500s, it has produced its own distinct breeding tradition centered around three historical.

Iris germanica is not a true wild species but a complex ancient hybrid cultigen of Mediterranean Europe, its exact parental history obscure. It is the classic 'tall bearded iris' of cottage gardens, and is the iris behind the French fleur-de-lis heraldic symbol.

Dutch iris is a 19th-century hybrid complex developed by Dutch breeder Van Tubergen from crosses of Iris xiphium, I. tingitana, and other bulbous Spanish/Moroccan iris species. It became a major commercial cut flower produced year-round in the Netherlands.

Munz's iris is endemic to a very restricted area of Tulare County, California. Notable for producing some of the clearest pure-blue iris flowers of any wild iris species — an exceptionally rare color in the iris family. A critical parent of the blue-flowered lines in Pacific Coast Hybrid iris breeding.

Sweet iris or Dalmatian iris is native to Croatia's Dalmatian coast. Known since Renaissance herbals, its dried rhizome called orris root has been the primary natural fixative for perfumes for over 500 years, with intense violet-like fragrance developing over 3-year curing.

Yellow flag is native to Europe, western Asia, and North Africa. Widely believed to be the original heraldic "fleur-de-lys" (though this is contested) after Frankish king Clovis adopted it following a 6th-century river crossing. Now one of the worst invasive aquatic plants of North American and Australasian wetlands.

Netted iris is native to the Caucasus, Turkey, Iran, and Iraq. Described by Marschall von Bieberstein in 1808, its 4-inch-tall February-blooming purple flowers with yellow ridges made it a staple of modern rock gardens and winter bulb plantings.

Siberian iris is native to central Europe, Russia, and parts of Turkey. Described by Linnaeus in 1753, modern American breeders including Currier McEwen, Bob Bauer, and John Coble revolutionized it in the mid-20th century with tetraploid hybrids in an expanded color range.

Northern blue flag is native to eastern and central North America. The state flower of Tennessee (as part of the iris group) and provincial flower of Quebec, it was used medicinally by Indigenous Eastern Woodlands peoples including the Haudenosaunee and Wabanaki Confederacy for emetic and cathartic preparations.

Itea japonica grows on wooded slopes and stream banks of central and southern Japan, Taiwan, Korea, and much of China. The English botanist Daniel Oliver described it in 1891 at Kew from specimens sent by Japanese collectors.

Itea virginica grows along streams, swamps, and wet woods across the southeastern United States from New Jersey to Florida and west to east Texas. Linnaeus described it in Species Plantarum (1753); the genus name is Greek for willow, alluding to the willow-like leaves.

Ixora coccinea is native to southern India and Sri Lanka, where it grows as an evergreen shrub in forest edges and rocky slopes. The genus name derives from the Portuguese corruption of "Ishwara," a name of the Hindu god Shiva — reflecting the plant's religious role in Hindu worship.

Dusty miller is a silvery-leaved Mediterranean perennial grown as an annual worldwide for its woolly white felt-covered foliage. Roman gardeners and medieval herbalists cultivated it, and Victorian bedding designers popularized its cool accent role.

Common jasmine is native to the Caucasus, Iran, Afghanistan, and the western Himalaya, reaching the Mediterranean via the Silk Road. The defining fragrance note of Grasse perfumery since the 16th century, it remains one of the two most expensive floral absolutes in commerce.

Arabian jasmine is native to South and Southeast Asia despite its name. The national flower of the Philippines (Sampaguita), Indonesia (Melati putih), and integral to jasmine tea, Hindu garlands, and traditional weddings across South and Southeast Asia.

Common soft rush is a circumboreal wetland plant native to Europe, Asia, and North America. A foundational material of traditional European rushlight candles — stems were peeled and soaked in fat to provide cheap lighting for cottage and farmhouse use into the 19th century — and of traditional Japanese tatami mat manufacture.

Chinese juniper is native to China, Mongolia, Japan, Korea, and the Russian Far East. Cultivated in Chinese and Japanese temple gardens for over 2,000 years; the foundation species of Japanese shimpaku bonsai — particularly the Itoigawa and Kishu wild-collected forms from the Japanese mountains, which are among the most valuable and culturally revered of all bonsai materials.

Common juniper is the most widely distributed conifer in the world, native across the entire Northern Hemisphere from the Arctic to high-elevation Mediterranean and Himalayan slopes. Its cones (called juniper berries) are the essential flavoring of gin — a distilling tradition that arose in 17th-century Netherlands and England.

Shore juniper is a coastal Japanese species now often treated as Juniperus rigida subsp. conferta. Parlatore described it in 1868. Forms prostrate 30-45 cm mats of stiff bright green needles on sandy maritime dunes. 'Blue Pacific' and 'Emerald Sea' are widely planted cultivars.

Creeping juniper is native to northern North America. Moench described it in 1794. The only prostrate juniper of the continent, it hugs the ground to form dense evergreen carpets on exposed rocky sites from Alaska to Newfoundland. Dozens of cultivars including 'Blue Rug' and 'Bar Harbor' are landscape groundcover staples.

Rocky Mountain juniper is native to the western North American mountains. Sargent described it in 1897. Its dense slow-growing wood was used by Plains tribes for bows and ceremonial objects. The cultivar 'Skyrocket', a narrow blue-green column, is one of the most widely planted vertical accents in modern landscapes.

Juniperus squamata occurs naturally across the Himalayas—from Afghanistan through Pakistan, India, Nepal, Bhutan, and southwestern China—and into Taiwan, typically at elevations of 1600-4500 meters on exposed mountain slopes and rocky ridges.

Eastern redcedar is native to eastern North America. Linnaeus described it in 1753. Its rot-resistant, aromatic heartwood is traditionally used for closet and chest linings to repel clothes moths, and was the original wood of pencil casings until supplies were depleted in the early 20th century.

Japanese kadsura is native to Japan, Korea, Taiwan, and southern China. Dunal described it in 1817. An evergreen vine bearing small cream flowers and showy clusters of bright red berries in autumn. The aromatic sap—named saneko or binankazura in Japan—was traditionally used as a hair-dressing oil by the samurai class.

Felt bush or velvet leaf kalanchoe is a Madagascar endemic. Drake described it in 1903 from the Behara region. Forms upright shrub-tree to 3 meters with enormous triangular leaves densely felted in silver or bronze velvet-like trichomes. Iconic dry-garden architectural succulent.

Flaming Katy is native to Madagascar. Introduced to Europe by German hybridizer Robert Blossfeld in 1932, it is now among the world's best-selling flowering houseplants. Significantly toxic to pets due to cardiac glycosides.

Paddle plant or flapjack plant is a southern African succulent. Raymond-Hamet described it in 1908, honoring horticulturist Luci. Forms basal rosette of large flat paddle-shaped leaves that flush intense red in sun and cold. Often confused in trade with K. thyrsiflora.

Panda plant or pussy ears is a Madagascar endemic. Baker described it in 1882. Forms 30-45 cm shrublet with thick oblong leaves densely covered in soft white felt tipped with chocolate-brown markings along the edges. One of the most popular 'fuzzy' succulents.

Japanese kerria is native to China, Japan, and Korea. DC. (de Candolle) described it in 1818, naming the genus for William Kerr, the Scottish plant collector who introduced the double form 'Pleniflora' from China to Kew in 1805. Its bright yellow flowers and green winter stems have made it a cottage garden standard.

Regal red hot poker is a high-altitude South African species from the Drakensberg mountains. Described by John Gilbert Baker in 1874, its blue-gray foliage and coral-to-yellow flower spikes make it a distinctive architectural plant for modern perennial gardens.

Koeleria macrantha, commonly called prairie junegrass, is a cool-season clumping perennial grass in the family Poaceae with a broad circumpolar distribution across temperate North America (from Alaska and Canada south through most US states except the southeast) and much of Eurasia from.

Golden rain tree is native to China and Korea. Introduced to Europe in 1747 by Jesuit missionary Pierre Nicolas d'Incarville, it was among the earliest Chinese ornamental trees to reach Western cultivation. Its golden summer flowers and pink papery seed lanterns remain distinctive.

Beauty bush, native to Hubei and Shaanxi in central China, was introduced to the West by E.H. Wilson for the Arnold Arboretum in 1901. The genus honors German botanist Richard Kolkwitz. Its arching sprays of pink bell flowers made it a 20th-century garden classic.

Common laburnum or golden chain tree is native to central and southern Europe. Cultivated since Roman times, its dramatic cascading 12-18 inch pendant racemes of yellow flowers in spring made it an iconic ornamental—despite all parts being dangerously toxic.

Waterer's laburnum is a hybrid of L. anagyroides and L. alpinum from the mid-19th century. The cultivar 'Vossii' produces the longest flower racemes (up to 24 inches) and is the most widely planted laburnum in Western gardens, forming the iconic Bodnant Laburnum Walk.

Lettuce was domesticated in ancient Egypt from bitter wild L. serriola, and was sacred to the Egyptian fertility god Min for its phallic appearance and milky sap. Persian, Greek, and Roman cultures adopted it; Roman emperors were said to credit it for cures and good digestion.

Prickly lettuce is the direct wild ancestor of cultivated lettuce (L. sativa), domesticated in ancient Egypt around 4500 years ago. Linnaeus described it in 1756. Its milky sap was used by Greek and Roman physicians as a mild opium-like sedative called 'lactucarium'.

Bottle gourd or calabash is one of the earliest domesticated plants. Molina described it as Cucurbita siceraria in 1782; Standley transferred to Lagenaria in 1930. Archaeological evidence shows worldwide cultivation by 10,000 BCE. Large vining annual producing bottle or dipper shaped hard-shelled fruits.

Japanese crape myrtle is native to Yakushima Island, Japan. Discovered by Père Urbain Faurie around 1900, John Creech of the US National Arboretum collected seed in 1956, and it became the crucial parent of disease-resistant US crape myrtle hybrids including 'Natchez' and 'Tuscarora'.

Lagerstroemia indica is native to China, Korea, and Japan, where it has been cultivated for more than a millennium — the Chinese "zi wei" was grown in Tang dynasty (7th-10th century) palace gardens and features in classical poetry.

Lamium amplexicaule grows natively across Europe, North Africa, and western Asia. Linnaeus described it in Species Plantarum (1753); the epithet amplexicaule means 'clasping the stem', referring to the round upper leaves.

Yellow archangel is native to Europe and western Asia. Known as a medieval monastery garden plant, the variegated cultivar 'Florentinum' (Hermann's Pride) spread widely in 20th-century shade gardens but has become invasive in Pacific Northwest forests.

Red deadnettle is a Eurasian annual described by Linnaeus in 1753 and now a widespread cool-season lawn and field weed in North America. Its young leaves are edible and historically foraged in European cuisine; its purple flowers are early-spring bee forage.

Lampranthus spectabilis, trailing ice plant or showy dewflower, is a trailing succulent native to South Africa's Cape region whose ground-covering mats explode with brilliant magenta daisy-like flowers in spring. Introduced to California horticulture in the early twentieth century, it is widely planted on dry slopes and coastal embankments for drought tolerance and spectacular bloom.

Old-fashioned bleeding heart, native to China, Korea, and eastern Siberia, was introduced to England in 1846 by Scottish plant collector Robert Fortune and became a Victorian sensation. Originally Dicentra spectabilis, it was moved to Lamprocapnos in 1997.

Lantana is a tropical American shrub carried by European colonists to the Old World in the 16th century. Spectacularly beautiful and spectacularly invasive, it now infests 50+ countries and is listed among the world's 100 worst invasive species.

Trailing lantana is native to southern Brazil, Uruguay, and northeastern Argentina. A spreading perennial groundcover producing continuous clusters of small tubular flowers in violet-purple, pink, or white — widely planted as a heat- and drought-tolerant trailing groundcover in warm climates worldwide, though classified as invasive in Australia and some tropical regions.

Bay laurel was the sacred tree of Apollo at Delphi and the source of Greco-Roman victory crowns — the origin of the word "laureate." The tree remains central to Mediterranean cuisine, and classical sculptures of emperors and poets continue to depict laurel wreaths.

Lavandula angustifolia — 'English Lavender' despite being native to the western Mediterranean — is native to rocky limestone slopes in the Pyrenees, the French Alps, and northern Spain, where it grows wild at elevations between 800 and 1800 meters.

French lavender is a Mediterranean species distinguished by scalloped (dentate) leaves — the basis of its common name. Less hardy than English lavender, it flowers for much longer and is widely grown across Mediterranean, Californian, and Australian gardens.

Spanish or French Lavender is named after the Stoechades (modern Hyères Islands off France). Distinguished by the showy purple 'rabbit ear' bracts atop its short flower spikes. Less hardy than English Lavender but more heat-tolerant.

Lavandin is a sterile hybrid of English Lavender × Spike Lavender, produced commercially for perfume and detergent industries since the 1920s. Yields about 4 times as much oil per hectare as true lavender, though of lower perfumery quality.

Tidy tips is a quintessential California spring annual whose yellow daisies tipped in pure white carpet valley grasslands each March. John Torrey named the genus for English botanist George Tradescant Lay, and the plant remains a wildflower meadow staple.

Common duckweed is a tiny free-floating aquatic flowering plant distributed worldwide. Each plant consists of a single 2-5 mm green thalloid leaf and is among the smallest and fastest-reproducing flowering plants — doubling its population every 1-2 days in favorable conditions. Extensively researched as animal feed, wastewater remediation, and future human food crop.

Brass buttons or New Zealand brass buttons is a New Zealand native in the aster family. Hooker described it in 1853. Forms flat 2-5 cm ferny green or bronze mats with tiny yellow button flowers. 'Platt's Black' with near-black foliage is a famous ornamental selection.

Snowland daisy is a low annual native to the western Mediterranean — Portugal, Spain, and Morocco — covered in miniature white daisies and often sold as Chrysanthemum paludosum or Mini Marguerite. German and American bedding programs introduced it widely in the 1980s, making it a cool-season edging staple.

Leucanthemum vulgare is native to Europe and temperate Asia — essentially the entire Eurasian temperate zone — and has been the quintessential European meadow "daisy" of children's flower-chain making, fortune-telling ("he loves me.

Shasta daisy is a hybrid developed by Luther Burbank in California around 1890. Burbank crossed Leucanthemum maximum, L. lacustre, and Japanese L. nipponicum over 17 years to create the iconic American garden daisy named for Mt. Shasta.

Summer snowflake, native to Europe and the Caucasus, was described by Linnaeus in 1759. Despite its name it blooms in spring, with nodding white bell-shaped flowers tipped with green. The cultivar 'Gravetye Giant', selected by William Robinson around 1910, became the standard form.

Spring snowflake, native to central and southern Europe, was described by Linnaeus in 1753. With nodding green-tipped white bells on short stems, it blooms in late winter alongside snowdrops and has been a cottage-garden staple since the 16th century.

Chihuahuan sage is a lighter, more delicate cenizo native to Chihuahua and southern New Mexico, bearing small bright green leaves and fragrant lavender flowers. American southwestern horticulturists value it for a softer appearance than the silver-leaved cenizos.

Leucophyllum langmaniae, commonly called Rio Bravo cenizo or Rio Bravo sage, is an evergreen shrub native to limestone slopes and foothills of northeastern Mexico, particularly in Nuevo León and Tamaulipas.

Coastal doghobble is native to the southeastern US Coastal Plain. Lamarck described it as Andromeda axillaris in 1783; D. Don transferred it to Leucothoe in 1834. A graceful arching evergreen of acidic woodland, with fragrant urn-shaped white flowers and lustrous foliage.

Florida hobblebush, also called Agarista populifolia in modern treatments, is a graceful arching evergreen shrub of southeastern United States ravines. Collected by early American botanists in the 1700s, it is prized in southern gardens for deep shade tolerance and cascading foliage.

Lovage is a tall European umbellifer with a celery-like flavor, cultivated in monastery gardens since Charlemagne's 8th-century capitulary. It is the herb base of Maggi seasoning — not actually containing lovage but imitating its distinct savory flavor.

Columbian lewisia is a tough little rosette-forming alpine succulent of the northern Rockies and Cascades. Like its cousin the bitterroot, it honors Meriwether Lewis and thrives in the lean rocky soils of western North American mountain outcrops.

Siskiyou lewisia is a rosette-forming alpine succulent of the Klamath and Siskiyou mountains of Oregon and California. The genus Lewisia honors Meriwether Lewis, who collected the related bitterroot during the 1805 Corps of Discovery expedition.

Dense blazing star (also called gayfeather or marsh blazing star) is native to the eastern and central United States. Its distinctive upright purple flower spikes — opening from the top down, unlike most spike-flowering plants — make it a signature prairie wildflower and a key nectar source for monarch butterflies during their fall migration.

Bigleaf ligularia is native to China and Japan. Collected by Robert Fortune in the 1840s, the purple-leaved cultivars 'Desdemona' and 'Othello' from the 1940s transformed it into an iconic bog garden specimen valued for bold rounded foliage.

Ligularia fischeri grows across moist mountain meadows of Korea, Japan, northeastern China, and the Russian Far East. Nikolai Turczaninow described it in 1838, naming the species in honor of Friedrich Ernst Ludwig von Fischer, longtime director of the St.

Ligularia przewalskii grows wild in wet meadows and streamside thickets of Gansu, Qinghai, Sichuan, and Tibet. The Russian explorer Nikolay Mikhailovich Przewalski collected the first specimens during his Central Asian expeditions, and Carl Johann Maximowicz of the St.

Ligularia stenocephala inhabits moist mountain meadows and forest edges across China, Japan, Korea, and Taiwan. Franchet and Savatier published the name in 1879 based on Japanese collections. The species became horticulturally transformative when British nurseryman Alan Bloom at Bressingham Gardens.

Glossy privet (nǚ zhēn) is native to eastern Asia — China, Korea, Japan. Its fruit is one of the "51 fundamental herbs" of traditional Chinese medicine. Widely planted as a street tree in subtropical regions, it is now a serious invasive in the southern US, Argentina, Australia, and New Zealand.

Ligustrum sinense, Chinese privet, is a semi-evergreen shrub native to southern China introduced to the United States in 1852 as a hedge and ornamental. Birds spread the blue-black fruits, and the species now dominates millions of hectares of southeastern forest understories, suppressing native plants with dense shade and allelopathic leaf litter.

The Madonna Lily is among the oldest cultivated flowers in the Mediterranean, depicted in Minoan frescoes c. 1600 BCE. Across medieval Christian iconography it symbolized the purity of the Virgin Mary; crusaders brought it to northern Europe. Lethal to cats.

Henry's lily was discovered by Irish botanist Augustine Henry in central China around 1888. Its orange Turk's cap flowers on 8-foot stems, disease resistance, and shade tolerance made it a foundational parent of the Aurelian and modern Orienpet hybrid lily groups.

Tiger lily is native to China, Japan, and Korea, where it has been cultivated for over 1500 years for edible bulbs. Introduced to Europe in 1804 by William Kerr, it became the prototype summer cottage garden lily across Western gardens.

The Easter Lily is native to the Ryukyu Islands of southern Japan and northern Taiwan. After Japan's 1941 attack on Pearl Harbor cut off bulb imports, US commercial supply shifted to a small stretch of the Oregon-California coast, where it remains concentrated today. Famously toxic to cats.

Martagon lily ranges from Europe to Mongolia. Cultivated in Europe since the Middle Ages, it was described by Linnaeus in 1753. Its Turk's cap flowers appear in Renaissance tapestries, and it parents the Martagon Hybrid group popular in modern shade gardens.

The Oriental Lily is a hybrid group developed from four Japanese wild lilies starting in the 1950s. 'Stargazer' (1978) was the first upward-facing form and transformed the cut-flower market. Lethal to cats like all true lilies.

Regal lily was discovered by Ernest Wilson in 1903 in the Min River Valley of Sichuan, China. Wilson broke his leg collecting bulbs in 1910 but shipped thousands to Boston's Arnold Arboretum, making it one of the most influential garden lilies ever introduced.

Speciosum lily is native to Japan and eastern China. Introduced to Europe by Philipp Franz von Siebold in 1832, it became a key parent of Oriental hybrid lilies bred in 20th-century Oregon by Jan de Graaff and now dominating the commercial lily trade.

Moroccan toadflax is a slender North African annual bearing miniature snapdragon-like flowers in every imaginable color. Seed mixes such as Fairy Bouquet and Northern Lights have made it an easy cool-season cottage garden and border edging staple.

Twinflower is a delicate circumboreal groundcover that Carl Linnaeus chose as his personal emblem, writing that it was a lowly, insignificant, overlooked, flowering only for a brief time, like his own life. It bears paired pink nodding trumpets on short stalks.

Chinese sweetgum is native to China, Taiwan, Korea, Laos, and Vietnam. Described by Hance in 1866, the name formosana refers to Taiwan (Formosa). Traditional Chinese medicine uses the dried fruit (lu lu tong) for rheumatism and circulation. Prized for brilliant red-purple autumn color in mild-winter gardens.

Tulip tree (also called tulip poplar or yellow poplar) is native to eastern North America and is one of the tallest hardwoods on the continent, reaching 60 meters. Its straight, rot-resistant timber was used for Indigenous dugout canoes, colonial-era house framing, and much of the interior trim of the White House.

Big blue lilyturf is native to East Asia (China, Taiwan, Japan). Widely planted across the American South since the mid-20th century as an evergreen edging and groundcover, it produces grape-hyacinth-like purple flower spikes in late summer. Despite the "turf" common name, it is a lily-family perennial, not a grass.

Creeping lilyturf is native to China, Japan, and Korea. Unlike clumping Liriope muscari, it spreads aggressively by underground rhizomes and is widely used as a tough utility groundcover, though it can become invasive in eastern US gardens and is now listed as invasive in several Mid-Atlantic states.

Living Stones mimic the pebbles of their South African habitat so precisely that they were unknown to Western science until 1811, when British explorer William Burchell discovered them when he 'picked up a curiously shaped pebble'.

Cardinal flower is native to the Americas from southeastern Canada south to Colombia. Its brilliant scarlet-red flower spikes in mid-to-late summer make it one of the most visually striking native wildflowers of North America, and — along with bee balm — one of the most important hummingbird flowers of the continent.

Lobelia erinus is native to the fynbos and grasslands of the Cape Province of South Africa. The genus commemorates Matthias de l'Obel, 16th-century physician-botanist to James I of England. European gardeners cultivated L.

Lobelia inflata was the signature herb of Samuel Thomson, founder of 19th-century American botanic medicine. Used as an emetic and asthma remedy, its active compound lobeline is now studied for smoking cessation and addiction research.

Lobelia x speciosa is a 20th-century hybrid between Lobelia cardinalis and L. siphilitica, developed to combine cold hardiness with the cardinal flower's brilliant red. The 'Queen Victoria' cultivar dates from the late 1800s.

Sweet alyssum is a low-growing Mediterranean mustard family annual whose honey-scented white, pink, or purple flower clusters are a staple of cottage gardens, edging, and hanging baskets. Victorian gardeners popularized it worldwide in the 19th century.

Italian ryegrass is a fast-growing cool-season annual or biennial grass from southern Europe. Lamarck described it in 1779. Deliberately cultivated as a pasture and cover crop, it has escaped to become a major herbicide-resistance hotspot in global wheat agriculture.

Japanese honeysuckle is native to East Asia (China, Korea, Japan). Introduced to the US as an ornamental in 1806 and heavily planted for erosion control through the early 20th century, it has become one of the worst invasive vines of the eastern and central United States, smothering native forest understory across more than 30 states.

European honeysuckle or woodbine is native to Europe, North Africa, and western Asia. Linnaeus described it in 1753. The classic English cottage honeysuckle—featured in Shakespeare, Keats, and Victorian poetry—its richly fragrant cream-pink flowers have scented European gardens for over a thousand years.

Coral honeysuckle or trumpet honeysuckle is native to the eastern United States. Linnaeus described it in 1753. Unlike invasive Japanese honeysuckle, this native is well-behaved and a premier hummingbird flower—the bright red-orange trumpet blooms are synchronized with ruby-throated hummingbird spring migration.

Goldflame honeysuckle is a hybrid probably of L. sempervirens and L. x americana, raised before 1895 and named after the German horticulturist Heckrott. The two-tone coral-pink-and-gold tubular flowers bloom for months, are fragrant (unlike most red honeysuckles), and are non-invasive—making it one of the best garden honeysuckles.

Loropetalum chinense is native to southern and central China, northeastern India, Myanmar, northern Thailand, Laos, Vietnam, and Japan. Scottish botanist Robert Brown described the species in 1818. The genus name Loropetalum means strap-petal from Greek loron (strap), describing the narrow ribbon-l.

Egyptian cucumber or sponge gourd is a tropical Asian native. Miller described it in 1768. Climbing annual vine producing elongated cucumber-like fruits that dry to fibrous interior used as natural bath and kitchen sponges. Young fruits eaten as vegetable in Asian cuisines.

Lupinus albifrons is native to California and southwestern Oregon, growing in chaparral, oak woodland, open coniferous forest, and rocky slopes from the coast to middle Sierra Nevada elevations, up to about 1600 meters.

Yellow bush lupine is native to the central California coast. Yellow-flowered nitrogen-fixing shrub; widely planted for dune stabilization in early 20th-century California. Now considered invasive north of its native range along the Pacific Northwest coast where it disrupts native dune ecosystems.

Large-leaved lupin is native to western North America and was collected by David Douglas in the 1820s for British gardens. George Russell, a Yorkshire railway worker, spent 25 years in the early 20th century selecting the "Russell lupins" — the rainbow-bicolor spires that define modern garden lupins.

Snowy woodrush is a European mountain native in the rush family. Linnaeus described it as Juncus niveus in 1753; Lamarck & de Candolle transferred it to Luzula in 1805. Tufted evergreen grass-like perennial with hairy olive-green leaves producing distinctive clusters of small snow-white flowers in early summer.

Rose campion, or dusty miller rose, is a southeastern European biennial whose silvery white woolly foliage sets off brilliant magenta flowers. Medieval European gardeners cultivated it as a flower for chaplets, giving it the name Flower of Jove.

Resurrection lily, also called naked ladies or magic lily, is likely of Japanese origin and was described by Maximowicz in 1885. A sterile triploid known only in cultivation, it sends up pink flower stalks in late summer long after its spring leaves have vanished.

Fetterbush is a glossy-leaved evergreen shrub of southeastern United States pocosins and flatwoods, bearing clusters of pink urn-shaped flowers in spring. The common name refers to the way its dense interlaced branches can entangle or fetter travelers.

Lythrum salicaria, purple loosestrife, is a tall perennial native to Europe and Asia whose magenta flower spikes dominate summer wetlands. Introduced to North America in the early 1800s, it spread through canals and ship ballast to cover millions of hectares of wetland. Biological control with leaf-eating beetles has since reduced its impact.

Maclura pomifera was the 19th-century Midwest's premier living-fence tree, planted for thousands of miles before barbed wire replaced it. Its giant inedible fruits are an evolutionary anachronism, adapted for Pleistocene megafauna (mastodons, ground sloths) that went extinct ~13,000 years ago.

Magnolia acuminata ranges widely across the eastern United States from New York and southern Ontario south through the Appalachians to Georgia and Alabama, west to Illinois and Arkansas. Linnaeus formally described it in 1759 (he recognized it before the transition to the modern.

Ashe magnolia is a rare small tree endemic to a handful of Florida panhandle counties, with some of the largest leaves and flowers of any North American tree. Botanist William Willard Ashe described it in 1926, and conservation efforts now protect its fragmented native populations.

Magnolia campbellii was discovered by Joseph Hooker and Archibald Campbell during Hooker's 1848-1851 Himalayan expedition. It is one of the largest and most spectacular flowering trees in the world, with pink tulip-shaped flowers opening in late winter on bare branches.

Yulan magnolia is native to central and eastern China. Cultivated in Buddhist temple gardens for over 1300 years, it reached Europe through Sir Joseph Banks in 1789. With M. liliiflora it parented the saucer magnolia, revolutionizing Western ornamental horticulture.

Southern magnolia is native to the southeastern United States and is the state flower of Mississippi and Louisiana. Its enormous creamy-white flowers exude such powerful lemon-citrus fragrance that a single flower can perfume an entire room, and its cultural resonance in Southern US literature is profound.

Kobushi magnolia is native to Japan and South Korea. Introduced to Europe by Philipp Franz von Siebold in 1865, its white star flowers on bare branches in early spring made it a parent of many modern magnolia hybrids including the hugely popular M. x loebneri group.

Lily magnolia is native to southwestern China. Cultivated in China for over 1000 years as mulan (a symbol of female virtue), it reached Europe in 1790 and became the primary parent, with M. denudata, of the hugely popular saucer magnolia Magnolia x soulangeana.

Pyramid magnolia is an uncommon deciduous magnolia of the southeastern United States, distinguished by its pyramidal crown and auriculate leaf bases. French botanist André Michaux collected and described it in the late 1700s during his North American expeditions.

Oyama magnolia is native to Japan, Korea, and eastern China. Introduced to Europe by Philipp Franz von Siebold in the 1860s, its pendulous fragrant white flowers with crimson stamens bloom in summer—one of the few magnolias flowering on current-year growth.

Star magnolia is native to central Honshu, Japan, and was introduced to Western horticulture in 1862 by George Rogers Hall. Its early-spring starlike white blooms open before the leaves, providing the earliest reliable ornamental tree bloom in many temperate gardens.

Magnolia tripetala grows in rich cove forests of the southern Appalachians from Pennsylvania south to Georgia and Alabama, with scattered populations westward. Linnaeus described it in 1753 as Magnolia virginiana var. tripetala before it was separated as a distinct species.

Magnolia x soulangeana is a hybrid created around 1820 by Étienne Soulange-Bodin at his estate near Paris, crossing Magnolia denudata (Chinese white yulan magnolia) with Magnolia liliiflora (Chinese purple lily magnolia).

Leatherleaf mahonia is native to western and central China. Fortune introduced it in 1849, and Carrière described it in 1854, naming it for English horticulturist Thomas Beale. Fragrant yellow winter flowers and blue berries made it popular in the Southeast, but it is now invasive in many areas.

Creeping Oregon grape is a low evergreen shrub of western North American forests. Indigenous peoples used its bright yellow root as a dye and medicine, and the related M. aquifolium was designated Oregon's state flower in 1899.

Hybrid mahonia is a cross between M. japonica and M. lomariifolia, raised by Lionel Fortescue at his Devon garden around 1951. The cultivars 'Charity', 'Winter Sun', 'Lionel Fortescue', and 'Buckland' combine the hardiness of M. japonica with the spectacular flower spikes of M. lomariifolia.

The domestic apple descends primarily from Malus sieversii, a wild apple of the Tien Shan mountains in Kazakhstan, carried west along the Silk Road and hybridized with European crab apples. Johnny Appleseed (John Chapman) spread apples across 19th-century America for cider — not eating.

Japanese flowering crabapple was introduced to Western horticulture from Japan by Philipp Franz von Siebold in 1862. Its heavy spring display of pink-budded, white-opening flowers on an arching small tree makes it one of the most widely planted ornamental crabapples worldwide and a key parent of modern disease-resistant crabapple cultivars.

Common mallow is a Eurasian annual or biennial described by Wallroth in 1824, now naturalized worldwide. Its mucilaginous leaves and round flat seedpods ('cheeses') are a traditional European spring edible. Cherokee and other Indigenous peoples adopted it for sore throats.

Ladyfinger cactus is a Mexican cactus native to Hidalgo and Querétaro, introduced to European horticulture in the 19th century. Its finger-like clustering stems and ease of cultivation made it one of the most commercially produced houseplant cacti in the world.

Twin-spined cactus is a central Mexican endemic. Haworth described it in 1824. Forms dense mounding clumps of cylindrical 15-20 cm stems densely covered in paired long white spines. Produces rings of small pink-red flowers in late winter. Widely collected classic Mammillaria.

Mammillaria hahniana is native to dry limestone slopes in central Mexico, primarily in Guanajuato and Querétaro states. It was described by German botanist Werdermann in 1929 and named for German cactus collector Alfred Hahn.

Mandevilla sanderi is native to the Atlantic coastal forests of southeastern Brazil. The genus is named for Henry Mandeville, British minister to Buenos Aires in the 1830s; the species commemorates the German-British nursery firm F.

The mango is native to the Indian subcontinent and has been cultivated there for over 4,000 years. Called "the king of fruits" in India, it is the national fruit of India, Pakistan, and the Philippines, and features centrally in Hindu religious art and ceremony.

The Prayer Plant is native to the Brazilian rainforest. Its leaves fold upright at night in a slow visible movement that gave the family Marantaceae its common name. Genus named after 16th-century Venetian physician Bartolomeo Maranta.

German chamomile is the annual chamomile used in most commercial chamomile tea. The essential oil contains chamazulene, which gives the oil a distinctive deep blue color and potent anti-inflammatory activity used in cosmetic and pharmaceutical formulations.

Ostrich fern is a circumboreal species native to North America, Europe, and East Asia. Its young emerging fronds — "fiddleheads" — are the most commercially important wild-harvested edible fiddlehead in eastern North America, a spring seasonal vegetable in Quebec, New Brunswick, Maine, and across Japan, where they are called "kogomi."

Stock is a Mediterranean biennial grown for its intensely clove-scented flower spikes. Cultivated in Europe since the 16th century, it became the signature fragrant cut flower of cottage gardens and a staple of the 19th-century British and German cut-flower trade.

Night-scented stock is a Mediterranean annual whose unassuming lilac flowers open at dusk to release an intense sweet clove fragrance. Victorian evening gardens featured it prominently beneath open windows for night perfume, a tradition alive in cottage gardens today.

Creeping mazus is a Himalayan native in the Mazaceae. Buchanan-Hamilton described it as Lindernia reptans in 1825; N.E. Brown transferred it to Mazus in 1914. Forms 2-5 cm mats with purple-blue tubular flowers resembling miniature snapdragons. Used between stepping stones.

Meconopsis betonicifolia seed was collected by plant hunter Frank Kingdon-Ward in Tibet's Tsangpo Gorge in 1924. Its 1927 debut at the Chelsea Flower Show caused one of the greatest horticultural sensations in British history. The sky-blue flowers remain among the most coveted and difficult garden plants.

Medicago lupulina is native to Europe, North Africa, and western Asia. Linnaeus described it in Species Plantarum (1753). As a Medicago, it is closely related to cultivated alfalfa (M. sativa) and fixes atmospheric nitrogen through Rhizobium root nodules, contributing soil fertility in.

Medinilla magnifica is native to the mountain forests of the Philippines, particularly Luzon, where it grows as an epiphytic shrub on tree branches at mid-elevation in humid cloud-forest conditions. The genus was named by French botanist Charles-Jacques Gaudin for José de Medinilla y Pineda.

Melaleuca citrina was collected by Joseph Banks during Captain Cook's 1770 Endeavour voyage and introduced to British horticulture. Widely planted in Southern California and Mediterranean gardens since the early 20th century. Molecular work in 2006 moved it from Callistemon into Melaleuca.

Blackfoot daisy is a compact mounding perennial of the Chihuahuan Desert and Edwards Plateau, producing white daisies with golden centers throughout the warm season. Its drought resilience and long bloom have made it a Texas xeriscape classic.

Lemon balm is a Mediterranean mint-family herb used since ancient Greek antiquity. The name "Melissa" means "honey bee" in Greek — beekeepers rubbed the plant on hive interiors to keep bees at home. It is the principal herb in the medieval "Carmelite water" tonic.

Pennyroyal is a European and western Asian native mint. Linnaeus described it in Species Plantarum 1753. Low prostrate to semi-upright mint with strong peppermint scent. Used since Roman times as insect repellent and emmenagogue but highly toxic to pets and pregnant women due to pulegone.

Corsican mint is a Corsican and Sardinian endemic. Bentham described it in 1834 honoring French botanist Esprit Requien. Extremely tiny creeping mint forming moss-like 2-3 cm tall mats with minute peppermint-scented leaves. Used between pavers and in fairy gardens.

Spearmint is a European and western Asian mint cultivated since antiquity. Essential to traditional Maghreb mint tea (thé à la menthe), Greek cuisine, and the American mint julep, it is the second most commercially produced mint after peppermint.

Apple mint or pineapple mint is a European and North African native. Ehrhart described it in 1792. Vigorous rhizomatous perennial with fuzzy rounded apple-scented leaves. The white-variegated 'Variegata' (pineapple mint) is one of the most widely grown ornamental mints.

Peppermint is a sterile hybrid between watermint and spearmint, first recorded in England in 1696 by John Ray. Its high menthol content made it the dominant medicinal and flavoring mint globally, commercially produced on industrial scale in Michigan, Washington, and India.

Ice plant, or common iceplant, is a succulent annual covered in glistening water-filled bladder cells that look like ice crystals. Native to South Africa, it is cultivated as a salty salad green and has naturalized widely on California coasts.

Metasequoia glyptostroboides is one of the most dramatic botanical rediscoveries of the 20th century. Japanese paleobotanist Shigeru Miki described the genus Metasequoia in 1941 from Miocene fossils, arguing that what had previously been classified as Sequoia fossils actually represented a distinct.

Japanese stiltgrass is an annual grass native to East Asia, introduced to Tennessee around 1919 as packing material. It has spread across the eastern US, forming dense forest-floor carpets that suppress native plants and tree seedlings. Deer avoid it, compounding its advantage over native flora.

Scarlet monkeyflower is a California and western US wetland perennial with brilliant red hummingbird-pollinated flowers. Widely studied alongside pink-flowered M. lewisii as a model of pollinator-driven speciation, it has transformed evolutionary biology textbooks.

Seep monkeyflower is a widespread western North American wetland perennial bearing bright yellow snapdragon-like flowers. Meriwether Lewis collected it in 1806, and the species has become a model organism in evolutionary biology and speciation research.

Monkey flower (bedding mimulus) is a complex hybrid group derived from crossing Chilean Mimulus species (M. luteus and M. cupreus). Cool-season bedding annual producing spectacular large 3-5 cm flat-faced tubular flowers in bright yellow, orange, red, and bicolored spotted patterns — "tiger-face" effect is the distinctive feature.

Giant Chinese silvergrass or Pacific island silvergrass is a robust bamboo-like ornamental grass of East Asia and the Pacific. Labillardière described it as Saccharum floridulum in 1825; Warburg transferred to Miscanthus in 1898. Reaches 3-4 meters tall with feathery silver-white plumes.

Small Japanese silvergrass is a compact Japanese native. Stapf described it in 1899. Smaller and earlier-flowering than Miscanthus sinensis, reaching only 90-120 cm with slender cream-tan plumes. Unlike other Miscanthus it thrives in partial shade. 'Nanus Variegatus' is variegated cultivar.

Chinese silver grass ("susuki" in Japanese) is native to East Asia — a central grass of Japanese autumn imagery and the traditional thatching material of rural Japanese farmhouses. Introduced to Western horticulture in the 19th century, it became one of the defining ornamental grasses of late-20th-century naturalistic design, but is now invasive in parts of the eastern US.

Evergreen Miscanthus or Taiwan silvergrass is an endemic of high elevations on Mount Yushan and Mount Morrison in Taiwan. Hayata described it in 1918. Unusual among Miscanthus for near-evergreen foliage even in mild climates. Produces silver-white plumes in late summer on 2 meter clumps.

Mollugo verticillata originated in the tropical Americas and has spread worldwide as a warm-season prostrate annual weed. Linnaeus described it in Species Plantarum (1753); the epithet verticillata refers to the whorled arrangement of its narrow leaves.

Bitter melon or balsam pear is a tropical Asian native widely naturalized in warm regions. Linnaeus described it in Species Plantarum 1753. Climbing annual vine producing warty oblong green fruits with intensely bitter flesh. Staple vegetable in Asian cuisine and traditional medicine for diabetes.

Eastern bee balm is native to the central US. Named for English botanist John Bradbury who explored the Missouri River region in 1810-11, it is the earliest-blooming Monarda and more mildew-resistant than M. didyma and M. fistulosa.

Lemon bee balm is an annual native to the south-central US and Mexico, described around 1800 by Mariano Cervantes and colleagues of the Royal Botanical Expedition to New Spain. Tohono O'odham and Hopi peoples used it as a flavoring and ceremonial herb; it is now a widely sown wildflower.

White bergamot is a woodland bee balm of eastern North America, described by Linnaeus in 1753. Appalachian settlers used it as a cold-remedy tea, and it remains a shade-tolerant, mildew-resistant species valued in native plantings.

Bee balm is native to eastern North America. After the 1773 Boston Tea Party, American colonists boycotted British tea and turned to bee balm as "Oswego tea," a patriotic substitute taught to them by Oswego Native Americans in upstate New York.

Wild bergamot is native to North America. Its leaves produce an aromatic tea — "Oswego tea" — that American colonists adopted as a substitute for Chinese black tea after the Boston Tea Party of 1773. An important nectar source for native bees, bumblebees, and hummingbirds, and a critical prairie and meadow wildflower.

Purple bergamot is a rare North American native of Appalachian meadows, described by William Willdenow in 1809. Used sparingly by Cherokee herbalists for fevers, it entered Victorian gardens as a shade-tolerant, purple-flowered alternative to bee balm.

Spotted bee balm is native to eastern North America. Linnaeus described it in 1753. The plant is a commercial source of thymol and was used medicinally by the Meskwaki and other tribes for respiratory ailments and fevers.

Monstera adansonii is a climbing tropical aroid native to a broad tropical American range — from southern Mexico through Central America, the Caribbean, and into much of tropical South America (including parts of the Amazon basin).

First described by Danish botanist Frederik Liebmann in 1849. The genus name comes from Latin 'monstrum' (monster), referring to its enormous perforated leaves. It rose to global fame as a mid-century modern design icon in the 1950s.

Standley's monstera or Philodendron cobra is a Central American native. Standley described it as Philodendron standleyi in 1940; G.S. Bunting transferred it to Monstera in 1968. Climbing vine with narrow dark green leaves streaked with cream-white variegation. Popular houseplant.

White mulberry is a deciduous tree native to China, cultivated for millennia as the obligate food plant of the silkworm. Imperial China guarded silk secrets for centuries. Introduced to colonial America for failed silk ventures, it now naturalizes widely and hybridizes with the native red mulberry.

Black mulberry is a Southwest Asian tree grown for at least 4,000 years for its richly flavored fruit. Romans disseminated it across Europe, and British monarchs later planted orchards in a failed attempt to rival Italian silk production.
