Bamboo muhly is a Mexican and southwestern US native. Scribner ex Vasey described it in 1890. Unusual among Muhlenbergia for its bamboo-like branching stems and finely divided airy chartreuse foliage. Forms soft 1-1.5 meter mounds. Popular Southwestern ornamental grass.

Lindheimer's muhly or big muhly is a central Texas and northeastern Mexico native. Hitchcock described it in 1913 honoring German-Texan botanist Ferdinand Lindheimer. Large clumping grass 120-150 cm tall with tall erect silver-purple plumes in fall. Important xeric landscape grass.

The banana was first domesticated in Papua New Guinea around 7,000 years ago — archaeological evidence from Kuk Swamp is among the earliest agricultural evidence on Earth. Nearly all commercial bananas today are the Cavendish cultivar, a parthenocarpic clone vulnerable to Panama disease.

Japanese fiber banana is native to the Ryukyu Islands and probably southern mainland Japan. Cultivated for centuries as the source of bashofu, the traditional Okinawan banana-fiber textile, it is also the most cold-hardy banana, surviving USDA zone 6-7 with rhizomes resprouting after winter dieback.

Armenian grape hyacinth, native to southeastern Europe, Turkey, and the Caucasus, was described by Baker in 1878. One of the most widely planted spring bulbs, it carpets gardens with cobalt-blue flower spikes that smell faintly of grape jelly.

Common grape hyacinth is a European native, one of the first grape hyacinths described by Linnaeus in 1753, and possibly the oldest Muscari in European cultivation. Its distinctive small flower spikes have been grown in gardens since the 16th century.

Muscari latifolium grows in open pine forests and rocky slopes of western and southern Turkey. The British botanist John Kirk described the species at Kew in 1874 from collections made during the 19th-century Anatolian botanizing expeditions.

Myoporum parvifolium, commonly called creeping boobialla or creeping myoporum, is a prostrate evergreen shrub native to inland southern Australia from Western Australia through South Australia, Victoria, and New South Wales, growing on sandy soils and along seasonal watercourses.

Wood forget-me-not is a European biennial whose name derives from a medieval German legend of a knight drowning in a river. Adopted as the symbol of remembrance for Alzheimer's Disease worldwide and of Masonic persecution during the Nazi era.

Parrot feather is native to South America (Amazon basin and southern South America). Introduced worldwide as an aquarium and pond plant for its attractive fine feathery foliage, it has become one of the worst invasive aquatic weeds in the US, Europe, Australia, New Zealand, and South Africa — banned or restricted in most developed countries.

Sweet cicely is a European montane native. Linnaeus described it as Scandix odorata in 1753; Scopoli transferred it to Myrrhis in 1772. Early-season umbellifer 60-120 cm tall with fern-like leaves tasting of anise. Used historically in European monastic gardens as sugar-saving sweetener.

Myrtus communis has been sacred in Mediterranean cultures for over 3000 years. Sacred to Aphrodite/Venus as the love plant, one of the Jewish Four Species of Sukkot, and carried by every British royal bride since Queen Victoria's 1840 wedding via cuttings from her original plant.

Heavenly bamboo is native to eastern Asia (China, Japan, India). Despite the name it is not a bamboo — it is in the Berberidaceae family. A New Year good-luck plant in Japan and China, valued for red autumn foliage and persistent bright red winter berries. In the southeastern US the berries have been definitively linked to fatal cyanide poisoning of cedar waxwings and are restricted by several invasive-species councils.

Cyclamen-flowered daffodil is native to Iberian Peninsula stream banks. Rediscovered in 1885 after being considered extinct, its unique swept-back petals made it parent to beloved early cultivars 'February Gold', 'Tête-à-Tête', and 'Jetfire'.

Jonquil is native to the Iberian Peninsula. Distinguished from standard daffodils by multiple small highly fragrant flowers on each stem, it is the traditional 'jonquil' of 19th-century English and French perfumery, though commercial fragrance is now largely synthetic.

Narcissus poeticus is believed to be the narcissus of Greek mythology. Described in Ovid's Metamorphoses as the flower into which the self-gazing youth Narcissus was transformed. Cultivated for millennia; its flowers distilled in Grasse, France, for narcissus absolute perfume.

The Wild Daffodil is native to western Europe. Its name comes from the Greek myth of Narcissus, the youth who fell in love with his own reflection. All daffodils are significantly toxic due to lycorine alkaloids in the bulb.

Tazetta daffodil or paperwhite is native to the Mediterranean basin and extends to China. Cultivated since ancient Egyptian times—bulbs found in Ramesses II's tomb—it is the source of the most fragrant daffodils including commercial forcing paperwhites.

Angel's tears daffodil is native to Portugal, Spain, and southwestern France. Described by Linnaeus in 1753, its pendant clusters of pale ivory flowers with swept-back petals inspired the Triandrus Group hybrids, Division 5 in RHS classification.

Mexican feather grass is a Mexican, southwestern US, and Argentinian native. Trinius described it as Stipa tenuissima in 1829; M.E. Barkworth transferred to Nassella in 1990. Forms 45-60 cm clumps of extremely fine threadlike green leaves that billow in wind. Invasive concern in California and Australia.

Sacred lotus is native to tropical and temperate Asia from Iran east through India, China, and into northern Australia. One of the most culturally significant plants in the world — sacred in Hinduism, Buddhism, and Jainism, featured in Egyptian iconography, and the national flower of India and Vietnam. Edible roots, seeds, and leaves are staple foods in East Asian cuisine.

Confetti nemesia, is a sub-shrubby South African perennial released widely in the 1990s as a hardier cool-season alternative to the annual Nemesia strumosa. Its fragrant pink, lavender, and white flowers perfume spring and fall patio containers.

Shrubby nemesia is a low South African subshrub that became the breeding backbone of the modern perennial nemesia trade. Cultivars such as Blue Bird and the Sunsatia series dominate the cool-season container market worldwide.

Cape jewels (nemesia) is native to coastal South Africa. A small cool-season bedding annual producing masses of small snapdragon-like flowers in vivid bicolored patterns of yellow, orange, red, pink, white, and purple — widely grown as a late-winter and spring annual in Mediterranean and mild coastal climates.

Baby blue eyes is endemic to California and adjacent southern Oregon and Baja California. A small California-native spring annual producing sheets of pure sky-blue flowers with pale centers — one of the classic "California wildflower blue" species. Named for Scottish surgeon-naturalist Archibald Menzies, who collected it during the 1790s Vancouver Expedition.

Neoregelia carolinae is native to the Atlantic Forest of southeastern Brazil, where it grows as a tree-mounted epiphyte or terrestrial rosette. Like other 'tank bromeliads' it holds pooled rainwater in the cup formed by its overlapping leaf bases, creating miniature aquatic habitats that in the wil.

Catnip is a European mint that affects about two-thirds of domestic cats through the compound nepetalactone, which binds to feline olfactory receptors producing euphoric rolling behavior. The response is genetically inherited and does not occur in kittens under 6 months.

Nepeta racemosa grows wild across the Caucasus, Anatolia, Iran, and Central Asia on rocky slopes and dry meadows. Known in older literature as N. mussinii, the species reached European cultivation through Russian collecting expeditions in the 18th century.

Siberian catmint, from the mountains of Central Asia and Siberia, was described by Linnaeus in 1753. Russian herbalists used it as a mild sedative. It reached British gardens via 18th-century plant exchanges and is now grown for long-lasting blue flower spikes.

Faassen's catmint is a hybrid of N. racemosa and N. nepetella discovered in the early 20th century by Dutch nurseryman J.H. Faassen. Its sterile nature and long bloom made it the dominant garden catmint, with 'Walker's Low' winning Perennial Plant of the Year 2007.

The Boston Fern was the defining Victorian and Edwardian parlor plant, its mass popularity dating to an 1894 cultivar ('Bostoniensis') discovered in a Philadelphia-to-Boston shipment. Native to the Americas, Africa, and Polynesia; non-toxic to pets.

Oleander is native to the Mediterranean basin, North Africa, and southwest Asia. One of the oldest ornamental shrubs in continuous cultivation — grown since ancient Egyptian and classical Mediterranean times — and also one of the most toxic common garden plants in the world. All parts contain cardiac glycosides; ingestion of a single leaf has killed adults.

Winged tobacco (also called jasmine tobacco or flowering tobacco) is native to southern Brazil, Paraguay, Uruguay, and northern Argentina. The trumpet-shaped white (wild type) or pink/lime/crimson (cultivars) flowers open in the late afternoon and evening and release an extraordinarily sweet jasmine-gardenia fragrance that carries across an entire garden.

Langsdorff's tobacco is a Brazilian annual with nodding bell-shaped apple-green flowers. Named for 19th-century German-Russian explorer Georg Heinrich von Langsdorff, it has become a favored ornamental tobacco for cottage gardens and cutting beds.

Brazilian flowering tobacco is a tall Brazilian native whose flowers open white and transition through pink to magenta as they age, creating a multicolored cloud. Discovered only in the 1980s, it has become a favorite of cottage gardeners worldwide.

Woodland tobacco is native to the eastern slopes of the Andes in Argentina and Bolivia. Grows as a dramatic architectural plant with a basal rosette of huge leaves up to 90 cm long and towering flower stalks reaching 1.5-2 meters, producing pendulous clusters of long pure-white trumpet flowers with powerful evening fragrance.

Nierembergia scoparia, sometimes sold under the older name N. hippomanica var. violacea, is a low-growing short-lived perennial native to Argentina and Uruguay. French botanists Sendtner and Grisebach worked out the modern taxonomy in the 19th century, and the genus honors Juan Eusebio.

Nigella damascena is native to the Mediterranean basin, from southern Europe through North Africa and into southwest Asia. The species epithet "damascena" ("of Damascus") reflects its frequent occurrence in Syria and its early association with the Damascus region in European botanical literature.

Black cumin, or black seed, is an annual of southwest Asia whose tiny black seeds have been a staple spice and traditional medicine for over 3000 years. Tutankhamun's tomb held a jar of them, and they remain a cornerstone ingredient of Middle Eastern cuisine.

European white water lily is native across Europe, North Africa, and western Asia. Claude Monet's water-lily paintings at Giverny — approximately 250 works including the massive Orangerie installation — are based principally on hybrids involving this species and are among the most famous images in Western art.

Water tupelo is native to swamps of the southeastern United States. Linnaeus described it in 1753. The swollen buttressed base is an adaptation to seasonal flooding. Its hollow old trunks historically provided nesting cavities for wood ducks, and the flowers are a prized nectar source for tupelo honey.

Sweet basil originated in India and has been cultivated for over 5,000 years. Sacred in Hindu tradition (though distinct from holy basil / tulsi), it spread west via Persian and Arab traders, became central to Mediterranean cuisine, and is the defining herb of Italian pesto.

Holy basil or tulsi is an Indian subcontinental sacred herb now classified as Ocimum tenuiflorum (synonym). Linnaeus described O. tenuiflorum in 1753; sanctum was coined by Linnaeus in 1767. Sacred to Hindu traditions and used in Ayurvedic medicine. Aromatic purple-green leaves.

Holy basil or tulsi is the priority name for the species also widely called Ocimum sanctum. Linnaeus described it in Species Plantarum 1753. Aromatic herb of the Indian subcontinent sacred in Hindu tradition and central to Ayurvedic medicine. Leaves brewed as adaptogenic tea worldwide.

Indian plum, or osoberry, is one of the earliest-blooming shrubs of the Pacific Northwest, its white racemes appearing in late winter. Indigenous peoples ate the bitter-sweet fruits, and settlers likened the flavor to almond.

Common evening primrose is native to North America and has spread globally as a weed and wildflower. Its seed oil — rich in gamma-linolenic acid (GLA) — is one of the most widely sold herbal supplements in Europe and North America, with documented research on eczema and cyclic mastalgia. The flowers open dramatically at dusk, timed for pollination by hawkmoths.

Oenothera lindheimeri (formerly and widely still Gaura lindheimeri — recent molecular phylogenetic work moved the genus Gaura into Oenothera in 2007) is native to Texas and Louisiana, growing in prairies, roadsides, and open woodland.

Oenothera speciosa, pinkladies or showy evening primrose, is a rhizomatous perennial native to prairies, rocky hillsides, and open woodlands from Missouri and Kansas south through Texas into northern Mexico.

Mondo grass is native to Japan, Korea, and China, where it has been used in traditional medicine since the Han dynasty — the root tubers ("mai men dong") are a classical Chinese herb for lung dryness. In Western horticulture it is used as a fine-textured shade-tolerant grass-like groundcover.

'Nigrescens' is a Japanese-bred black-foliage cultivar of Ophiopogon planiscapus, introduced to Western horticulture in the 1970s. It is among the few temperate-hardy plants with truly black foliage and has become a signature of modern architectural garden design.

Devil's club is a spiny understory shrub of Pacific Northwest rainforests, considered a sacred medicinal plant by many coastal First Nations. Its large palmate leaves and densely thorned stems make it both forbidding and unmistakable in its shaded habitat.

Prickly pear is native to central Mexico where it was domesticated at least 9,000 years ago, and it appears on the Mexican flag. Spanish colonizers spread it around the Mediterranean and beyond; today it feeds humans and livestock across five continents.

Bunny ears cactus is native to central and northern Mexico and deceptively dangerous: its apparently soft-looking white or yellow tufts are actually clusters of barbed glochids that embed painfully in skin. It remains one of the most widely sold decorative houseplant cacti.

Santa Rita prickly pear or purple prickly pear is a southwestern US and Mexican native. Rose described it in 1908. Forms 1-2 meter shrubs of round flat pads that flush striking purple-red in winter cold and drought stress. Yellow flowers followed by red-purple fruit.

Oregano is native to the Mediterranean and Asia, used since ancient Greek antiquity. The modern Italian-American pizza boom after World War II transformed it from minor herb to global staple — American oregano imports multiplied over 5,000% between 1948 and 1956.

Native to southern Europe and the Mediterranean basin, Orlaya grandiflora was historically a weed of calcareous grain fields before modern herbicides reduced its presence. The genus was named by German botanist Johann Konrad Wilhelm Hoffmann in 1814 to honor Johann Orlay.

Sweet osmanthus ("guì huā" 桂花) is native to southern China, Taiwan, and the Himalayas. Its small apricot-colored autumn flowers produce one of the most beloved fragrances in East Asian culture — the signature scent of Chinese Mid-Autumn Festival. The city flower of Hangzhou, Suzhou, Guilin (named after the flower), and several other Chinese cities.

Osmanthus heterophyllus is native to Japan (all main islands) and Taiwan. The species had been known under multiple names (Olea ilicifolia, O. aquifolium) before British botanist Peter Shaw Green established the current combination.

Burkwood osmanthus is a hybrid of O. delavayi and O. decorus, raised at Burkwood & Skipwith nursery in Kingston, England around 1930. Combining the compact dark-foliaged habit of O. delavayi with the hardiness of O. decorus, it produces intensely fragrant white flowers in mid-spring.

Royal fern is a large, almost cosmopolitan temperate fern native to Europe, Asia, Africa, and North America. It is one of the oldest ferns on Earth — the Osmundaceae family is documented in the fossil record from 220 million years ago (Triassic), making royal fern a true "living fossil" lineage.

African daisy is a showy South African perennial whose cultivars dominate modern spring bedding. Danish chemist Christian Ecklon collected the type, and breeding programs in Denmark, Japan, and the United States produced today's expansive Osteospermum trade.

Trailing African daisy is a sprawling South African perennial used as evergreen coastal ground cover in Mediterranean climates. Introduced to California in the 20th century, it covers freeway cuts, bluffs, and residential slopes with pink and purple spring bloom.

Eastern hophornbeam is native to eastern North America. The genus Ostrya was coined from Greek ostrua by Scopoli in 1760; Philip Miller named the species in 1768. Pioneers valued the extremely hard wood for tool handles, sled runners, and ox yokes, earning it the name ironwood.

Creeping woodsorrel is a cosmopolitan low-growing annual or perennial of uncertain origin, possibly Southeast Asia or the Mediterranean. Linnaeus described it in 1753. Its lemony leaves are a globally shared edible, and it is now one of the most common greenhouse weeds worldwide.

Common yellow woodsorrel is a North American native described by Linnaeus in 1753. Its lemony-tart trifoliate leaves were used as a vitamin-C source by Cherokee and other tribes and by European colonists, making it a familiar spring-forage plant before modern citrus.

The Purple Shamrock is native to Brazil. Its deep burgundy triangular leaflets fold closed at night and open at sunrise in a visible nyctinastic movement. A popular gift plant for St. Patrick's Day despite its South American origin.

The Money Tree — with its characteristic braided trunk — is native to Central and South American wetlands. The decorative braided form was invented by a Taiwanese truck driver in the 1980s and became an icon of feng shui practice.

Pachyphytum oviferum is native to the limestone cliffs and scree slopes of the Sierra Madre Oriental in San Luis Potosí, Mexico. It was described by the German-Mexican botanist Carl Albert Purpus in 1909.

Japanese pachysandra is native to Japan, Korea, and China, introduced to American horticulture by the Arnold Arboretum around 1882. For over a century it was the default evergreen groundcover of shady American suburbs. Since the 2000s Volutella blight has caused widespread decline.

Pachyveria glauca is an intergeneric hybrid between Pachyphytum and Echeveria in the stonecrop family. The nothogenus xPachyveria was established by Haage & Schmidt in 1935. Forms compact rosettes of thick blue-gray fleshy leaves with pink tips. Common in succulent mixes.

The Chinese Peony has been cultivated in China for over 2,000 years as both an ornamental and a medicinal plant. Its root (bai shao) is a fundamental ingredient in Traditional Chinese Medicine, listed in the Shennong Bencao Jing (c. 200 CE).

Molly-the-witch, or Caucasian peony, is a Georgian peony famed for its clear soft yellow flowers. Polish-Russian botanist Ludwik Mlokosiewicz discovered the species in Lagodekhi in the 1880s, and it remains a highly coveted collector's peony despite a tongue-twisting scientific name.

European peony is one of the oldest medicinal plants of Western horticulture. Greek physician Dioscorides documented its use in the 1st century CE, and medieval monastic gardens cultivated it for gout, epilepsy, and childbirth remedies long before ornamental breeding.

Feng Dan Bai is a central Chinese tree peony species cultivated for medicinal Moutan bark and for its single white fragrant flowers. Commercial tree peony culture in Henan and Shandong relies heavily on this species as rootstock and medicinal plantation material.

Rock's tree peony (Chinese "zǐbān mǔdān" 紫斑牡丹) is native to the mountains of central and western China. Named for Austrian-American plant explorer Joseph Rock, who collected it in Gansu Province monasteries in the 1920s. Distinguished by its dramatic dark-purple-to-maroon petal blotch at the base of each white petal — the source of famous modern hybrid lines.

The Tree Peony was the undisputed 'King of Flowers' in Tang dynasty China, celebrated in imperial poetry and painting. The ancient cultivation center around Luoyang has held an annual tree peony festival for over 1,400 years.

Fernleaf peony is a steppe perennial of Eastern Europe and the Caucasus with threadlike foliage and brilliant scarlet flowers. Russian and Ukrainian gardens have grown it for centuries, and it is a favorite spring ephemeral of rock and cottage gardens in temperate climates.

Pandorea jasminoides is native to eastern Australia (Queensland, New South Wales), typically in subtropical rainforest margins and riverine forests. English botanist Henry Charles Andrews originally described the species as Bignonia jasminoides in 1807.

Bitter panicum or coastal panicgrass is an Atlantic and Gulf Coast dune grass of eastern North America. Elliott described it in 1816. Rhizomatous warm-season grass 90-180 cm tall that stabilizes beach dunes. 'Dewey Blue' is a popular glaucous blue-foliaged cultivar for ornamental use.

Bulb panicgrass is a Mexican and southwestern US native. Kunth described it in 1816. Warm-season clumping grass with short thickened bulb-like stem bases at ground level, reaching 60-120 cm with airy purplish panicles in late summer. Drought-tolerant southwestern native.

Iceland poppy is native to cold-temperate and subarctic regions of Europe, Asia, and North America. A short-lived perennial typically grown as a cool-season biennial or annual bedding plant — produces exquisite crepe-paper-textured 6-10 cm flowers in every warm color from pure white through cream, yellow, gold, orange, salmon, pink, and red.

Oriental poppy is native to the Caucasus, northeast Turkey, and northern Iran. Introduced to Western horticulture by French botanist Joseph Pitton de Tournefort around 1700. Its massive ornate papery flowers — often 15 cm across in scarlet, salmon, white, or pink — made it a foundational Victorian border plant.

Common (Flanders) poppy is native to Europe and western Asia. Its brilliant red flowers blooming in the disturbed soils of WWI battlefields became the universal remembrance symbol of British Commonwealth war memorial, through the 1915 poem "In Flanders Fields" by Canadian military physician John McCrae.

Papaver somniferum has been cultivated for at least 6000 years, first recorded in Sumerian tablets as 'hul gil' (joy plant) circa 3400 BCE. It is the source of opium and, via morphine, most modern medical opioid painkillers. Dried seeds are entirely legal and widely used in baking.

Persian ironwood is native to the Hyrcanian forests south of the Caspian Sea in Iran and Azerbaijan. Named in 1831 for German naturalist F.W. Parrot, its spectacular multicolored fall foliage and exfoliating mottled bark have made it a premier small specimen tree.

Chinese Virginia creeper or silvervein creeper is native to central China. Hemsley described it in 1894, naming it for Augustine Henry who collected it. The purple-green leaves with bright silvery-white veins are extraordinary, especially in shade—turning brilliant red in autumn. Less aggressive than other Parthenocissus species.

Virginia creeper is native to eastern and central North America and is widely grown for its brilliant scarlet-crimson autumn color, which matches or exceeds that of its Asian cousin Boston ivy. Unlike true poison ivy (Toxicodendron radicans), with which it is often confused, Virginia creeper has five leaflets, not three.

Parthenocissus tricuspidata is native to eastern Asia — China, Korea, Japan, and Taiwan — despite the common name "Boston ivy," which derives not from the plant's origin but from its 19th-century cultivation in Boston and its strong association with Boston's Fenway Park (where the outfield walls.

Blue passionflower is native to central and southern South America. 17th-century Spanish Jesuit missionaries read in its elaborate floral anatomy a symbol of the Passion of Christ — the filaments as the crown of thorns, the five anthers as the five wounds — giving the genus its name Passiflora.

Passionfruit is native to Paraguay, Argentina, and Brazil, with the name "passion flower" coined by 17th-century Spanish missionaries who saw the flower's complex structures as symbols of Christ's Passion — the 10 petals representing the apostles, the radial filaments as crown of thorns, and the five stamens as the wounds.

Maypop or purple passionflower is native to the southeastern United States. Linnaeus described it in 1753. Named for Christian missionaries who saw in the flower's parts symbols of the Passion of Christ. Host plant of the Gulf Fritillary and zebra longwing butterflies, and a traditional anxiolytic herb.

Paulownia tomentosa, empress tree or princess tree, is a fast-growing deciduous tree native to central and western China. Cultivated in Japan for centuries to make tansu chests and koto soundboards, it reached Europe in 1834 via Siebold. In the United States it escaped cultivation and now colonizes disturbed hillsides, reaching five metres in a season.

Rose-scented geranium is a South African species whose leaf essential oil became a major perfume ingredient after French distillers established commercial cultivation on Reunion Island in the 1870s. Its oil is used as an affordable rose substitute and remains a staple of the fragrance industry.

Ivy-leaf geranium is native to the Eastern Cape and KwaZulu-Natal provinces of South Africa. Introduced to European horticulture in 1701 by Simon van der Stel, Dutch governor of the Cape Colony, its trailing habit made it the signature window-box plant of Mediterranean Europe, iconic in Alpine villages.

Pelargonium x hortorum is a complex hybrid derived primarily from Pelargonium zonale and P. inquinans, both endemic to the Cape region of South Africa. Dutch and British nurseries imported the parent species during the 17th and 18th centuries.

Chinese fountain grass is native to East Asia and Australia. Its soft bottlebrush flower spikes emerging like fountain jets from arching fine foliage made it one of the most popular ornamental grasses of the late 20th century. Recent taxonomic work has moved it into the genus Cenchrus as Cenchrus purpurascens.

Oriental fountain grass is a widespread native of the eastern Mediterranean through central Asia. Richard described it in 1805. Smaller and more refined than P. setaceum, forming 60-90 cm clumps with soft pinkish-white bottlebrush plumes. 'Karley Rose' and 'Tall Tails' are popular cultivars.

Pennisetum setaceum, commonly called fountain grass, is a tufted perennial (often grown as annual) grass in the family Poaceae native to tropical and subtropical North Africa, the Middle East, and southwestern Asia from Algeria and Sudan through Egypt and the Arabian Peninsula.

Feathertop grass is an Ethiopian highland native. R. Brown ex Fresenius described it in 1837. Produces distinctive fluffy white cotton-ball terminal panicles 10 cm across in late summer. Grown as annual in cooler climates and can become weedy in warm Mediterranean regions.

Rock penstemon, or cutleaf penstemon, is an Edwards Plateau and Chihuahuan Desert shrublet bearing bright rose-red flowers across a long season. Xeriscape programs promoted it through Texas A&M and the Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center.

Penstemon barbatus was described by Linnaeus in 1767 from New Mexico specimens. One of the earliest North American penstemons in horticulture, it remains a defining hummingbird perennial of Southwestern and Rocky Mountain gardens.

Cardwell's penstemon is a low evergreen subshrub of volcanic slopes in the Cascade Range of Oregon and Washington. It was named for James Cardwell, a 19th-century Oregon physician and plant collector, and is a staple of Pacific Northwest rock gardens.

Davidson's penstemon is a creeping alpine subshrub of high western North American ridges, named for Scottish-American botanist George Davidson. Its rich purple trumpets brighten rocky cirques and are prized in alpine and rock garden horticulture.

Foxglove beardtongue is native to the central and eastern United States. Its upright spikes of large white (occasionally pink-tinged) tubular flowers make it one of the most horticulturally useful native Penstemons for eastern gardens, particularly the burgundy-foliaged cultivar "Husker Red," the 1996 Perennial Plant of the Year.

Foothill penstemon is endemic to California. Produces spectacular iridescent electric-blue-to-violet tubular flowers in late spring — widely considered one of the most beautiful blue flowers of the North American flora. Cultivar "Margarita BOP" is a foundational California-native garden plant.

Showy penstemon is native to Southern California and northern Baja California. Produces spectacular tall spikes of large tubular lavender-to-magenta-to-blue flowers in late spring. One of the most dramatic native California penstemons for garden and meadow planting.

Egyptian star cluster is native to eastern Africa and the Arabian Peninsula — despite the common name it is not specifically Egyptian. Its flat-topped clusters of starry five-petaled flowers are one of the best butterfly and hummingbird flowers for warm-climate gardens, and the plant is widely grown as a tender perennial or summer annual across the US.

Peperomia argyreia, commonly called watermelon peperomia or watermelon begonia, is a small rhizomatous perennial in the pepper family (Piperaceae) native to rainforest understory of northern South America, particularly Brazil, Bolivia, Venezuela, Peru, and Ecuador.

The Ripple Peperomia is native to the Brazilian Atlantic rainforest. Its deeply corrugated heart-shaped leaves and compact size made it a mid-20th-century terrarium staple. Like other Peperomias it is non-toxic to pets.

The Baby Rubber Plant is native to the Caribbean, Mexico, and northern South America. Unlike most tropical foliage plants, it is fully non-toxic to pets and has been popular as a small desk plant since the 1970s.

String of turtles is a Brazilian rainforest epiphyte. B.S. Williams described it around 1874 from Brazilian collections. Trailing vine with tiny round dark green leaves marked with distinct pale veins resembling miniature turtle shells. Popular terrarium and small hanging plant.

Trailing jade or round-leaf peperomia is a widespread Neotropical epiphyte. Linnaeus described it as Piper rotundifolium in 1753; Kunth transferred it to Peperomia in 1816. Trailing succulent stems with tiny round succulent green leaves. Excellent hanging basket and terrarium plant.

Cineraria is a group of hybrids derived from Canary Islands species, long grown as cool-season conservatory and gift plants. Vivid daisy clusters in blue, pink, purple, red, and white make cineraria a signature late-winter florist item worldwide.

Shiso or perilla is an East and Southeast Asian native. Linnaeus described it as Ocimum frutescens in 1753; Britton transferred to Perilla in 1894. Annual mint-family herb with strongly aromatic green or purple crinkled leaves. Essential culinary herb in Japan, Korea, and Vietnam.

Perovskia atriplicifolia inhabits rocky hillsides and dry valleys across the Hindu Kush, Afghanistan, Pakistan, and Tibet. The Russian botanist George Karelin named the genus in 1841 to honor General Vasily Perovski, then governor of Orenburg.

The avocado originates in central Mexico with domestication dating to 5,000 BCE. The Aztec name "ahuacatl" meaning "testicle" evolved into Spanish "aguacate" and English "avocado." The Hass cultivar — accounting for roughly 80% of global production — came from a single mutant seedling grown by a postman in 1926 La Habra, California.

Persicaria maculosa (formerly Polygonum persicaria) is native to Europe and temperate Asia. Linnaeus described it as Polygonum persicaria in 1753; Samuel Frederick Gray transferred it to Persicaria in 1821, and molecular work in the 2000s confirmed the split.

Persicaria perfoliata, mile-a-minute weed or Asiatic tearthumb, is an herbaceous annual vine in the buckwheat family native to East Asia, South Asia, and parts of the western Pacific. It arrived in the United States around 1930 as a contaminant in holly seed received by a York County.

Parsley is a Mediterranean biennial used by Greeks for funeral wreaths and by Romans for garnishing tables. The curly form was selected in 16th-century Germany; the flat-leaf Italian parsley is the preferred culinary form in most of southern Europe and the Middle East.

Garden petunia is a 19th-century hybrid between South American species Petunia axillaris and Petunia integrifolia, first crossed by European nurseries in the 1830s. 20th-century Japanese and American breeders developed F1 grandiflora and trailing "Wave" series that dominate modern bedding and hanging-basket markets.

Lacy phacelia is a California and southwestern United States native annual whose coiled lavender flower spikes are among the top-ranked bee forage plants worldwide. European and American organic farmers sow it as a cover crop for beneficial insects.

Moon orchid is native to Southeast Asia, the Philippines, Indonesia, and northern Australia. The national flower of Indonesia ("puspa pesona") and one of the three national flowers of the Philippines. Foundation of the modern commercial phalaenopsis industry — worldwide the most commercially produced orchid, with over 100 million plants sold annually from Dutch and Taiwanese nurseries.

Reed canary grass is native to North America, Europe, and temperate Asia. While the native form was long used for erosion control and forage, Eurasian cultivar strains introduced into North America have become one of the worst wetland invasives in the northern US and Canada. The variegated cultivar "Picta" is a popular ornamental but can revert to invasive form.

Common beans are native to the Americas and were independently domesticated in Mexico and the Andes, producing distinct Mesoamerican and Andean gene pools. Kidney, black, pinto, navy, and green snap beans all derive from P. vulgaris; the Columbian Exchange made them global staples in under 200 years.

Sweet mock orange is native to southern Europe and has been grown in European gardens since at least the 16th century, introduced via the Ottoman court. Its intensely orange-blossom-scented white June flowers gave the genus its common name, though it is unrelated to true orange (Citrus).

Philadelphus lewisii grows wild along rocky slopes and streambanks across the Pacific Northwest, from British Columbia south to northern California and east to Montana. Meriwether Lewis collected specimens in 1806 along the Clearwater River in present-day Idaho; the German-American botanist Frederi.

Lemoine mock orange is a hybrid group created around 1884 by Victor Lemoine of Nancy, France, crossing the small but intensely fragrant P. microphyllus with the larger-flowered P. coronarius. The resulting compact, heavily scented shrubs revolutionized garden mock oranges.

Virginal mock orange, introduced by Lemoine in 1909, is a double-flowered hybrid derived from P. x lemoinei crossed with P. x nivalis. Its pure-white semi-double flowers and strong fragrance made it one of the most widely grown 20th-century garden mock oranges.

Tree philodendron (selloum) is native to central and southern Brazil, Paraguay, northern Argentina, and Bolivia. Its deeply lobed leaves on a thick rising trunk made it a recognizable tropical foliage plant of Mid-Century Modern interiors. Now reclassified by many as Thaumatophyllum bipinnatifidum.

'Brasil' is a variegated cultivar of Philodendron hederaceum selected in Brazil in the 1990s. Each heart-shaped leaf carries bright chartreuse-yellow central flashes on deep green. Patented in 2002. One of the best-selling trailing philodendrons of the 21st century.

Blushing philodendron or red-leaf philodendron is a Colombian native. K. Koch & Augustin described it in 1854. Climbing aroid with reddish stems and petioles and large glossy arrow-shaped green leaves flushed red underneath. Parent of major cultivars including 'Pink Princess'.

Philodendron gloriosum is native to the rainforests of Colombia. Its large heart-shaped velvety green leaves with silver-white veins, growing on a terrestrial (not climbing) rhizome, made it one of the most desirable collector's philodendrons of the 2020-2022 pandemic-era houseplant boom, with specimens selling for hundreds to thousands of dollars.

Philodendron hederaceum was described in 1829 and became one of the first tropical climbing aroids to be successfully cultivated in European heated glasshouses. Its ability to survive in low light and dry indoor air made it a Victorian parlor favorite, and it has remained a staple houseplant ever.

'Micans' is a velvet-leaved form of Philodendron hederaceum from the Caribbean and Mexico. K. Koch & Augustin described it as Philodendron micans in 1854; now treated as P. hederaceum var. hederaceum. Small heart-shaped leaves have soft iridescent bronze-green velvet texture.

Heart-leaf philodendron is a Central and South American native and one of the most widely grown houseplants. K. Koch & Sello described it in 1853. Now treated as synonym of Philodendron hederaceum by most authorities. Climbing vine with glossy heart-shaped green leaves.

'Xanadu' is a compact self-heading philodendron cultivar, now recognized as a selection of Philodendron bipinnatifidum (= Thaumatophyllum xanadu per Sakuragui et al. 2018). Discovered in Australia in the 1980s and patented. Forms 60-90 cm clumps of deeply lobed dark green leaves.

Carolina phlox is native to the southeastern US. Linnaeus described it in 1762 from specimens sent by colonial botanists. The cultivar 'Miss Lingard' from c.1900 became one of the oldest continuously grown phlox cultivars in America.

Phlox divaricata occurs naturally throughout eastern and central North America from Quebec and New England south through Georgia and west to Minnesota and east Texas, typically in rich deciduous woodlands, floodplains, and shaded slopes.

Meadow phlox is native to eastern North America along streams and wet meadows. Linnaeus described it in 1753 based on Virginia specimens sent by John Clayton. It has been cultivated since colonial times and is less mildew-prone than garden phlox.

Garden phlox is a tall-stemmed perennial native to the eastern United States. Sent to European gardens in the 1730s, it was developed into hundreds of named cultivars by French, German, and Dutch breeders and returned to America as an icon of the cottage border.

Downy phlox is a fragrant eastern and central North American prairie phlox with soft pink flowers in spring. Indigenous peoples used it in traditional medicine, and it remains a cornerstone of native prairie, meadow, and pollinator plantings in the Midwest.

Creeping phlox is native to the Appalachian Mountains of the eastern United States. Sims described it in 1805. Unlike the better-known moss phlox (P. subulata), this species is a shade-loving woodland groundcover with larger flowers and runs on stolons—perfect for woodland gardens and shaded rock gardens.

Phlox subulata grows wild on sandy and rocky slopes from New York to North Carolina. William Bartram and other early American plant collectors sent material to Europe in the late 18th century; by the early 19th century it was an established feature of British alpine and rock gardens.

Fraser's photinia is a hybrid between P. glabra and P. serratifolia, discovered in 1940 at Fraser Nurseries in Birmingham, Alabama. Its brilliant red new growth flushing through spring made it the dominant evergreen hedge plant across the American South and southern Europe.

Phragmites australis, common reed, is one of the most widespread plants on Earth, growing in wetlands across every continent except Antarctica. North America has its own native haplotypes used for thousands of years by Indigenous peoples for mats, arrow shafts, and thatch.

Turkey tangle frogfruit or matchweed is a pantropical creeping perennial. Linnaeus described it as Verbena nodiflora in 1753; Greene transferred it to Phyla in 1899. Forms flat mats with small lavender-and-white flower heads. Larval host for three US butterfly species.

Phyllostachys aureosulcata is native to Zhejiang Province on the east coast of China. The species epithet refers to the distinctive yellow sulcus (the longitudinal groove on one side of the culm between successive branches) running down the otherwise green culm — a subtle but reliable.

Phyllostachys nigra is native to Hunan Province in south-central China, where it grows in temperate hill forests. Its defining horticultural feature is its distinctive dark-black culms — new culms emerge green and gradually darken through olive, brown.

Pacific ninebark is a western North American native first described by Thomas Nuttall as Spiraea capitata in 1834 and transferred to Physocarpus by Kuntze in 1891. Used by Chehalis, Quinault, and Lummi peoples for tuberculosis and childbirth, it is now valued for riparian restoration.

Common ninebark is an eastern and central North American native, described by Maximowicz in 1879. The name 'ninebark' refers to its distinctive layers of exfoliating bark. Purple-leaved cultivars introduced in the 1990s revolutionized modern shrub landscaping.

Norway spruce is native across northern and central Europe and is the iconic European Christmas tree. London's Trafalgar Square has received an annual Norway spruce from Oslo since 1947 as a thank-you for WWII support, and Old Tjikko, a clonal Norway spruce in Sweden's Fulufjället National Park, has a root system dated at over 9,500 years old.

Engelmann spruce is native to high elevations of western North America. Parry discovered it and Engelmann described it in 1863, naming it for the Missouri botanist George Engelmann. The resonant, straight-grained wood is prized for musical instrument soundboards, including many fine acoustic guitars and violins.

White spruce is native across the boreal forest of North America. Moench described it in 1785. Indigenous peoples used roots as cordage for canoes (watap), pitch as a wound sealant, and needle tea as a source of vitamin C. The dwarf 'Conica' from Alberta, discovered in 1904, is a ubiquitous garden conifer.

Serbian spruce is a narrow endemic of the Drina River basin in Serbia and Bosnia. Discovered by Josif Pančić in 1855, it survived the Ice Ages in this small refugium. Its narrow spire-like form, tolerance of urban pollution, and adaptability have made it one of the most widely planted landscape spruces.

Oriental spruce is native to the Caucasus Mountains and northeastern Turkey. Linnaeus described it as Pinus orientalis in 1753, transferred to Picea by Link in 1847. Distinguished by the shortest needles of any spruce (6-8 mm), it is a graceful, dense, long-lived tree valued in estate and arboretum plantings.

Mountain pieris is native to the southern Appalachian mountains. Pursh described it as Andromeda floribunda in 1814; Bentham and Hooker transferred it to Pieris. More compact and heat-tolerant than the Asian P. japonica, it bears upright white bell flower clusters in early spring, fragrant to some noses and reminiscent of lily-of-the-valley.

Japanese andromeda (also called lily-of-the-valley shrub) is native to Japan, eastern China, and Taiwan. Its drooping panicles of urn-shaped white flowers in early spring and its bright red new foliage made it a staple of 20th-century American and British shade-garden planting. Like rhododendrons, it contains grayanotoxins and is toxic.

Pilea peperomioides has one of the most unusual introduction histories of any modern houseplant. It is native to Yunnan province in southern China, where it grows on damp shaded rocks in the Cangshan mountains.

Blue torch cactus is native to the Caatinga dry forests of northeastern Brazil, noted for its striking electric-blue color that intensifies with sun exposure. Its dramatic architecture has made it a signature plant of modern Brazilian landscape design, particularly in the work of Roberto Burle Marx.

Anise is an eastern Mediterranean and Levantine native. Linnaeus described it in Species Plantarum 1753. Annual umbellifer 60 cm tall with feathery leaves and umbels of tiny white flowers. Seeds are the flavoring of anise liqueurs, pastries, and aguardientes. Cultivated since Ancient Egypt.

Pinckneya, or fevertree, is a small southeastern United States tree whose showy pink bracts mimic tropical dogwoods. Colonial and early American physicians used its quinine-like bark as a malaria remedy, inspiring the common name before synthetic drugs displaced it.

Jack pine is native to the Canadian boreal forest and northern United States. Lambert described it in 1803, naming it for Joseph Banks. Serotinous cones require fire to open—an adaptation central to boreal fire ecology. It is the exclusive nesting habitat of the endangered Kirtland's warbler in Michigan.

Pinus bungeana occurs naturally in central and northern China, including Shaanxi, Shanxi, Henan, Hubei, Sichuan, and Gansu provinces, typically on limestone mountain slopes between 500 and 1800 meters.

Japanese red pine is native to Japan, Korea, northeastern China, and far-eastern Russia. Siebold and Zuccarini described it in 1842. Known as aka-matsu (red pine) for its reddish upper bark, it is culturally central in East Asia—featured in classical painting, pine mushroom (matsutake) cultivation, and bonsai.

Limber pine is native to the western North American mountains. Edwin James described it in 1823 from Colorado's Pike's Peak region. The name flexilis refers to the remarkably pliable young branches. Specimens in California's White Mountains exceed 1700 years, making it one of the longest-lived pines.

Mountain pine (mugo pine) is native to high elevations of the European Alps, Pyrenees, Carpathians, and Balkans, where it grows as a dwarf shrubby krummholz form above the upper tree line. Its compact habit has made it the standard evergreen landscape shrub pine of cool-temperate rock gardens and foundation plantings.

Pinus nigra occurs naturally in a broad band from Spain and Morocco across southern Europe through the Balkans and Turkey to Crimea and Cyprus, typically at 250-2000 m elevation. Austrian physician and botanist Johann Franz Xaver von Arnold described the species in 1785.

Pinus parviflora occurs naturally in Japan—on all four main islands—and on the Korean Peninsula, typically in mountain forests between 100 and 2500 meters elevation. German botanists Philipp Franz von Siebold and Joseph Gerhard Zuccarini described the species in 1842 in Flora Japonica.

Ponderosa pine is native across western North America from southern British Columbia to northern Mexico — a defining tree of interior western forests and an important softwood timber. Mature specimens have reddish-orange furrowed bark whose plates smell of butterscotch or vanilla on warm days.

Monterey pine is native to only five tiny relict populations — three on the California central coast (Monterey, Año Nuevo, Cambria) and two on Mexican islands (Guadalupe, Cedros). Despite being a globally-rare endangered native, it is simultaneously one of the most widely-planted timber trees in the world — the dominant softwood plantation species of New Zealand, Australia, Chile, and Spain.

Scots pine is native across Eurasia from Scotland to the Russian Far East — the most widely distributed pine in the world. It is the only pine native to Britain and the national tree of Scotland. The fragmentary ancient Caledonian Forest of Scots pines in the Scottish Highlands represents the remnant of post-glacial pine forest that once covered much of northern Europe.

Japanese black pine is native to coastal Japan and Korea. Parlatore described it in 1868, honoring Carl Peter Thunberg. Called kuro-matsu (black pine) for its dark bark, it is culturally paramount in Japan—planted on coastal dunes for centuries as wind protection, featured in classical painting, and prized as the masculine counterpart to red pine in bonsai.

Torrey pine is native to only two tiny relict populations — Torrey Pines State Reserve in San Diego County and Santa Rosa Island in the California Channel Islands. The rarest pine species in North America. Total wild population is approximately 9,000 trees in the San Diego population and 2,000 on Santa Rosa Island.

Water lettuce is most likely native to tropical Africa, though distributed through tropical freshwater globally for so long that truly wild populations are hard to identify. One of the world's worst invasive aquatic weeds, it forms dense floating mats and is banned or restricted in Florida, Texas, South Africa, and Australia.

The garden pea is one of humanity's oldest crops, domesticated in the Fertile Crescent 10,000 years ago. Gregor Mendel's 1856-1863 pea-crossing experiments at St. Thomas' Abbey in Brno founded the science of genetics, though his results went unrecognized until 1900.

Pittosporum tenuifolium is native to both North and South Islands of New Zealand, occurring naturally in coastal and lowland forests. German botanist Joseph Gaertner described the species in 1788. In the Māori language it is kohuhu or kōhūhū, and it was valued for its resinous bark (chewed as a gum.

Mock orange or Japanese pittosporum ("tobira") is native to Japan, southern Korea, and coastal southeast China. A foundational hedging and foundation shrub in Mediterranean-climate gardens worldwide, valued for dense glossy dark-green foliage, tolerance of salt spray, and creamy-white orange-blossom-scented flowers in spring.

Plantago lanceolata grows natively across Europe, North Africa, and temperate Asia. Linnaeus described it in Species Plantarum (1753). Archaeobotanical evidence shows it spread with early Neolithic agriculture across Europe.

Broadleaf plantain is a Eurasian native introduced globally with European settlement. Anishinaabe and other Indigenous peoples called it 'white man's footstep' because it spread wherever Europeans walked. Its leaves were a traditional first-aid poultice worldwide.

Blackseed plantain is an eastern North American native described by Alphonso Wood in 1847, honoring the botanist Ferdinand Rugel. Closely resembling the introduced P. major, it is distinguished by black seeds and a purplish leaf base and is a common US lawn weed.

American sycamore is native to eastern North America. Linnaeus described it in 1753. Among the largest native eastern trees—reaching 100-175 feet—its distinctive exfoliating camouflage bark and capacity to form hollow trunks sheltering wildlife have long been celebrated.

London plane is a hybrid between American sycamore and Oriental plane, probably arising in 17th-century Spain or southern France where the parent species overlapped in botanical gardens. Its astonishing urban-pollution tolerance has made it the defining street tree of London, Paris, and New York, with roughly half of London's street and park trees belonging to this hybrid.

Platycerium bifurcatum is native to Java, New Guinea, and eastern Australia, where it grows as a tree-mounted epiphyte producing two wildly different frond types in the same plant — a feature called dimorphism.

Chinese arborvitae is native to northern China, Korea, and the Russian Far East. Linnaeus described it as Thuja orientalis in 1753, and Portuguese botanist Joao Manuel Franco transferred it to its own genus Platycladus in 1949. Sacred in Chinese tradition, specimens at the Temple of Heaven in Beijing are over 600 years old.

Silver spurflower is native to northeastern Australia (Queensland and northern New South Wales). A soft silvery-gray-leaved subshrub valued as a foliage plant in subtropical and temperate summer gardens worldwide, particularly in the "Silver Shield" cultivar developed for upright growth and intense silver tone.

Cape leadwort (also called blue plumbago) is native to South Africa. Its long-blooming pale sky-blue phlox-like flower clusters have made it one of the iconic flowering shrubs of Mediterranean-climate gardens in California, the Riviera, Australia, and South Africa itself.

Plumeria rubra is native to Mexico, Central America, and Venezuela, where it was cultivated by the Aztec and Maya; Aztec nobles planted it in garden colonnades, and its flowers appear in Mesoamerican codices as a symbol of noble status.

Annual bluegrass is a cool-season European native that has become one of the most globally distributed plants, reaching even sub-Antarctic islands. Described by Linnaeus in 1753, it is the world's most troublesome winter-annual weed of lawns, golf courses, and crop fields.

Buddhist pine or kusamaki is native to southern Japan and southern China. Widely used in Chinese and Japanese temple gardens and as "luó hàn sōng" (罗汉松, "arhat pine") symbolizing Buddhist longevity, the species has become one of the most popular formal hedging conifers in the southern United States.

Podophyllum peltatum was used medicinally by Cherokee, Iroquois, and Menominee peoples. Podophyllotoxin from its rhizome is the parent compound of etoposide (VP-16), one of the most important 20th-century chemotherapy drugs for lung cancer, testicular cancer, and leukemia.

Jacob's ladder is native to temperate Europe and northern Asia. The common name refers to the plant's compound leaves whose paired leaflets arranged along a central rachis suggest the rungs of a ladder — linked in European Christian folk tradition to Jacob's biblical dream of a ladder to heaven.

Polygonatum biflorum is the North American Solomon's seal, named for rhizome scars said to resemble King Solomon's seal. Used medicinally by Iroquois and Chippewa peoples. A staple refined woodland perennial.

Common knotweed is a tough cool-season annual likely native to Eurasia, described by Linnaeus in 1753. Thriving in compacted soils, it was used in traditional Chinese medicine as 'bian xu' for urinary complaints and in Europe as an astringent herb for diarrhea.

Christmas fern is native to eastern North America. Its name refers to the traditional 18th- and 19th-century use of its durable evergreen fronds — which remain green through winter — as Christmas decoration by colonial and early American households, along with holly, ivy, and pine.

Poncirus trifoliata is the most cold-hardy citrus relative, surviving to -25°C. Native to China and Korea, it has been cultivated for 1000+ years and is now the world's primary citrus rootstock for grafting, imparting cold-hardiness and disease resistance.

Pontederia cordata is native to a remarkable latitudinal range across the Americas — from southeastern Canada south through the eastern and central United States, the Caribbean, Central America, and into the Amazon basin, south to Argentina.

Populus alba occurs naturally from Morocco and Iberia through central Europe to central Asia and western Siberia. Linnaeus described the species in Species Plantarum (1753), the name alba meaning white for the dense white woolly tomentum on leaf undersides and the smooth chalky-white to.

European aspen has the loosely-hinged leaves that produce the characteristic trembling in the slightest breeze — hence both the species name "tremula" and the common name. It is the primary host plant for many forest butterflies and forms some of Europe's most ancient clonal colonies.

Quaking aspen has the widest natural range of any North American tree. Michaux described it in 1803. Indigenous peoples used bark and leaf buds medicinally. The famed Pando clone in Utah is a single male individual spanning 43 hectares and is estimated to be one of the oldest living organisms.

Moss rose is a South American succulent annual whose brilliant jewel-toned flowers open in sunshine. Introduced to Europe in the 1820s, it became a Victorian favorite and remains a classic hot, dry-spot annual for borders, containers, and rock gardens.

Common purslane is a warm-season succulent annual likely native to northern Africa and western Asia, described by Linnaeus in 1753. A despised lawn weed to many gardeners, it is simultaneously one of the most nutritious leafy vegetables in global traditional cuisine.

Wingpod purslane, or broadleaf purslane, is a tropical American succulent annual with flat leaves rather than needle-like foliage. It tolerates partial shade better than moss rose and is marketed widely as Rio Grande Purslane and Fairytales series.

Elephant bush is a South African succulent shrub and a primary food source for wild elephants and black rhinoceros in the Eastern Cape thicket. Its exceptional carbon-sequestration capacity has made it the focus of major thicket-restoration projects and a popular bonsai subject worldwide.

An obsolete synonym of Epipremnum aureum (Golden Pothos), retained in popular horticultural literature. All descriptive and toxicity data apply as for E. aureum: native to the Solomon Islands, mildly toxic due to calcium oxalate, and a NASA-listed air-purifying houseplant.

Blue star creeper is an Australian native now classified as Lobelia pedunculata. R. Brown described it as Lobelia pedunculata in 1810; Bentham transferred it to Pratia. Forms flat 2-5 cm mats with tiny pale blue star-shaped flowers. Popular lawn substitute in mild climates.

Primula veris is native to unimproved chalk and limestone meadows from Britain to western Siberia. The name "cowslip" derives from Old English cu-slyppe ("cow dung"), reflecting its habit of flowering in cow-grazed pastures.

Primula vulgaris is native to deciduous woodlands and hedge banks across Europe, from Britain and Norway to North Africa and the Caucasus. The Latin vulgaris means "common," and prim-rose derives from Old French primerose, "first rose of spring." Chaucer, Shakespeare, and Milton reference.

Prunus avium is native to Europe, western Asia, and North Africa. Wild cherries have been collected for millennia; archaeological evidence at sites from Switzerland to the Black Sea shows consumption stretching to prehistoric times.

Taiwan or bellflower cherry is native to Taiwan, southern Japan, and southern China. Named for its distinctive bell-shaped deep-pink to crimson flowers, it blooms earlier than any other flowering cherry and thrives in mild-winter regions unsuitable for other Prunus.

Sour cherry is the primary cooking cherry of European cuisine and the ancestor of maraschino cherries (originally Croatian) and of Polish and Hungarian cherry liqueurs. Lucullus-era Romans imported cherries from Cerasus, giving the species its name and the city its enduring fruit fame.

The European plum is a hybrid of P. cerasifera and P. spinosa, first documented in the Caucasus and widely cultivated by Romans. California's Santa Clara Valley became "the Prune Capital of the World" before 20th-century urbanization converted its orchards into Silicon Valley.

Dwarf flowering almond is native to China and Japan. Described by Thunberg in 1784, its compact 4-foot shrub form smothered with double pink or white pompom flowers in spring became a Victorian garden staple and remains popular in small residential landscapes.

Fuji cherry is native to the Mt. Fuji region of Honshu, Japan. Named by Thunberg in 1784, its compact size and profuse early bloom made it a foundational parent of modern dwarf cherry hybrids including Collingwood Ingram's 'Okame' and 'Kursar'.

Cherry laurel is native to southwestern Asia and southeastern Europe. Linnaeus described it in 1753. Its evergreen leaves contain hydrocyanic acid and were used historically as a source of laurel water, a medicinal/culinary flavoring that caused many accidental poisonings before its toxicity was fully understood.

Japanese apricot (Chinese "méi" 梅, Japanese "ume") is native to southern China. Its early-winter flowers blooming in snow have been a central subject of East Asian poetry, painting, and philosophy for over 1,500 years — one of the "Three Friends of Winter" (with pine and bamboo) and one of the "Four Gentlemen" of Chinese brush painting.

Prunus persica is native to China, not Persia, despite its scientific name. Archaeological evidence from the Yangtze River valley places peach cultivation in China at least 6,000 years ago, and modern genetic studies confirm Chinese origin.

Sargent's cherry is native to Japan, Korea, and Sakhalin. Collected by Charles Sprague Sargent of the Arnold Arboretum in 1892 and named in his honor, it is among the hardiest flowering cherries and celebrated for brilliant orange-red autumn color, unusual among cherries.

Prunus serrulata is a species name historically applied to many Japanese flowering cherry cultivars ("sato-zakura," or "village cherries"), though modern taxonomy has separated some into other species or hybrid groups.

Higan cherry is a Japanese ornamental cherry cultivated for centuries, likely of hybrid origin. Introduced to Western horticulture in the 1890s, its autumn-flowering selection 'Autumnalis' blooms sporadically from fall through spring and is unique among flowering cherries.

Nanking cherry is native to northern China, Korea, and the Himalayas. Described by Thunberg in 1784, it produces pollinator-friendly pink-white flowers followed by edible tart-sweet scarlet fruits. Widely planted in Midwestern US windbreaks and homestead orchards.

Flowering almond is native to northern China. Introduced to Europe by Robert Fortune in 1855, its densely double pink flowers smothering bare branches in early spring made it a popular small ornamental. Cultivated in Chinese gardens for centuries as yu ye mei.

Yoshino cherry is a hybrid cherry (likely P. speciosa x P. subhirtella) that originated in 19th-century Tokyo. Virtually all cultivated Yoshino cherries are clones of a single seedling selected in the Somei district of Edo (modern Tokyo) around 1870, giving Japan and Washington their synchronized cherry-blossom bloom.

Pueraria montana, kudzu, is a vigorous climbing vine native to East Asia introduced to the American South in the 1870s for erosion control. Under a subsidized planting program in the 1930s it covered vast areas and is now notorious for engulfing forests and buildings at up to thirty centimetres per day in summer.

Common lungwort is a European woodland perennial described by Linnaeus in 1753. Under the medieval doctrine of signatures, its spotted leaves were believed to cure lung diseases, and herbalists used it from at least the 12th century.

Pulmonaria saccharata grows wild in damp limestone woodlands of the Italian Apennines and southeastern France. Miller described the species in 1768. The genus name Pulmonaria (from pulmo, lung) reflects the medieval doctrine of signatures.

Punica granatum is native to Iran and northern India, with domestication at least 5,000 years ago. Carbonized pomegranate remains have been found at Bronze Age archaeological sites across the Near East.

Pyracantha coccinea has been cultivated since medieval Europe as a hedging plant. Its name combines Greek 'fire' and 'thorn.' Widely planted in American gardens since the 19th century. 'Mohave' and related cultivars provide modern fireblight resistance.

European pear is native to western Asia and has been cultivated since antiquity. Homer called the pear tree "a gift from the gods," Romans carried pears across their empire, and 19th-century Belgian breeders created most of the modern buttery-fleshed cultivars including Bosc and Anjou.

Coast live oak is native to the coastal ranges of California and northern Baja California — the iconic dark-green evergreen oak of coastal Southern California from Marin south to Ensenada. Indigenous acorn harvest from this oak was a foundation of Native Californian culture for 10,000+ years.

White oak is native to eastern North America and is the premier American cooperage timber — nearly all US bourbon whiskey by federal law must be aged in new charred white oak barrels. Individual trees live 300-500 years; the Wye Oak of Maryland was estimated over 460 years old when it fell in 2002.

Swamp white oak is native to eastern North America. Described by Carl Ludwig Willdenow in 1801, it was chosen as the primary tree for the 9/11 Memorial in New York City—400+ specimens planted to symbolize resilience and regrowth around the Reflecting Pools.

Scarlet oak is native to eastern North America. Named by Carl Ludwig Münchhausen in 1770, its fiery crimson autumn color is considered the most brilliant of any American oak, making it a top selection for large residential and park landscapes.

California black oak is native to mountains of California, southwestern Oregon, and northern Baja California. The acorns were the single most important traditional food of interior California Indigenous peoples — preferred over all other oak species for flavor and oil content — and the species was systematically managed by Indigenous burning for over 1,000 years before European arrival.

Laurel oak is a fast-growing semi-evergreen southeastern United States oak of floodplains and stream terraces. Colonial naturalists described it in the 1700s, and the species became a common southern shade tree prized for quick establishment despite a shorter lifespan than other oaks.

Valley oak is native to interior valleys of California. The largest oak in western North America — individual trees can exceed 35 meters in height with trunks over 3 meters in diameter and live 400+ years. Massive mature valley oaks are a defining feature of California Central Valley landscape, though much of the species' native range has been lost to agriculture and development.

Quercus montana (also commonly Q. prinus) grows on dry rocky ridge tops throughout the Appalachian Mountains from New England and southern Ontario to northern Alabama. German botanist Carl Ludwig Willdenow described it in 1801.

Quercus nigra, the water oak or spotted oak, is native to the southeastern United States coastal plain and piedmont from New Jersey to Texas. Mark Catesby illustrated it in The Natural History of Carolina, Florida and the Bahama Islands in the 1730s, noting its variable leaves.

Pin oak is native to the east-central United States and has become one of the most widely planted urban street-tree oaks in North America due to its straight trunk and fast growth, though iron chlorosis on alkaline soils has limited its success in many regions.

Willow oak is native to the southeastern US. Described by Linnaeus in 1753, its narrow willow-like leaves are unusual among oaks. Tolerance of clay soils and poor drainage combined with fast growth and fine texture made it a premier southern street tree.

English oak is native to Europe and western Asia and has been a central cultural symbol of England, Germany, France, and Russia for over a thousand years. The HMS Victory's oak hull required 6,000 mature trees, and the loss of English oak forests to naval construction drove early forest-conservation law.

Shumard oak is native to the southeastern US and Midwest. Named by Samuel Buckley in 1860 honoring Texas geologist Benjamin F. Shumard, it combines scarlet fall color with faster growth and better urban tolerance than scarlet oak, becoming a popular modern street tree.

Post oak is a slow-growing, drought-tolerant oak of the southern and central United States, dominant in the Cross Timbers ecoregion. Colonial and pioneer settlers prized its rot-resistant wood for fence posts, barn timbers, and railroad ties, giving the tree its common name.

Meadow buttercup is a Eurasian native described by Linnaeus in 1753. A common meadow wildflower that colonized North America with European settlers, it was avoided by grazing animals due to acrid protoanemonin, which inspired folk sayings about 'buttercup burn'.

The Persian Buttercup is native to the eastern Mediterranean and western Asia. Cultivated in Ottoman Constantinople as a rival to the tulip in the 17th century, it has more recently become a defining premium spring cut flower associated with weddings.

Ranunculus ficaria, lesser celandine or fig buttercup, is a small European perennial with glossy heart-shaped leaves and bright yellow flowers in early spring. Introduced to North America as a garden plant, it has escaped into floodplain forests where it carpets the ground before native ephemerals emerge, suppressing them.

The radish was among the first crops domesticated in the Old World, with Greek inscriptions recording it as offerings at Delphi. The Japanese daikon form grew enormous — an individual specimen from Sakurajima can weigh 45 kilograms — and underlies much of East Asian cuisine.

Mexican hat (also called prairie coneflower or upright prairie coneflower) is native to the Great Plains of the United States and Canada and northern Mexico. Its distinctive tall conical central disk surrounded by drooping yellow (or red-brown) ray petals — suggesting a sombrero — gives the species its memorable common name.

Rebutia minuscula, commonly called red crown cactus, is a tiny globose cactus endemic to the mountains of northwestern Argentina in the provinces of Tucumán and Salta at 1500-2500 meters elevation. German botanist Karl Moritz Schumann described the species in 1895 and established the genus Rebutia.

Mignonette was introduced to Europe from Egypt around 1798 via Napoleon's expedition and Joséphine Bonaparte's Malmaison gardens. Its raspberry-violet fragrance defined 19th-century European garden scent. Heirloom strains preserve the original powerful fragrance, largely lost in modern breeding.

Rhamnus cathartica, common buckthorn, is a European shrub or small tree introduced to North America in the early 1800s as a hedging plant. Its black berries are laxative (hence cathartica) and spread rapidly by birds. It now dominates midwestern woodlands and is an obligate overwintering host of soybean aphid.

Mini monstera or monstera minima is a Malaysian and southern Thailand native. Hooker fil. described it in 1893. Climbing aroid with small fenestrated leaves resembling miniature Monstera deliciosa. Became a viral houseplant trend in 2018-2021 despite being neither Monstera nor Philodendron.

The Lady Palm was cultivated in Japanese imperial gardens for centuries before Western botanists encountered it. Its actual wild origin is unclear — it may be endemic to southern China but has been known only from cultivation for hundreds of years.

Chinese rhubarb or Turkish rhubarb is a Chinese and Tibetan native. Linnaeus described it in 1759. Medicinal rhubarb cultivated for millennia in China as source of dahuang, a major purgative drug. Tall dramatic plants with deeply palmately-lobed leaves and 2-meter red-plumed flower stalks.

Mistletoe Cactus has the most unusual distribution of any cactus: it is the only cactus native outside the Americas, with populations in Africa and Sri Lanka, likely carried long ago by migratory birds. Non-toxic to pets.

Rhododendron arborescens occurs naturally along mountain streams and wet woodlands of the Appalachians from New York and Pennsylvania south through West Virginia, Virginia, the Carolinas, Tennessee, and Kentucky, to northern Georgia and Alabama.

Rhododendron augustinii was collected by Irish plant hunter Augustine Henry in Hubei, China, in 1885. It is one of very few truly blue-flowering rhododendrons and became the primary parent of 20th-century blue rhododendron hybrid breeding.

Rhododendron austrinum, the Florida flame azalea or orange azalea, is a deciduous shrub native to north Florida, south Alabama, south Georgia, and southeast Mississippi, growing along streambanks and rich wooded slopes.

Piedmont azalea, or mountain honeysuckle, is the most widespread deciduous azalea of the southeastern United States. Its fragrant pink spring flowers colored early American woodlands and inspired collectors, including Thomas Jefferson, who admired native azaleas as garden subjects.

Catawba rhododendron is native to the southern Appalachians of the eastern US, where its mass purple-pink June bloom covers mountain balds in Tennessee, North Carolina, and Virginia. One of the hardiest rhododendrons, it is the parent of the most cold-tolerant modern hybrids.

Pacific rhododendron is Washington State's official flower, designated in 1892. Native to coastal forests from British Columbia to California, it was documented by Archibald Menzies during the Vancouver Expedition and became an icon of the Pacific Northwest.

Piedmont or Carolina rhododendron is native to the southern Appalachians. Michaux described it in 1803. A smaller-leaved, heat-tolerant alternative to R. catawbiense and R. maximum, it grows where other rhododendrons fail in the hot humid southeastern US and has contributed heat tolerance to modern hybrids.

Pontic rhododendron is native to the Black Sea region (Turkey, Georgia, Bulgaria) and small relict populations in Iberia. Introduced to British horticulture in 1763, it has become one of the most serious invasive plants in the UK and Ireland, where it forms dense thickets that exclude native flora. Its nectar contains grayanotoxins, producing the historically famous "mad honey."

Royal azalea is native to Korea, Manchuria, and Russian Far East. Maximowicz described it in 1870, naming it for Russian naval captain Baron Alexander von Schlippenbach who collected it in Korea in 1854. Among the most elegant azaleas, its large soft-pink flowers open before the whorled leaves expand.

Yakushima rhododendron is endemic to Yakushima Island, southern Japan. Nakai described it in 1921. Compact, wind-sculpted, and ultra-cold-hardy, a single plant gifted to the Royal Horticultural Society in 1934 revolutionized modern rhododendron breeding and is the ancestor of hundreds of dwarf cultivars.

Rhodotypos scandens grows wild in thickets and forest edges of central China, Korea, and Japan. Philipp Franz von Siebold and Joseph Gerhard Zuccarini described it in 1843 from specimens collected in Japan; the genus name combines Greek rhodon (rose) and typos (pattern).

Ribes divaricatum, the coastal black gooseberry or spreading gooseberry, is a deciduous thorny shrub native to the Pacific coast of North America from British Columbia to California, typically in moist forest edges and riparian zones.

Blackcurrant is native to central and northern Europe and was banned in parts of the US for much of the 20th century because it hosts white-pine blister rust, threatening lumber. The British WWII effort made blackcurrant central to children's nutrition when citrus imports ceased.

Ribes rubrum is native to western Europe, from Britain and France east to Belarus and Poland. Its cultivation began in European monasteries during the 15th century; it was among the earliest garden small fruits to be systematically grown in Renaissance Europe.

Gooseberry is a European species cultivated in English gardens since Tudor times. 19th-century British "gooseberry clubs" competed for heaviest fruit — the surviving tradition in Cheshire continues annual weigh-offs, with modern record berries exceeding 60 grams from a single fruit.

Castor bean is likely native to eastern Africa. One of the most ancient oil crops — seeds found in Egyptian tombs from 4000 BCE; mentioned in the Biblical Book of Jonah as the "gourd" providing shade. The seeds contain ricin, among the most deadly natural toxins known — a single seed can kill a child.

Bristly locust or rose acacia is native to the southern Appalachians. Described by Linnaeus in 1767, its pink pea-like flower racemes and bristly-hairy stems made it a popular lawn shrub, though its suckering habit limits contemporary use.

Fingerleaf rodgersia is native to central and western China. Batalin described it in 1893 from specimens collected by the Potanin expedition. Its palmate leaves resembling horse chestnut foliage give it its specific epithet and distinguished garden presence.

Featherleaf rodgersia is a Chinese perennial from Yunnan and Sichuan. Described by William Hemsley in 1886 from specimens collected by Augustine Henry, it became prized in European bog and woodland gardens for bold pinnate foliage and pink plume flowers.

Rodger's bronze-leaf, from Japan and Korea, was named for US Navy Commodore John Rodgers, who led the 1853 North Pacific Expedition that collected the type specimen. Asa Gray described the genus in 1857. It is grown for dramatic palmate leaves and cream flower plumes.

Elderberry-leaved rodgersia, from western China, was described by Hemsley in 1906 from collections made by E.H. Wilson. It has pinnate foliage resembling elderberry and was introduced to Veitch Nursery in 1903, becoming a favored bog-garden plant.

Matilija poppy is native to Southern California and northern Baja California chaparral. Produces enormous white crepe-paper-textured flowers 15-20 cm across with huge golden central staminal disks — "fried-egg plant" in common parlance, the largest flowers of any California-native plant. Named for Irish astronomer Thomas Romney Robinson and Irish botanist Thomas Coulter.

Rosa 'About Face' is a bronzy-orange grandiflora rose bred by Tom Carruth at Weeks Roses in 2005, distinguished by inside-out colouring: petal reverses show deep bronzy-orange while insides are lighter golden-orange, so buds look very different from open flowers. It won the All-America Rose Selections award in 2005.

Rosa 'Abraham Darby' is an apricot-pink English shrub rose bred by David Austin at Albrighton, England, released in 1985 from a cross between the climber 'Aloha' and 'Yellow Cushion'. Austin named the cultivar for Abraham Darby I.

Rosa 'America' is a coral-salmon climbing rose bred by William Warriner at Jackson & Perkins in 1976 for the American bicentennial. It won the All-America Rose Selections award that year, the first climber to do so, with high-centred coral blooms and strong spicy clove fragrance.

Rosa 'Angel Face' is a lavender floribunda rose bred by American breeder Herb Swim at Armstrong Roses in 1968, a cross between ('Circus' x 'Lavender Pinocchio') and 'Sterling Silver'. Strongly fragrant, it won the All-America Rose Selections award in 1969 and the American Rose Society's James Alexander Gamble Fragrance Medal in 2001.

Rosa 'At Last' is a warm apricot-orange shrub rose released by Proven Winners in 2017, bred to combine the full old-rose form and strong fragrance of hybrid teas with the disease resistance of modern landscape roses. Fully double cupped peach-apricot blooms with a rich tea-rose fragrance appear continuously on compact mounded plants that need no spraying.

Rosa 'Barbra Streisand' is a lavender hybrid tea rose bred by Tom Carruth at Weeks Roses in 2001, chosen and named by the singer herself. The large richly fragrant mauve-pink blooms with ruffled petals won the American Rose Society's James Alexander Gamble Fragrance Medal in 2010 for exceptional citrus-and-rose scent.
