Rosa 'Baronne Prevost' is a clear pink Hybrid Perpetual rose bred by French nurseryman Jean Desprez in 1842, among the earliest Hybrid Perpetuals and one of the few still widely grown today. Huge quartered rose-pink blooms with a strong old-rose fragrance on stiff upright canes made it a staple of Victorian cutting gardens and rose-exhibition halls.

Rosa 'Betty Boop' is a bicoloured creamy-yellow-and-red floribunda rose bred by Tom Carruth at Weeks Roses in 1999, named with permission from King Features Syndicate. Wavy single blooms with yellow centres edged crimson bloom in huge clusters on disease-resistant plants and won the All-America Rose Selections award in 1999.

Rosa 'Blaze' is a bright scarlet-red climbing rose bred by American breeder J. H. Nicolas in 1932, a cross between 'Paul's Scarlet Climber' and 'Gruss an Teplitz'. Its vivid scarlet semi-double blooms in huge clusters, repeat-flowering habit, and vigorous hardy climbing growth made it the most widely planted red climber in North America for decades.

Rosa 'Bonica' is a soft pink shrub rose bred by French breeder Marie-Louise Meilland in 1981, the first shrub rose ever to win the All-America Rose Selections award (1987). Arching canes carry abundant clusters of small double pink blooms continuously; red-orange hips follow autumn flowers, and the plant is exceptionally disease-resistant and hardy.

Rosa 'Boscobel' is a rich salmon-pink English shrub rose bred by David Austin in 2012, named for the seventeenth-century Boscobel House in Shropshire where Charles II hid in an oak tree after defeat at Worcester. Dense rosette-shaped blooms in warm coral-salmon tones with a strong myrrh fragrance bloom continuously on a compact upright plant.

Rosa 'Brass Band' is a warm apricot floribunda rose bred by American hybridizer Jack E. Christensen at Armstrong Roses in California, released in 1993 from a complex cross involving 'Gold Badge', 'Zorina', and 'First Edition'.

Rosa 'Carefree Beauty' is a medium-pink shrub rose bred by Dr. Griffith Buck at Iowa State University in 1977, part of his program for hardy disease-resistant roses for the American upper Midwest. Semi-double pink blooms, continuous repeat, and Zone 4 winter survival made it a landmark Buck rose.

Rosa 'Carefree Wonder' is a medium-pink shrub rose bred by the Meilland family in France in 1990, released in the Carefree series to continue Dr. Buck's legacy of hardy disease-resistant roses. Semi-double bright pink blooms with white reverses appear continuously on rounded dense plants ideal for hedging, and it won the All-America Rose Selections award in 1991.

Rosa 'Cecile Brunner', sometimes called the sweetheart rose, is a delicate pink polyantha rose bred by French breeder Joseph Pernet-Ducher in 1881 from a cross involving Rosa multiflora. Tiny perfectly formed miniature hybrid-tea-shaped buds open to soft pink blooms a few centimetres across, and the rose has been a cottage-garden favourite for nearly 150 years.

Rosa 'Cherish' is a coral-pink floribunda rose bred by William Warriner at Jackson & Perkins in 1980, a cross between 'Bridal Pink' and 'Matador'. It won the All-America Rose Selections award in 1980 for its perfectly formed high-centred coral-pink blooms carried singly on long stems, making it an exhibition-quality floribunda often grown as a hybrid tea.

Rosa 'Chrysler Imperial' is a dark red hybrid tea rose bred by Dr. Walter Lammerts in California in 1952, named after the Chrysler luxury car. It won the All-America Rose Selections award in 1953 and the American Rose Society's David Fuerstenberg Prize in 1956, prized for classic high-centred crimson blooms and powerful old-rose fragrance.

Rosa 'Cupcake' is a medium-pink miniature rose bred by American amateur hybridizer Mark C. Spies and released by Justice Miniature Roses in 1981, from a cross of 'Gene Boerner' and a 'Rumba' x 'Over the Rainbow' seedling. It won the ARS Award of Excellence for Miniatures in 1983.

Rosa 'Dainty Bess' is a single-flowered pink hybrid tea rose bred by English breeder W. E. B. Archer in 1925, a cross between 'Ophelia' and 'Kitchener of Khartoum'. With just five wavy soft-pink petals surrounding striking maroon stamens, it broke from the full-petalled hybrid tea norm and won the Royal National Rose Society's Gold Medal the year it was introduced.

Rosa 'Don Juan' is a velvety dark red climbing hybrid tea rose bred by Italian breeder Michele Malandrone in 1958, a cross between 'New Dawn' seedling and 'New Yorker'. Long pointed buds open to classic high-centred deep crimson blooms with a powerful damask fragrance, making it one of the most sought-after red climbers in American gardens for over sixty years.

Rosa 'Double Delight' is a bicoloured hybrid tea rose bred by Herb Swim and A. Ellis in California in 1977, a cross between 'Granada' and 'Garden Party'. Creamy white petals blush strawberry red at the edges in response to sun, giving each flower a unique pattern. It won the 1977 AARS award and was inducted into the Rose Hall of Fame in 1985.

Rosa 'Double Knock Out' is a fully double cherry-red shrub rose bred by William J. Radler and released by Conard-Pyle in 2004 from an unnamed seedling crossed with 'Knock Out'. The cultivar was developed to answer one of the commonest criticisms of the original 'Knock Out': its single semi-double.

Rosa 'Drift Coral' is a dwarf groundcover shrub rose from the Meilland Drift series released by Conard-Pyle in the late 2000s. Clusters of double coral-orange miniature blooms open continuously on compact spreading plants, sharing the series' disease resistance and easy-care habit.

Rosa 'Drift Pink' is a dwarf groundcover shrub rose released by Conard-Pyle in 2008 as part of the Meilland-bred Drift series, crosses of full-size groundcover roses with miniatures. The compact spreading plant covers itself with clusters of double soft pink blooms all season and is ideal for small gardens, containers, and front-of-border plantings.

Rosa 'Drift Red' is a dwarf groundcover shrub rose from the Meilland Drift series, producing clusters of bright cherry-red double miniature blooms on compact low-spreading plants. Like its Drift siblings, it offers exceptional disease resistance, continuous flowering, and cold hardiness, making it ideal for small gardens and container plantings.

Rosa 'Easy Going' is a warm yellow-apricot floribunda rose bred by English breeder Harkness Roses in 1996 as a sport of 'Livin' Easy'. It inherits its parent's easy-care habit and disease resistance, producing clusters of ruffled peachy-yellow blooms on a tough compact plant that thrives with minimal spraying or attention.

Rosa 'Eden' (also called 'Pierre de Ronsard') is a soft pink-and-cream romantica climbing rose bred by Jacques Mouchotte at the Meilland nursery in 1985. Huge old-fashioned cupped blooms with ruffled pink petals fading to cream on the outer edges made it the World's Favourite Rose in 2006, and it remains the most popular wedding-garden climber worldwide.

Rosa 'Elina' is a pale primrose-yellow hybrid tea rose bred by Northern Irish breeder Pat Dickson in 1984, a cross between 'Nana Mouskouri' and 'Lolita'. Renowned for long stems and disease resistance, it won the World Federation of Rose Societies Hall of Fame in 2006 and is a standard cut-flower cultivar across Europe.

Rosa 'Europeana' is a dark red floribunda rose bred by Dutch breeder Gerrit de Ruiter in 1963. Huge trusses of deeply ruffled velvety crimson blooms with bronzy foliage won it the Gold Medal of The Hague in 1962 and the All-America Rose Selections award in 1968, setting a new standard for floribunda colour intensity.

Rosa 'Flower Carpet Pink' is a groundcover shrub rose bred by German breeder Werner Noack in 1989, the first of the landmark Flower Carpet series designed for low-maintenance mass planting. Sprawling arching canes carry carpets of bright pink semi-double blooms continuously from spring to frost on nearly disease-free evergreen-looking plants.

Rosa 'Flower Carpet Red' is a bright red groundcover shrub rose released by Werner Noack in the 1990s as a colour addition to the Flower Carpet series. It retains the series' low spreading habit, continuous bloom, and exceptional disease resistance, producing carpets of small semi-double cherry-red blooms suitable for slopes, borders, and mass landscape planting.

Rosa 'Fourth of July' is a striped red-and-white climbing rose bred by American hybridizer Tom Carruth at Weeks Roses in Upland, California, released in 1999 from a cross involving 'Roller Coaster' and 'Altissimo'.

Rosa 'Fragrant Cloud' is a coral-red hybrid tea bred by German breeder Mathias Tantau Jr. in 1963, regarded as one of the most powerfully fragrant roses ever introduced. It won the James Alexander Gamble Fragrance Medal in 1969 with a rich old-damask scent that carries metres across a garden.

Rosa 'Garden Party' is a creamy white hybrid tea rose bred by Herb Swim in California in 1959, a cross between 'Charlotte Armstrong' and 'Peace'. The elegant high-centred blooms show a soft pink flush at the petal edges, inheriting 'Peace' coloration. It won the All-America Rose Selections award in 1960 for exceptional form and garden performance.

Rosa 'Gertrude Jekyll' is a strong pink English shrub rose bred by David Austin in 1986, named for the pioneering English garden designer. Quartered rosette-shaped deep pink blooms carry the quintessential old-damask fragrance that Austin describes as the definitive old-rose scent, and it won the American Rose Society's Gamble Fragrance Medal in 2002.

Rosa 'Gold Medal' is a golden yellow grandiflora rose bred by Jack Christensen at Armstrong Roses in 1982, a cross between 'Yellow Pages' and ('Granada' x 'Garden Party'). Long pointed buds open to high-centred deep yellow blooms lightly flushed coral on the petal edges, and the cultivar remains one of the finest yellow grandifloras for cutting and exhibition.

Rosa 'Golden Celebration' is a rich golden-yellow English shrub rose bred by David Austin in 1992, one of his largest-flowered introductions at up to fourteen centimetres across. Deeply cupped luminous gold blooms with strong tea-rose-and-Sauternes fragrance appear continuously on arching shrubs that can be trained as short climbers in warm climates.

Rosa 'Golden Showers' is a bright yellow climbing rose bred by Dr. Walter Lammerts at Germain Seed and Plant Company in California, released in 1956 from a cross of 'Charlotte Armstrong' and 'Captain Thomas'. Loose semi-double blooms open golden yellow and fade to cream, winning the 1957 AARS award.

Rosa 'Graham Thomas' is a golden-yellow English shrub rose bred by David Austin in 1983, named for the horticulturist and old-rose champion Graham Stuart Thomas. Combining the fullness and fragrance of old roses with modern repeat bloom, its deep cupped yellow blooms helped launch the English Rose revolution and won the World Federation of Rose Societies Hall of Fame in 2009.

Rosa 'Heritage' is a soft blush-pink English shrub rose bred by David Austin in 1984, a cross involving 'Wife of Bath' and an iceberg seedling. Perfectly symmetrical cupped pale pink blooms carry a strong lemon-and-honey fragrance, and the cultivar is widely regarded as one of the most beautifully formed English Roses Austin ever bred.

Rosa 'Home Run' is a bright scarlet-red shrub rose bred by American breeder Tom Carruth and Ping Lim for Weeks Roses in 2006, developed specifically to resist both black spot and downy mildew, diseases that affect even the original Knock Out rose. Single vivid red blooms cover the compact plant continuously on nearly disease-free foliage.

Rosa 'Hot Cocoa' is a smoky chocolate-russet floribunda rose bred by American hybridizer Tom Carruth at Weeks Roses in Upland, California, released in 2002 from a complex cross of 'Altissimo' and 'Livin' Easy'.

Rosa 'Iceberg' is a pure white floribunda bred by Reimer Kordes in 1958, released in Germany as 'Schneewittchen' (Snow White). Profuse semi-double white blooms on disease-resistant plants made it the most widely planted white rose of the 20th century, inducted into the Rose Hall of Fame in 1983.

Rosa 'Ingrid Bergman' is a deep red hybrid tea rose bred by Danish breeder Niels Poulsen in 1984, named with permission from the estate of the Swedish film star. The velvety dark red blooms on vigorous upright plants won the World Federation of Rose Societies Hall of Fame in 2000 and remain one of the most planted red roses in Europe.

Rosa 'John F. Kennedy' is a white hybrid tea rose bred by Eugene Boerner at Jackson & Perkins in 1965, introduced shortly after the American president's death. Large greenish-white buds open to classic high-centred pure white blooms with a strong tea-rose fragrance, making it a longtime favourite memorial rose in gardens and cemeteries.

Rosa 'Joseph's Coat' is a multicoloured climbing rose bred by American breeders Herb Swim and O. L. Weeks in 1964, a cross between 'Buccaneer' and 'Circus'. Each cluster shows flowers in every shade from gold to orange to crimson simultaneously, evoking the biblical Joseph's coat of many colours. It won the Bagatelle Gold Medal in 1964.

Rosa 'Jude the Obscure' is a deep cupped buff-yellow English shrub rose bred by David Austin in 1995 and named for Thomas Hardy's 1895 novel. Incurved chalice-shaped blooms of pale buff yellow release an extraordinarily powerful fruity fragrance reminiscent of guava and sweet white wine, regarded as one of the strongest-scented English Roses.

Rosa 'Julia Child' is a butter-yellow floribunda rose bred by Tom Carruth at Weeks Roses and personally chosen by the American chef, released in 2004. Clusters of perfectly formed buttery-gold blooms with a strong licorice-and-anise fragrance and exceptional disease resistance won it the All-America Rose Selections award in 2006.

Rosa 'Just Joey' is a coppery-buff hybrid tea rose bred by English breeder Roger Pawsey of Cants of Colchester in 1972, a cross between 'Fragrant Cloud' and 'Dr. A. J. Verhage'. Named for Pawsey's wife Joey, its ruffled apricot-copper petals and strong fruity fragrance won it the World Federation of Rose Societies Hall of Fame in 1994.

Rosa 'Knock Out' is a cherry-red shrub rose bred by amateur Wisconsin hybridizer William Radler and released by Conard-Pyle in 2000. Its nearly disease-free foliage, continuous bloom without deadheading, and cold hardiness revolutionized the American rose market and it became the best-selling rose in North American history.

Rosa 'Lady of Shalott' is a warm orange-apricot English shrub rose bred by David Austin in 2009, named for Tennyson's poem. Loosely cupped chalice-shaped blooms open warm salmon-pink with golden yellow undersides, and the exceptionally disease-resistant plant is one of the toughest and most reliable later Austin introductions for beginners and tough climates.

Rosa 'Louis Philippe' is a crimson China rose bred by French nurseryman M. Guerin in 1834 and named for King Louis Philippe I, the liberal Orleanist monarch who reigned from 1830 until the 1848 revolution.

Rosa 'Macy's Pride' is a cream-yellow shrub rose released by the Brownell family of New Hampshire as part of their hardy subzero rose program. Named for the American department store partnership, it offers ivory blooms with lemon-yellow centres on cold-hardy plants for northern gardens.

Rosa 'Madame Plantier' is a pure white hybrid alba-noisette rose bred by French nurseryman Plantier in 1835, once-blooming but prolific in a single spectacular early-summer display. Arching canes covered in creamy-white pompon blooms with a powerful old-rose fragrance make it a classic of nineteenth-century French rose gardens, still widely grown in heritage collections.

Rosa 'Marilyn Monroe' is a creamy apricot hybrid tea rose bred by Tom Carruth at Weeks Roses in 2002, released with licensing approval from the Monroe estate. Long elegant buds open to classic high-centred blooms in shades of buff apricot with cream and coral tones, carried on long straight stems above disease-resistant foliage.

Rosa 'Mary Rose' is a medium-pink English shrub rose bred by David Austin in 1983, named for the Tudor warship raised from the Solent in 1982. Loosely cupped rose-pink blooms with honey-and-almond fragrance bloom continuously on a bushy reliable plant that became one of Austin's most popular introductions for cottage gardens.

Rosa 'Meidiland Red' (also called 'Red Meidiland') is a low spreading groundcover shrub rose bred by the Meilland family in France in 1987. Small single cherry-red blooms with white eyes appear continuously in large clusters on dense low-mounded plants, one of the earliest successes of the Meidiland landscape series that pioneered the modern low-maintenance shrub-rose category.

Rosa 'Mister Lincoln' is a dark red hybrid tea rose bred by Herb Swim and O. L. Weeks in California in 1964, a cross of 'Chrysler Imperial' and 'Charles Mallerin'. Named for President Abraham Lincoln, it is prized for velvety crimson blooms and strong damask fragrance, winning the 1965 All-America Rose Selections award.

Rosa 'Munstead Wood' is a deep velvety crimson English shrub rose bred by David Austin in 2007, named for Gertrude Jekyll's own Surrey garden. Deep cupped blooms of near-black crimson with rich old-rose fragrance and a compact habit suit small gardens, and the rose won the American Rose Society's James Alexander Gamble Fragrance Medal in 2016.

Rosa 'Nearly Wild' is a single-flowered floribunda rose bred by American breeder E. S. Brownell in 1941, a cross between 'Dr. W. Van Fleet' and 'Leuchtstern'. Clusters of five-petalled clear pink blooms with yellow centres look like wild roses, bloom continuously, and remain one of the hardiest and most disease-resistant older floribundas for northern gardens.

Rosa 'New Dawn' is a pale pink climbing rose bred in the United States by Henry Dreer and patented in 1930 as the first plant patent ever granted in the United States. A repeat-flowering sport of 'Dr. W. Van Fleet', it blooms all summer with soft pink semi-double flowers, was inducted into the World Federation of Rose Societies Hall of Fame in 1997.

Rosa 'Old Blush' (also called 'Parson's Pink China', 'Common Monthly', and 'Monthly Rose') is a pale pink China rose of uncertain Chinese origin, grown in China for centuries before Swedish East India Company ships carried it to European gardens around 1752.

Rosa 'Olivia Rose Austin' is a soft pink English shrub rose bred by David Austin in 2014 and named for his granddaughter. Austin called it possibly his most beautiful introduction, with rosette-shaped pale pink blooms, strong classic fragrance, and exceptional disease resistance.

Rosa 'Olympiad' is a brilliant red hybrid tea rose bred by Canadian-born breeder Sam McGredy IV in New Zealand in 1982, named for the 1984 Los Angeles Olympic Games where it was the official rose. It won the All-America Rose Selections award in 1984 and remains a standard for pure, non-fading, sun-resistant crimson in modern rose gardens.

Rosa 'Oso Easy Double Red' is a compact landscape shrub rose bred by David Zlesak in the United States and released by Proven Winners in the 2010s. It produces continuous clusters of fully double bright red blooms on an exceptionally disease-resistant plant, part of the Oso Easy series designed specifically for no-spray low-maintenance gardening.

Rosa 'Pascali' is a pure white hybrid tea rose bred by Belgian breeder Louis Lens in 1963, a cross between 'Queen Elizabeth' and 'White Butterfly'. It won the All-America Rose Selections award in 1969 and the World Federation of Rose Societies Hall of Fame in 1991, recognized as one of the finest white hybrid teas ever bred.

Rosa 'Paul Shirville' is a salmon-pink hybrid tea rose bred by Pat Dickson of Dickson Roses in Northern Ireland and released by Harkness Roses in Hertfordshire, England, in 1983 from a cross involving 'Compassion' and 'Mischief'.

Rosa 'Peace' is a hybrid tea rose bred by Francis Meilland at Cap d'Antibes in 1935. Budwood reached Conard-Pyle in Pennsylvania days before France fell in 1940; the rose was launched in 1945 and renamed 'Peace' on the day Berlin fell, with blooms given to delegates at the UN's founding in San Francisco.

Rosa 'Perfume Delight' is a medium pink hybrid tea rose bred by Herb Swim and O. L. Weeks in California in 1973. Named for its intense old-rose fragrance, it won the All-America Rose Selections award in 1974 and remains prized by home gardeners for its sweet damask scent and large perfectly formed high-centred pink blooms on vigorous upright plants.

Rosa 'Pink Knock Out' is a medium-pink shrub rose released in 2004 by William Radler and Conard-Pyle as a colour sport of the revolutionary 'Knock Out'. The single bright pink blooms share the parent's disease resistance, continuous flowering, and cold hardiness, expanding the low-maintenance Knock Out family into pink for landscape and home gardens.

Rosa 'Pink Promise' is a pale pink hybrid tea rose registered by Brad Jalbert in 2009. Jackson & Perkins designated a portion of sales to the National Breast Cancer Foundation, making the cultivar a popular symbol of breast cancer awareness. Large classic high-centred blooms open soft pink on long stems with light old-rose fragrance.

Rosa 'Playboy' is a vivid multicoloured floribunda rose bred by Scottish hybridizer Alec Cocker of James Cocker & Sons in Aberdeen, released in 1976 from a cross between 'City of Leeds' and ('Chanelle' x 'Piccadilly').

Rosa 'Popcorn' is a tiny white miniature rose bred by American hybridizer Dennison Morey in 1973, a cross of 'Katharina Zeimet' and 'Diamond Jewel'. Clusters of small semi-double white blooms with golden stamens resemble popcorn kernels, giving the rose its name and lasting container popularity.

Rosa 'Princess Anne' is a deep pink English shrub rose bred by David Austin in 2010 and named with permission of Her Royal Highness The Princess Royal. Rosette-shaped blooms open rich deep pink with yellow petal reverses, fading through rose to lilac-pink as they age. The compact, exceptionally disease-resistant plant blooms continuously on dense upright growth.

Rosa 'Queen Elizabeth' is a medium-pink grandiflora rose bred by American breeder Dr. Walter Lammerts in 1954, a cross between 'Charlotte Armstrong' and 'Floradora'. Introduced to mark the 1952 coronation of Queen Elizabeth II, its tall stately habit and classic high-centred pink blooms prompted the creation of the entirely new grandiflora class.

Rosa 'Queen of Sweden' is a soft apricot-blush English shrub rose bred by David Austin in 2004, named to celebrate the 350th anniversary of the 1654 treaty between Sweden and England. Upright neat habit with perfectly formed cupped flowers of pale apricot-pink makes it one of the most elegant Austin roses for formal mixed-border plantings.

Rosa 'Rainbow Knock Out' is a bicoloured coral-pink-and-yellow shrub rose released in 2007 by William Radler and Conard-Pyle, the fourth release in the revolutionary Knock Out series. Single blooms have yellow centres flushing outward into coral-pink petal tips, giving a rainbow effect, and the cultivar shares the series' legendary disease resistance.

Rosa 'Rainbow's End' is a bicoloured yellow-and-red miniature rose bred by American breeder Harmon Saville in 1984, one of the most celebrated miniature roses ever introduced. Each tiny high-centred bloom opens yellow with red petal edges that spread inward with sun exposure, so bushes carry flowers in every colour transition simultaneously. It won the ARS Award of Excellence in 1986.

Rosa 'Sally Holmes' is a single-flowered shrub rose bred by English amateur Robert Holmes in 1976 and named for his wife, a cross between 'Ivory Fashion' and 'Ballerina'. Enormous corymbs of up to forty large single creamy-white blooms with pink flushes cover the arching plant continuously, and it was voted into the World Federation of Rose Societies Hall of Fame in 2012.

Rosa 'Savoy Hotel' is a light pink hybrid tea rose bred by Pat Dickson in Northern Ireland in 1987, named for the London hotel's centenary. Classic high-centred soft pink blooms with a stronger rose pink reverse bloom almost continuously on upright vigorous plants, and the cultivar remains one of the best-selling pink hybrid teas in British gardens.

Rosa 'Scarlet Meidiland' is a bright cherry-scarlet groundcover shrub rose bred by Marie-Louise Meilland at the Meilland nursery near Antibes, France, released in 1987 as part of the pioneering Meidiland landscape series.

Rosa 'Scentimental' is a striped burgundy-and-cream floribunda rose bred by American hybridizer Tom Carruth at Weeks Roses in Upland, California, released in 1996 from a cross between 'Playboy' and 'Peppermint Twist'.

Rosa 'Sexy Rexy' is a medium-pink floribunda bred by New Zealand's Sam McGredy IV in 1984, a cross of 'Seaspray' and 'Dreaming'. Huge sprays of up to thirty flat camellia-like blooms per cluster made it one of the most awarded floribundas, including the World Rose Hall of Fame in 2006.

Rosa 'Sheer Bliss' is a pale pink-and-white hybrid tea rose bred by American breeder William Warriner at Jackson & Perkins in 1985, a cross between 'White Masterpiece' and 'Grand Masterpiece'. It won the All-America Rose Selections award in 1987 for its exceptionally long spiralled buds and delicate spicy fragrance with hints of clove.

Rosa 'Showbiz' is a bright medium-red floribunda rose bred by German breeder Mathias Tantau Jr. in 1981. Huge clusters of up to thirty glowing scarlet blooms cover the compact plant continuously, and exceptional disease resistance made it an immediate success. It won the All-America Rose Selections award in 1985.

Rosa 'Simplicity' is a medium pink floribunda rose bred by William Warriner at Jackson & Perkins in 1978. Marketed as the Living Fence rose, its profusion of clear pink blooms, easy care, and ability to form dense hedges from bare-root plantings made it one of the best-selling roses of the 1980s in North America.

Rosa 'Souvenir de la Malmaison' is a blush-pink Bourbon rose bred by French breeder Jean Beluze in 1843, named to honour Empress Josephine's legendary rose garden at Château de Malmaison which had been destroyed by that date. Perfectly quartered pale blush blooms with a rich tea fragrance made it one of the most cherished old roses of the Victorian era.

Rosa 'St. Patrick' is an unusual yellow hybrid tea rose bred by Texan breeder Frank Strickland in 1996 with a distinctive green cast on the outer petals, most pronounced in hot sunny weather. A cross between 'Brandy' and 'Gold Medal', it won the All-America Rose Selections award in 1996 for its heat tolerance and unique emerald-tinged golden blooms.

Rosa 'Sterling Silver' is a lavender hybrid tea rose bred by Gladys Fisher in California in 1957, one of the first commercially successful mauve roses. Though moderately vigorous, its unusual silvery-lilac colour and strong citrus fragrance made it an enduring sentimental favourite, and it appears in the pedigrees of most modern lavender rose cultivars.

Rosa 'Sun Sprinkles' is a bright yellow miniature rose bred by Keith Zary at Jackson & Perkins in 1999, one of the rare miniature roses to win the All-America Rose Selections award. Deep clear yellow blooms with good fragrance, exceptional disease resistance, and compact vigorous growth made it a landmark in modern miniature rose breeding.

Rosa 'Sunny Knock Out' is a bright yellow shrub rose released in 2008 by William Radler and Conard-Pyle, extending the Knock Out series into true yellow. Single blooms open clear lemon-yellow and fade to near white, have a stronger sweet fragrance than siblings, and retain the series' hallmark disease resistance and continuous bloom.

Rosa 'Sunsprite' (German: 'Friesia') is a bright yellow floribunda rose bred by Reimer Kordes at W. Kordes' Söhne in Sparrieshoop, Germany, released in 1973 from a cross between 'Friedrich Wörlein' and 'Spanish Sun'.

Rosa 'Tea Clipper' is an apricot-yellow English shrub rose bred by David Austin in 2006, named for the 19th-century clipper ships that raced China tea to London. Cupped apricot-yellow blooms carry a strong fruity scent with myrrh hints on a tall upright disease-resistant plant.

Rosa 'The Generous Gardener' is a soft blush-pink English shrub rose bred by David Austin in 2002, trained by many gardeners as a climber to four metres. The perfectly formed cupped pale pink blooms show a creamy heart and reveal golden stamens as they open, with a delicious myrrh-and-musk fragrance praised as among the finest Austin has produced.

Rosa 'Tiffany' is a two-toned pink and yellow hybrid tea rose bred by American breeder Robert Lindquist in 1954, a cross between 'Charlotte Armstrong' and 'Girona'. Released by Conard-Pyle, it won the All-America Rose Selections award in 1955 and the James Alexander Gamble Fragrance Medal in 1962 for its strong fruity scent.

Rosa 'Tournament of Roses' is a coral-pink grandiflora rose bred by William Warriner at Jackson & Perkins in 1988, named for the Pasadena, California New Year's parade. Classic high-centred warm pink blooms borne in small clusters on upright plants won the All-America Rose Selections award in 1989, the centennial year of the Tournament of Roses Parade.

Rosa 'Tropicana' is a vivid orange-red hybrid tea rose bred by German breeder Mathias Tantau Jr. in 1960, the first modern rose to display true fluorescent coral-orange colour. Released in Europe as 'Super Star' and in North America as 'Tropicana', it won the AARS award in 1963 and revolutionized breeding goals for vibrant colour.

Rosa 'Veterans Honor' is a deep dark red hybrid tea rose bred by Keith Zary at Jackson & Perkins in 1999. Selected by American military veterans' groups as the official rose of veterans' tribute gardens, it produces long-stemmed velvety crimson blooms with excellent vase life and a light raspberry fragrance, and a portion of sales supports veterans' charities.

Rosa 'Wild Blue Yonder' is a lavender-purple grandiflora rose bred by Tom Carruth at Weeks Roses in 2004, one of the most deeply purple modern roses. Ruffled mauve-purple blooms with a strong fruity citrus fragrance won the All-America Rose Selections award in 2006 and the James Alexander Gamble Fragrance Medal in 2013.

Rosa 'Zephirine Drouhin' is a thornless Bourbon climbing rose bred in France by Bizot and released in 1868, one of the most beloved old roses still in commerce. The loosely double cerise-pink blooms carry a powerful damask fragrance, and the cultivar's famously thornless canes make it a favourite for archways, walls, and places where roses will be brushed against.

Lady Banks' rose is native to central and western China. R. Brown described it in 1821, named for Lady Dorothea Banks, wife of Sir Joseph Banks. A thornless evergreen rambler, the world's largest rose—a single specimen in Tombstone, Arizona—covers 743 square meters and has bloomed annually since 1885.

The China Rose revolutionized Western gardening in the late 18th century, introducing the then-unknown trait of repeat blooming. Crosses with European once-flowering roses produced nearly all modern garden roses, including hybrid teas.

The Damask Rose is a hybrid of ancient origin, cultivated for rose oil since antiquity. Bulgarian Rose Valley and Turkish Isparta are the two main rose oil production centers, requiring about 4,000 kg of petals per liter of oil.

The Apothecary's Rose is one of the oldest cultivated roses in Europe, grown from Roman times. Its dried petals retain fragrance better than almost any other rose, making it the foundation of medieval herbal medicine and the rose-water trade.

Rosa gallica 'Officinalis' is the oldest named rose cultivar in Western horticulture, cultivated continuously since Greco-Roman times. Grown on industrial scale at Provins, France, since the 1200s for rose oil and rosewater. Reputed to be the Red Rose of Lancaster.

Redleaf rose is native to mountains of central and southern Europe. Pourret described it in 1788. Formerly known as Rosa rubrifolia, it is the rare rose grown more for foliage than flowers—its grape-purple-glaucous leaves and crimson rose hips give long-season garden interest and make it a favorite of flower arrangers.

Rosa multiflora is native to eastern Asia — Japan, Korea, and eastern China — where it grows as a sprawling shrub rose. It was introduced to North America in the 1860s as a rootstock for grafted hybrid tea roses, and became the standard rootstock of the global commercial rose industry for over a.

The Rugosa Rose is native to coastal northeast Asia — Japan, Korea, and Russian Far East — where it grows in beach dunes. Exceptionally cold-hardy and salt-tolerant, it has naturalized on beaches across northern Europe and North America.

Rosa virginiana occurs naturally along eastern North America from Newfoundland to Alabama and west to Ontario and Missouri, typically on coastal dunes, sandy woodland edges, and open fields. Scottish gardener Philip Miller described it in the 1768 edition of his Gardeners Dictionary.

Memorial rose is native to Japan, Korea, and eastern China. Crépin described it in 1886, named for Swiss botanist Max Wichura. Its trailing habit and glossy foliage made it the parent of many early 20th-century rambler hybrids including 'Dorothy Perkins' and 'American Pillar'.

Rosmarinus officinalis — now accepted as Salvia rosmarinus following molecular phylogenetic revision of Lamiaceae, though horticulture retains the older name — is native to the Mediterranean coast, with its name derived from Latin "ros marinus" ("dew of the sea") for its habit of growing.

Rubus armeniacus, Himalayan blackberry (despite originating in the Caucasus, not the Himalayas), is a biennial-caned bramble native to Armenia and northern Iran. German botanist Karl Koch formally described it in 1835 from European garden plants.

The common blackberry is a European species complex of hundreds of microspecies (apomicts) native to Europe, northern Africa, and western Asia. Biblical tradition identifies it as the "burning bush" that spoke to Moses, and its medicinal use is documented from classical antiquity.

The red raspberry is native to cool-temperate Eurasia and takes its specific name from Mount Ida in Turkey, where classical Greek sources reported its abundance. Medieval Europeans associated it with the Virgin Mary, and its cultivation dates to the Crusades.

Black raspberry is an eastern North American bramble whose fruit Indigenous peoples harvested for food, dye, and medicine. European settlers adopted it quickly, and 19th-century American breeders developed it into a distinct commercial crop separate from red raspberries.

Salmonberry is a Pacific Northwest bramble whose salmon-colored berries ripened alongside spawning salmon runs, marking an important seasonal food for coastal Indigenous peoples. Lewis and Clark and David Douglas both documented it during their expeditions.

Black-eyed Susan is a North American prairie biennial named by Linnaeus for Swedish botanist Olaus Rudbeck. Adopted as the state flower of Maryland in 1918, its bright yellow rays have made it a cottage-garden staple and a keystone nectar plant for native pollinators.

Cutleaf coneflower is native to eastern North America and was used medicinally by Cherokee and other tribes. Linnaeus named the genus in 1753 honoring botanists Olaus Rudbeck father and son. The double 'Golden Glow' has been in cultivation since 1894.

Native to tallgrass prairies from Louisiana to Wisconsin, Rudbeckia subtomentosa was formally described by Frederick Pursh in 1813. Its common name references the anise-like scent released when foliage is crushed.

Brown-eyed Susan is a short-lived perennial native to eastern North America. Described by Linnaeus in 1753, it was widely naturalized in 19th-century meadows and prairie gardens, prized for prolific late-summer bloom.

Mexican petunia, an aggressive perennial of tropical America, has colonized US southern landscapes with purple trumpet flowers since the early 1900s. Now reclassified as Ruellia simplex and listed as invasive in Florida, it is largely replaced by sterile cultivars.

Baja ruellia, or desert ruellia, is a compact evergreen shrub of Baja California and northwestern Sonora. Its violet trumpet flowers and extreme drought tolerance have made it a staple of Phoenix and Tucson xeriscape landscapes.

Curly dock is a deep-rooted perennial native to Europe, North Africa, and western Asia, now a cosmopolitan weed. Its young leaves have been eaten as a potherb since Neolithic times, and seeds provided famine food. Herbalists use the yellow taproot as a gentle laxative despite its oxalate content.

Rumex obtusifolius, broad-leaved dock, is a vigorous perennial native to Europe, famous as the traditional antidote rubbed on nettle stings. Its deep taproot makes it a persistent weed of pastures and gardens, and its seeds remain viable in soil for decades. Livestock generally avoid it because of bitter oxalates.

Common rue or herb-of-grace is a southeastern European and Balkan native. Linnaeus described it in Species Plantarum 1753. Evergreen subshrub 60-90 cm with divided blue-green foliage and yellow flowers. Used medicinally since antiquity. Causes severe phytophotodermatitis. Host plant for swallowtail butterflies.

Dwarf palmetto is a cold-hardy fan palm of southeastern United States bottomlands and northeastern Mexico, with populations in Arkansas and Oklahoma tolerating subzero winters. Mark Catesby illustrated it in the 1730s, and today it is cultivated as the most reliable trunkless palm for temperate gardens, thriving as far north as Washington DC and Long Island.

Bird's-eye pearlwort is a tiny mat-forming Eurasian perennial described by Linnaeus in 1753. A common weed of moist lawns, paving cracks, and gravel paths, it is sometimes called 'Irish moss' in the nursery trade though it is not a moss nor the same as Sagina subulata.

Irish moss (also called pearlwort) is native to western Europe, from Iceland and the British Isles south through Iberia. A low mossy-looking mat of fine needle-like leaves with tiny white summer flowers, it is widely used between stepping stones and as a lawn substitute in small-scale garden settings.

Broadleaf arrowhead (also called wapato, duck potato, or Indian potato) is native to wetlands across North America and parts of Central America. Its starchy underwater tubers were one of the most important plant foods of Indigenous Northwest Coast, Columbia Plateau, and Eastern Woodlands peoples — harvested by women who waded into waist-deep water and dislodged tubers with their feet.

White willow was the original botanical source of salicylic acid — isolated from its bark in 1828 by Johann Buchner and later chemically modified to produce acetylsalicylic acid (aspirin) by Bayer chemist Felix Hoffmann in 1897. Willow bark's analgesic use dates to Egyptian and Greek antiquity.

Weeping willow is native to northern China despite the misleading name — Linnaeus gave the specific epithet "babylonica" based on Psalm 137 ("By the rivers of Babylon... we hung our harps upon the willows"), though the biblical tree was likely Euphrates poplar rather than willow.

Painted tongue is native to southern Chile. Cool-season bedding annual producing spectacular trumpet-shaped flowers with dramatic contrasting veining in rich velvety colors — gold-veined mahogany, yellow-veined purple, cream-veined crimson — unlike any other bedding plant.

White sage is native to coastal sage scrub of Southern California and northern Baja California, ceremonially sacred to Indigenous peoples including the Chumash, Tongva, and Kumeyaay. Commercial smudge-stick overharvest has caused wild decline; Indigenous nations ask consumers to stop buying it.

Prairie sage, or azure blue sage, is a tall late-season perennial of the central and southern US Great Plains producing spires of pure sky-blue flowers. It was described in the 19th century and remains an iconic native wildflower of autumn prairies.

Cleveland sage is native to the chaparral of San Diego County and northern Baja California. Among the most intensely fragrant of all American sages — the powerful resinous-herbal scent of its gray foliage carries on hot summer afternoons in a manner unmatched by other salvias. The parent of popular garden cultivars "Allen Chickering," "Winnifred Gilman," and "Pozo Blue."

Scarlet sage (blood sage, tropical sage) is native to the American Southeast, Mexico, Central America, and northern South America. Tender tropical-origin salvia widely grown as a summer annual worldwide for its bright scarlet tubular flowers and exceptional attraction of hummingbirds and butterflies throughout the warm season.

Pineapple sage is a Mexican and Guatemalan native in the mint family. M. Vahl described it in 1794. Semi-evergreen shrubby perennial 1-1.5 meters tall with pineapple-scented leaves and brilliant scarlet-red tubular flowers in late summer and fall. Hummingbird favorite.

Mealycup sage is a Texan and Mexican perennial often grown as an annual for its tall spikes of violet-blue flowers and silvery mealy-dusted bracts. Its Victoria and Sallyfun cultivars remain the industry standard for dependable blue summer bedding.

Salvia greggii, the autumn sage or cherry sage, is a woody-based semi-evergreen perennial native to rocky slopes and canyons of the Chihuahuan Desert region from west Texas and southern New Mexico through the Sierra Madre Oriental of Mexico to San Luis Potosí.

Anise-scented sage (hummingbird sage) is native to Paraguay, northeastern Argentina, Uruguay, and southern Brazil. The classic intense-blue salvia of the American summer garden — "Black and Blue" cultivar with its near-black calyces and electric cobalt-blue flowers is among the most widely-grown hummingbird garden plants in the eastern US.

Mexican bush sage is native to central and eastern Mexico. Signature of the late-summer and autumn garden — spectacular tall spires of velvety-white-and-violet two-toned flowers bloom from late August through hard frost. Among the most widely-planted ornamental salvias in California, Florida, Texas, and Mediterranean Europe.

Salvia leucophylla is native to coastal sage scrub communities of Southern California — from San Luis Obispo County south through Santa Barbara, Ventura, and Los Angeles Counties to Orange and San Diego Counties — and into northern Baja California, Mexico.

Canyon sage is a compact aromatic shrublet of west Texas, New Mexico, and northern Mexico, bearing deep blue to violet flowers across a very long season. It is prized in southwestern xeriscape design for its drought tolerance and pollinator value.

Mountain sage, also called little-leaf or blackcurrant sage, is a Mexican perennial whose crushed foliage releases a fruity currant aroma. Its endless cultivar parade includes the famous Hot Lips bicolor, discovered at Strybing Arboretum in San Francisco.

Woodland sage is a Eurasian perennial ranging from central Europe to western Asia. Bred intensively by German nurseries including Foerster and zur Linden in the mid-20th century, it produced the violet-blue "Mainacht" and "Caradonna" cultivars now essential to the modern prairie-style garden.

Garden sage is native to the Mediterranean and has been a medicinal herb since antiquity — the name "salvia" derives from Latin "salvare," to save or heal. Central to Italian cuisine (saltimbocca), Thanksgiving stuffing tradition in America, and among the most-studied culinary herbs.

Gentian sage is native to the central Mexican highlands. Introduced to European horticulture in 1838 — its extraordinary pure-cobalt-blue flowers created an immediate horticultural sensation, as true blue in this intensity is exceptionally rare in garden flowers. Modern cultivar "Cambridge Blue" is one of the most widely-grown pale-blue garden salvias.

Meadow clary is native to European meadows. Described by Linnaeus in 1753, it has pollination biology famous in evolutionary biology—Charles Darwin's 1862 studies of its hinged stamens demonstrated key principles of co-evolution with bumblebees.

Mountain sage, or royal sage, is a tall autumn-blooming sage of the Chisos Mountains of Texas and northeastern Mexico. Its brilliant scarlet tubular flowers attract migrating hummingbirds and helped inspire the Plant Select program for southwestern gardens.

Salvia sclarea is native to the northern Mediterranean basin and Central Asia. The Latin epithet sclarea derives from clarus (clear), referencing medieval European use of seed mucilage as an eyewash to remove foreign particles—hence the common name clary or clear-eye.

Salvia splendens is native to the Atlantic forests of southeastern Brazil. It reached European horticulture around 1822, and by mid-century it was a mainstay of the carpet-bedding movement in England, France, and Germany, where massed scarlet rows were used to spell out words or produce.

Texas sage is a short-lived perennial of calcareous soils from central Texas into northern Mexico, producing deep blue to violet flowers in spring. Its restrained stature and drought tolerance make it well suited to rock gardens and Edwards Plateau landscapes.

Lilac sage is native to central and southern Europe and western Asia. Linnaeus described it in 1753. The cultivar 'Purple Rain' introduced by Piet Oudolf in the 1990s made it one of the signature plants of the New Perennial movement.

Wood sage is a naturally occurring hybrid of S. pratensis and S. nemorosa across central Europe. Ernst Pagels's breeding in the 1950s-70s produced iconic cultivars 'Mainacht' (May Night), 'Blauhügel', and 'Ostfriesland' that transformed perennial borders worldwide.

Floating fern (also called water moss) is native to Europe, Asia, and Africa — a small floating aquatic fern with pairs of oval water-surface leaves and a third deeply divided underwater leaf. The related Salvinia molesta has become one of the world's worst invasive aquatic weeds.

Sambucus nigra has been central to European folk medicine for over 2000 years. Hippocrates called it his 'medicine chest.' Elderflowers are the basis of cordial and St-Germain liqueur; elderberry syrup has clinically validated antiviral effects against influenza.

'Black Lace' is a near-black-foliage cultivar of European elder bred at East Malling Research Station in England and released in 2003. It revolutionized elderberry as an ornamental plant and became one of the most widely planted dark-leaved specimen shrubs of the 2010s.

Cylindrical snake plant is native to Angola and southern Africa. Used traditionally for bowstring fiber, it entered European horticulture in the 19th century and became a defining low-maintenance houseplant — now reclassified into Dracaena under modern phylogenetic taxonomy.

Whale fin or shark fin sansevieria is a central African native now classified as Dracaena masoniana following Takawira-Nyenya et al. 2018. Described by Chahinian in 2000 honoring Maurice Mason. Features massive single paddle-shaped green leaves with dark cross-banding.

Sansevieria trifasciata was first described in 1794 and named after Raimondo di Sangro, an 18th-century Italian prince. Molecular studies reclassified it as Dracaena trifasciata in 2017, though the older name remains commercially dominant.

Sansevieria zeylanica, Ceylon bowstring hemp, is an evergreen rhizomatous succulent in the asparagus family (Asparagaceae) native to Sri Lanka (formerly Ceylon, from which the epithet zeylanica derives) and the southeastern Indian Deccan Peninsula.

Sanvitalia procumbens is native to Mexico and adjacent southwestern United States (southern Arizona and New Mexico), growing in disturbed open ground, grassland edges, and roadsides. The genus was named for the noble Italian Sanvitali family (of Parma).

Sweet box is of uncertain wild origin (confusa means "confused") — likely a long-cultivated Chinese form. Sealy described it in 1949. A compact evergreen shrub for shade, its tiny cream flowers in midwinter carry a powerful vanilla-jasmine fragrance that can scent an entire garden on mild days.

Himalayan sweet box is native to the eastern Himalayas and western China. Baillon described it in 1871, naming it for Joseph Dalton Hooker, the great Himalayan explorer. The low-spreading var. humilis makes an excellent fragrant evergreen groundcover for shade, also blooming powerfully in winter.

Summer savory is an ancient Mediterranean herb, used by Romans as a sex-stimulant herb dedicated to the lustful satyrs (hence the genus name). Central to Bulgarian and Romanian cuisines as "chubritsa" and "cimbru," and a traditional seasoning for beans across southern Europe.

Fan flower (fairy fan-flower) is native to southern and eastern coastal Australia. Distinctive half-circle fan-shaped flowers with five petals spread on one side only give the plant its common and scientific names — 'scaevola' means 'left-handed' in Latin. A heat- and drought-tolerant trailing summer container plant now grown worldwide.

Fan flower is an Australian trailing perennial bearing fan-shaped one-sided blue-violet flowers. The aboriginal Dreamtime holds that an angry ancestor broke each flower in half, leaving fan-shaped blossoms. Proven Winners popularized it worldwide through the Surdiva series.

The Umbrella Tree is native to northeastern Australia and New Guinea. A major invasive weed in Florida and Hawaii, it is also one of the longest-running large-leafed 'indoor trees' in Western horticulture.

Schefflera arboricola is native to Taiwan and the southern Chinese provinces of Hainan and Guangdong, where it grows as a multi-stemmed evergreen shrub in the forest understory. It entered Western horticulture in the mid-20th century and quickly replaced its larger cousin S.

Five-flavor berry is native to northeastern China, Korea, Japan, and the Russian Far East. Turcz. described it in 1832. Its Chinese name wu wei zi (五味子) means five-flavor berry—the dried fruit tastes sweet, sour, bitter, salty, and pungent at once. A pillar of traditional Chinese medicine as an adaptogen for over 2000 years.

Schlumbergera truncata is native to a small area of the Serra dos Órgãos mountains in southeast Brazil, where it grows as an epiphytic cactus on mossy tree branches in cool cloud forest between 700 and 2000 meters elevation.

Japanese umbrella pine is the sole surviving species of an ancient lineage dating to the Triassic, 230 million years ago. Siebold and Zuccarini described it in 1842. Sacred in Japan as one of the five Kiso sacred trees, amber from extinct Sciadopitys relatives is the source of much Baltic amber.

Two-leaf squill is a Eurasian native described by Linnaeus in 1753, common across European mountain woodlands. With two strap-shaped leaves per bulb and sky-blue star flowers, it has been cultivated as a cottage-garden bulb since at least the 16th century.

Mishchenko squill is native to northern Iran and the Caucasus. Described in 1928 by Soviet botanist Grossheim, its nearly-white pale blue flowers emerge just after snowmelt, making it one of the earliest and most subtle of all spring bulbs.

Siberian squill, native to southern Russia, Ukraine, Turkey, and the Caucasus (despite the name), was described by Haworth in 1804. Its piercing gentian-blue nodding bells are one of the earliest and most reliable naturalizing spring bulbs in cold climates.

Biting stonecrop ("wall-pepper") is a European sedum so widely naturalized that it occurs on nearly every continent. Its peppery-biting leaves gave it medieval use as a famine food and a burn remedy; modern phytochemistry identifies alkaloids responsible for the taste.

White stonecrop is a Eurasian sedum dominating traditional European sod roofs for centuries. Since the 1980s it has been a backbone species of the modern extensive green-roof industry pioneered in Germany, now used on hundreds of thousands of rooftops worldwide.

Kamtschatka stonecrop is native to the Russian Far East (Kamchatka), northeastern China, Korea, and Japan. A tough low-growing succulent with yellow-orange summer flowers, it is widely used as a drought-tolerant groundcover and is a common component of modern extensive green-roof plantings.

Burro's Tail is native to a few cliff locations in southern Mexico's Veracruz state. Discovered in cultivation before being found in the wild, its exact native habitat was a mystery for decades after it entered the horticultural trade.

Showy stonecrop (now Hylotelephium spectabile) is native to northeastern China and Korea, introduced to Western gardens in the 19th century. The cultivar "Autumn Joy" ("Herbstfreude") by Georg Arends in 1955 became one of the best-selling perennials in horticultural history.

Sedum spurium (recently reclassified to Phedimus spurius by botanists splitting the genus, though horticulture retains the traditional name) is native to the Caucasus, northern Turkey, and northern Iran, growing in rocky meadows and cliff ledges.

Sedum telephium (now often placed in Hylotelephium) grows wild across Europe and northern Asia. The specific epithet refers to Telephus, mythical king of Mysia and son of Hercules, whose wound was said to be healed by this plant's poultice.

Jelly bean plant is a hybrid of disputed Mexican origin, probably between S. stahlii and S. pachyphyllum, widely grown as a beginner succulent. Its fat green-red leaves make it instantly recognizable and one of the most-propagated cuttings in the global succulent trade.

Krauss's spikemoss (also called "trailing spikemoss" and sold as "frosty fern" in some trade contexts) is native to southern and eastern Africa and the Azores. A moss-like clubmoss relative, it is widely used as a terrarium and houseplant ground cover. Now a serious invasive in New Zealand's beech forests.

Cobweb houseleek is an alpine European species whose dense white webbing between leaf tips — functioning as frost protection — gives it a uniquely furry appearance. Grown on medieval roofs alongside S. tectorum, it remains a signature rock-garden plant.

Common houseleek was planted on Roman and medieval European roofs in the belief it protected against lightning and fire — a custom Charlemagne made law in an 8th-century capitulary. The species name tectorum literally means "of roofs."

Dusty miller is the classic Victorian silver-leaved bedding foliage, now correctly named Jacobaea maritima. Its woolly white felted leaves accent almost every carpet-bedding scheme since 19th-century Paris made it fashionable in public parks.

Blue chalk sticks is a South African succulent popularized as a California groundcover in the mid-20th century. Its upright blue-grey finger leaves form eye-catching mass plantings and made it a signature plant of mid-century modern succulent landscaping.

String of Pearls is native to the arid regions of the Eastern Cape and Namibia. Its spherical pea-like leaves, evolved to reduce water loss, made it a viral houseplant hit in the 2010s. Moderately toxic to pets.

Blue chalksticks is a South African succulent with finger-like blue-glaucous leaves. Widely adopted in California and Mediterranean dry gardens as a groundcover, it took its modern role as a blue-foliage design accent in drought-tolerant landscaping.

Senecio vulgaris is native to Europe and western Asia. Linnaeus described it in Species Plantarum (1753). For centuries European folk herbalists used decoctions for wound poultices, menstrual regulation, and general 'women's complaints', reflected in old names like 'sention' and 'simson'.

Sequoia sempervirens is native to a narrow coastal fog-belt strip from southwestern Oregon (just north of Brookings, Curry County) south through the California coast to southern Monterey County — essentially the western-facing slopes that receive regular summer coastal fog.

Giant sequoia is native to a narrow band of the western Sierra Nevada in California. The most massive single-stem trees on Earth by volume — "General Sherman" measures 1,487 cubic meters. Trees have been dated at 3,200+ years, and around 75 distinct native groves survive in the Sierra Nevada.

Saw palmetto is a low-growing fan palm dominating southeastern United States pine flatwoods. Indigenous peoples used the fruit for food and medicine, and modern pharmaceutical extracts from its berries are a widely studied prostate-health supplement worldwide.

Snow rose (Chinese "liù yuè xuě" 六月雪, "June snow") is native to southeastern China, Taiwan, and southern Japan. A small-leaved shrub producing clouds of tiny white funnel-shaped flowers in early summer — one of the most important traditional bonsai subjects in Chinese penjing and Japanese bonsai, particularly favored for saikei (landscape) compositions.

Giant foxtail is a summer-annual grass native to China, introduced to the US around 1925 via contaminated millet seed imports from Japan. Named for German missionary Ernst Faber, it became one of the 10 worst weeds of US Midwestern corn and soybean agriculture.

Green foxtail is a cool-season Eurasian annual grass, the wild ancestor of cultivated foxtail millet (S. italica) domesticated in northern China about 8,000 years ago. Described by Linnaeus in 1753, it is now a common weed in summer annual crops worldwide.

Checkerbloom (dwarf checkerbloom) is native to California and southwestern Oregon coastal grasslands. A small mallow-family perennial producing pink-to-magenta hollyhock-like flowers 3-4 cm across on 30-60 cm stems in spring. A classic component of California coastal meadow and native-grass prairie plantings.

Silybum marianum, milk thistle, is a tall annual or biennial native to the Mediterranean whose marbled leaves and purple flowerheads made it a classic Old World medicinal plant. Since Roman times its seeds have been used to protect the liver, and modern research has isolated silymarin, a flavonoid complex now widely sold as a hepatic supplement.

Blue-eyed grass is native to eastern North America. Despite the common name "grass" it is not a grass but a small member of the iris family (Iridaceae), producing small bright sky-blue six-petaled flowers with yellow centers on grass-like fans of foliage.

Japanese skimmia is native to Japan, Korea, and China. Thunberg described it in 1784. Both male and female plants are needed for fruit, but the fragrant male flower clusters and the red berries on female plants, all borne on compact evergreen shrubs, make it a four-season performer in shaded gardens.

Solandra maxima is native to the rainforests of Mexico and Central America (Guatemala, Honduras, Nicaragua, Costa Rica). It is an enormous woody vine — capable of climbing 10-15 meters into rainforest canopy in its native habitat and of overwhelming trellises and pergolas in cultivation — producing.

Tamarillo or tree tomato is a South American Andean native. Cavanilles described it as Solanum betaceum in 1799. Small 3-4 meter tree producing distinctive egg-shaped red, orange, or yellow edible fruits with tart tangy flavor. Major commercial crop in New Zealand.

Carolina horsenettle is a native US southeastern perennial, described by Linnaeus in 1753. Despite being in the genus with tomatoes and potatoes, it is one of the most problematic and toxic agricultural weeds, with sharp stem spines and poisonous yellow fruit.

Chilean potato vine is native to Chile and Peru. Ruiz and Pavón described it in 1799. Clusters of purple-blue flowers with yellow anthers resemble its potato relatives and bloom throughout summer. The cultivar 'Glasnevin' was found at Dublin's Glasnevin Botanic Garden and is hardy to -10°C.

The tomato is native to western South America and was domesticated in Mexico before Spanish conquest. Introduced to Europe in the 1540s as a curiosity and often feared as poisonous (due to Solanaceae association), it became a fundamental Italian and Mediterranean ingredient only by the 18th-19th centuries.

Eggplant is native to South and Southeast Asia and was domesticated in India over 4,000 years ago. Carried to the Mediterranean by Arab traders in the 7th-8th centuries, it became central to Middle Eastern, Italian (melanzane), and Turkish cuisines — with over 1,000 named cultivars.

Black nightshade is a species complex of likely Eurasian origin, described by Linnaeus in 1753. Despite the family's deadly nightshade reputation, ripe fruit is eaten worldwide when fully mature; unripe berries and foliage contain toxic solanine and related alkaloids.

Solanum tuberosum was domesticated in the Andes of Peru and Bolivia at least 7,000 years ago from wild tuber-bearing Solanum species. Highland Andean cultures cultivated thousands of distinct cultivars at various altitudes and developed freeze-drying preservation (chuño).

Canada goldenrod is native to North America and has become one of the most serious invasive plants of European grasslands, Japanese meadows, and Chinese farmland. Unfairly blamed for hay fever since the 19th century — goldenrod is insect-pollinated and has heavy non-airborne pollen; the true culprit is co-flowering ragweed (Ambrosia).

Rough-stemmed goldenrod is a widespread eastern North American native, used by the Iroquois and Cherokee for kidney and liver complaints. It gained wide horticultural fame through the cultivar 'Fireworks', introduced by the North Carolina Botanical Garden in 1993.

Solidago sphacelata, autumn or false goldenrod, inhabits limestone glades, river bluffs, and open woodlands across Kentucky, Tennessee, and neighboring states. Rafinesque published the species in 1820.

Field or perennial sow thistle is a deep-rooted perennial native to Europe and western Asia. Unlike its annual relatives it spreads by creeping rhizomes as well as seed, making it a persistent weed of grain crops across the Northern Hemisphere. Its golden flowerheads are rich in nectar.

Sonchus asper, prickly sow thistle, is an annual native to Europe and western Asia, now a cosmopolitan weed. Its young leaves have long been eaten as salad greens and boiled potherbs around the Mediterranean. The hollow stems exude milky latex when broken, and the dandelion-like flowers close by midday.

Common sow thistle, native to Europe and western Asia, is one of the world's most widespread weeds and simultaneously a valued leafy vegetable. Linnaeus described it in 1753. Its milky sap was eaten by Greek runners preparing for Olympic games as a traditional stimulant.

Korean mountain ash is native to East Asia. Described by Koch as Crataegus alnifolia in 1856, later transferred to Sorbus. The simple (not pinnate) alder-like leaves distinguish it from most Sorbus species. Introduced to the West by Sargent around 1892, it is valued for spring flowers, fall color, and crimson fruit.

Sorbus americana grows in cool, moist soils across eastern Canada from Newfoundland to Manitoba and south in the Appalachians to Georgia, typically in boreal and mixed northern hardwood forests. American botanist Humphry Marshall described the species in 1785 in his Arbustrum Americanum.

European rowan (also called mountain ash) is native across Europe and western Asia. In Celtic, Norse, and northern European folklore it is a protective tree planted beside houses to repel witchcraft, and its bright red autumn berries are a traditional food for thrushes and, after processing, for humans (rowan jelly).

Slender Indian grass is a southeastern US native grass. C. Mohr described it in 1878 honoring naturalist Stephen Elliott. Warm-season clumping grass 90-120 cm tall with distinctive bronze-amber terminal panicles in late summer. Less aggressive than the better-known Sorghastrum nutans.

The Peace Lily was introduced to Europe in the 1870s by German plant collector Gustav Wallis, after whom it is named. Its white spathe was adopted as a symbol of peace and sympathy in 20th-century Western floristry.

Spinach originated in ancient Persia (modern Iran) and was introduced to China in the 7th century as "Persian vegetable" and to Europe by Moorish Spain around the 12th century. Catherine de Medici's 16th-century fondness gave "Florentine" its spinach meaning, and Popeye's 1930s cartoon launched its American myth.

Birchleaf spirea, native to northeast Asia and now understood to include North American populations, was described by Pallas in 1784. It is grown for compact mounded habit, white flower corymbs, and spectacular red-purple fall foliage.

Reeves's spirea, native to China, was introduced to the West by John Reeves of the East India Company in the 1820s. Named 'cantoniensis' after Canton (Guangzhou), it became one of the earliest Chinese shrubs grown in Victorian English gardens.

Japanese spiraea is native to Japan, Korea, and China, and was introduced to Western horticulture in the 1870s. Widely planted for its compact habit and summer pink flower clusters, it has become invasive in parts of the US Mid-Atlantic and Appalachian forests, where several states now restrict or prohibit its sale.

Nippon spirea is native to Shikoku Island, Japan. Maximowicz described it in 1866. Dense rounded foliage and arching sprays of white flowers in late spring have made it a staple of modern mixed borders. The cultivar 'Snowmound' (1969) is one of the most widely planted spireas worldwide.

Bridal wreath spirea, from central and eastern China and Korea, was sent to England in 1843 by Robert Fortune from a temple garden near Shanghai. Chinese gardeners had selected its fully double form centuries earlier, and its cascading arching branches of small white rosettes made it a Victorian symbol of spring weddings.

Thunberg spirea is native to China and Japan. Siebold ex Blume formalized the name in 1826, honoring Carl Peter Thunberg. Among the earliest spireas to bloom—its clouds of tiny white flowers open on bare stems in late March and April, a classic first-bloom-of-spring accent in temperate gardens.

Bumald spirea is a hybrid between Spiraea japonica and S. albiflora, raised in Europe around 1880. Named for Italian botanist Bumalda, it has become better known through its cultivar 'Anthony Waterer' (1890)—one of the most widely planted dwarf flowering shrubs in Western landscapes.

Stachys byzantina (formerly S. lanata or S. olympica) is native to the mountains of Turkey, the Caucasus, and Iran. The specific epithet byzantina references Byzantium (Constantinople), from where botanical specimens reached 18th-century European cultivation.

Stachys officinalis grows across European meadows and open woods. The species held extraordinary importance in Roman and medieval European medicine—Antonius Musa, personal physician to Emperor Augustus, reportedly authored a treatise listing 47 medicinal applications.

Stellaria media originated in Europe and has spread to every continent as a cool-season annual of gardens, lawns, and disturbed ground. Linnaeus described it as Alsine media in 1753, and Villars transferred it to Stellaria in 1789.

Madagascar Jasmine is endemic to Madagascar. Its intensely fragrant pure-white waxy trumpet flowers are a traditional component of Western wedding bouquets, earning it the name 'Bridal Wreath' or 'Bride's Flower'.

Stevia is native to Paraguay and Brazil, used by the Guaraní people for centuries to sweeten yerba mate. Commercial sweetener production emerged in 20th-century Japan after extraction of steviol glycosides; EU approval in 2011 launched a global zero-calorie sweetener industry.

Korean stewartia is native to mountains of South Korea. Described by Nakai in 1917, it produces camellia-like white flowers in summer and exceptional mottled exfoliating bark. Now often treated as S. pseudocamellia var. koreana, it is prized for its peeling patchwork trunk.

Tall stewartia is native to Japan's main islands. Described by Siebold and Zuccarini in 1846, it features smooth peeling cinnamon-red bark, smaller white flowers than S. pseudocamellia, and a more narrowly columnar habit suited to small gardens.

Mexican feather grass is native to Mexico, Texas, New Mexico, and parts of Argentina. Its extraordinarily fine, soft, silvery-green foliage that moves visibly in the slightest breeze made it one of the defining ornamental grasses of 21st-century naturalistic design — but it is also an emerging invasive in California, Australia, and parts of Europe. Taxonomically now known as Nassella tenuissima.

Strelitzia nicolai is native to the humid coastal forest and subtropical riverine habitats of South Africa's KwaZulu-Natal and Eastern Cape, extending into Mozambique, where it forms tree-like clumps up to 10 meters tall.

The Bird of Paradise is native to South Africa's Eastern Cape. Named in 1773 after Britain's Queen Charlotte, its electric orange-and-blue flowers made it the national cut flower of California, where it was formally designated the official flower of Los Angeles in 1952.

Native to the Brazilian Atlantic rainforest, Stromanthe sanguinea was a minor foliage plant until the 'Triostar' cultivar — with bold pink, cream, and green variegation — made it a mainstream houseplant hit in the 2000s.

Stromanthe 'Triostar' or tricolor ginger is a variegated form of Stromanthe thalia, a Brazilian rainforest native. Veloso described the species as Maranta thalia in 1829; L. Andersson & Engström transferred it to Stromanthe in 1988. 'Triostar' shows dramatic pink, cream, and green variegation with burgundy undersides.

Styphnolobium japonicum (formerly Sophora japonica) is native to China and Korea; the specific epithet japonicum is misleading as it reflects 18th-century European misunderstanding of Asian plant origins.

Styrax japonicus is native to Japan, Korea, and eastern China. English botanist Richard Oldham, working for Kew, collected specimens during his 1862 expedition to Japan before his untimely death in Amoy. The species reached American horticulture through the Arnold Arboretum in the late 19th century.

Fragrant snowbell is native to Japan, Korea, and northeast China. Introduced by Charles Maries in 1879, its large round leaves and drooping 8-inch racemes of intensely fragrant white bell flowers in late spring offer bolder texture and perfume than S. japonicus.

Bacopa, botanically Chaenostoma cordatum, is a South African trailing perennial with tiny white to lavender flowers. Proven Winners' Snowstorm Giant Snowflake release in the 1990s turned this obscure plant into a universal container basket staple worldwide.

Common snowberry is a North American shrub bearing clusters of waxy white berries that persist through winter. Indigenous peoples used it as soap and medicine, and Lewis and Clark introduced the plant to European gardeners through Thomas Jefferson.

Heath aster, native to North American prairies, was burned by the Lakota and Cheyenne as a smudging herb to drive away ghosts, while the Zuni chewed the root as a stimulant. Linnaeus described it in 1753 as Aster ericoides; Guy Nesom reclassified it into Symphyotrichum in 1994. It remains a favored late-season pollinator plant.

Symphyotrichum novae-angliae is the defining fall wildflower of the American Northeast and prairie. It is one of the most important late-season nectar plants for migrating monarch butterflies and native bees preparing for winter.

Aromatic aster is a compact North American native of rocky prairies, historically used by the Meskwaki for smudging. European botanists described it in 1818. It gained horticultural popularity after the 1980s with cultivars such as 'October Skies' and 'Raydon's Favorite'.

The Arrowhead Vine is native to Mexico, Central America, and northern South America. Widely naturalized as an invasive weed across tropical regions. Juvenile and adult leaves are dramatically different, confusing 19th-century botanists for decades.

Syringa meyeri is known only from a single specimen that the US Department of Agriculture plant explorer Frank Meyer found in a private garden in Fengtai near Beijing in 1908 and sent home. Alfred Rehder of the Arnold Arboretum formally described it in 1913.

Syringa vulgaris is native to the rocky hills of the Balkans — Bulgaria, Romania, Serbia, North Macedonia, and Albania. The plant reached western Europe in the 16th century via the Ottoman imperial gardens: Holy Roman Empire ambassador Ogier Ghiselin de Busbecq brought lilacs (and tulips).

Tagetes erecta is native to Mexico and Central America, where it was cultivated and culturally important long before European contact. Its Nahuatl name is cempasúchil ('twenty-flower' or 'many-petal flower').

The French Marigold is, confusingly, not French — it is native to Mexico and Guatemala. Its use as a companion plant in vegetable gardens to deter root-knot nematodes is supported by solid scientific evidence.

Signet marigold (also called lemon gem marigold) is native to Mexico and Central America. A small delicate marigold species producing tiny 2 cm single-form flowers in pure gold, lemon-yellow, and tangerine-orange on finely-dissected lacy foliage — distinct from the larger African and French marigolds. The flowers are edible with a pleasant citrus-tarragon flavor.

Tamarix ramosissima, saltcedar, is a deciduous shrub or small tree native to Eurasia introduced to the American West in the 1800s as an ornamental and erosion-control plant. Its deep roots tap groundwater, and its salt-excreting leaves poison surrounding soil, transforming riparian habitats along hundreds of thousands of kilometres of western rivers.

Feverfew is a European herb used by Greeks to reduce fever (the name traces to Latin "febrifugia," fever-driver). Clinical trials from the 1980s onward confirmed its use for preventing migraines, and standardized extracts are now sold as over-the-counter migraine prophylactics worldwide.

Common dandelion, native to Eurasia, was introduced globally with European settlement. Its bitter leaves are a traditional spring tonic across Europe, China ('pu gong ying'), and many Indigenous cultures. It is now one of the most widespread weeds worldwide.

English yew is native to Europe, North Africa, and southwest Asia, and is among the longest-lived trees in Europe — several documented specimens exceed 2,000 years old. The English longbow was made exclusively of yew, and churchyard plantings have preserved ancient specimens for over a millennium. All parts except the red aril flesh are highly toxic.

Japanese yew is native to Japan, Korea, northeastern China, and Russian Far East. Siebold and Zuccarini described it in 1846. Hardier than English yew and central to Japanese garden tradition, it is the main source of paclitaxel (Taxol) precursor 10-deacetylbaccatin III for cancer chemotherapy.

Anglojap yew is a hybrid of Japanese yew (T. cuspidata) and English yew (T. baccata), originated around 1900 in Hatfield, Massachusetts by T.D. Hatfield. It combines the cold-hardiness of Japanese yew with the refined form of English yew. Cultivars 'Hicksii' and 'Densiformis' are landscape workhorses across North America.

Orange bells is a warm-climate shrub of Bolivia, Peru, and Argentina whose clusters of tubular orange and yellow flowers feed hummingbirds. Hybrids with the US native Tecoma stans have produced many of the popular Tecoma cultivars in Southwest landscapes.

Tecoma stans is native to a broad American range — from the southwestern United States (southern Arizona, New Mexico, southernmost Texas), through all of Mexico, Central America, and into significant parts of tropical and subtropical South America.

Fringecup is a Pacific Northwest shade perennial bearing slender spikes of small fringed bell flowers that age from cream to pink. Scottish botanist Archibald Menzies collected it in the 1790s, and its genus name is an anagram of Mitella, a related genus.

Japanese ternstroemia or cleyera is native to southern Japan, Korea, China, and Southeast Asia. Sprague formalized the name. Often sold as Cleyera japonica—and frequently confused with that species—its broader reddish new growth and rounded denser habit distinguish it. Widely planted in the southeastern US as a foundation evergreen.

Powdery alligator-flag (also called hardy water canna) is native to the southeastern United States and eastern Mexico. Its large paddle-shaped blue-green leaves on tall stems and panicles of small dark-purple flowers give it dramatic tropical appearance while remaining cold-hardy to USDA zone 6 — unusual among tropical-look water plants.

Columbine meadow-rue is a European mountain perennial described by Linnaeus in 1753. The epithet refers to its columbine-like foliage. It has been grown in cottage gardens since at least the 16th century for airy plumes of lavender-pink flowers.

Chinese meadow rue was discovered by French missionary Père Jean-Marie Delavay in Yunnan in the 1880s. Its airy lavender flowers with cream stamens made it a prized plant in Edwardian gardens; 'Hewitt's Double' from around 1900 remains the classic cultivar.

Lavender mist meadow-rue, native to Japan, was described by Franchet and Savatier in 1878 and named for French botanist Alfred de Rochebrune. It reached US gardens by the mid-20th century and is prized as a tall, airy back-of-border perennial.

Greenthread is a thread-leaved annual or short-lived perennial of the US Southern Plains and northern Mexico whose golden-yellow daisies carpet roadsides in spring. Indigenous peoples brewed its leaves as a tea known as Navajo tea or cota.

Northern white-cedar (also called arborvitae) is native to eastern North America. In 1535-1536, Iroquoian people of Stadacona (modern Quebec City) taught Jacques Cartier's scurvy-stricken crew to prepare a vitamin-C-rich tea from the tree, saving the expedition — one of the earliest documented transfers of Indigenous medicinal knowledge to Europeans.

Thunbergia alata is native to the tropical and subtropical highlands of eastern Africa — primarily Kenya, Tanzania, Ethiopia, Uganda, and parts of adjacent countries — growing in montane forest edges, savanna margins, and disturbed open ground at 500-2000 meters elevation.

Thunbergia grandiflora is native to a wide arc of South and mainland Southeast Asia — India, Nepal, Bangladesh, Myanmar, southern China, Thailand, Laos, Vietnam, and the Malay Peninsula — where it grows as a robust vigorous woody climbing vine of tropical forest edges and river margins.

Dahlberg daisy is a small Texan and Mexican annual whose fine-textured lacy foliage carries miniature yellow daisy flowers. Heat- and drought-tolerant, it is a classic cottage-garden and xeric border edging plant across the US Sunbelt.

Creeping thyme or mother-of-thyme is a prostrate aromatic subshrub native to Europe. Anton Opiz described it in 1824. Forms dense 5-10 cm mats with pink-purple flowers in early summer. Used as fragrant groundcover, lawn substitute, and between stepping stones.

Woolly thyme is a densely silver-wooly prostrate thyme of uncertain wild origin, possibly a hybrid. Ronniger described it in 1924. Forms flat 2-3 cm silvery mats with sparse pink flowers. Prized as ornamental groundcover for rock gardens and crevices rather than culinary use.

Wild thyme (also called creeping thyme or mother-of-thyme) is native across Europe and northern Asia. A low aromatic mat-forming groundcover whose flowers are intensely attractive to bees, it has been the source of "wild thyme honey" since antiquity and appears as "Thymus serpyllum" in Titania's famous bower in Shakespeare's A Midsummer Night's Dream.

Common thyme is native to the western Mediterranean and has been a culinary and medicinal herb since Egyptian and Greek antiquity. Thymol, the primary antimicrobial compound, was isolated in 1719 and remains a key ingredient in Listerine mouthwash and hospital disinfectants.
