Lemon thyme is a hybrid between Thymus pulegioides and Thymus vulgaris cultivated since the 17th century. Schreber described it in 1774. Low aromatic subshrub with lemon-scented leaves used culinarily. Popular cultivars include 'Silver Queen', 'Aureus', and 'Doone Valley'.

Foamflower is an eastern North American woodland native described by Linnaeus in 1753. Used by the Iroquois as a mouth and eye wash, it became popular in 20th-century shade gardens and is the parent of many Heuchera-Tiarella hybrids (×Heucherella).

Threeleaf foamflower is a western North American woodland perennial described by Linnaeus in 1753. Indigenous peoples of the Pacific Northwest used it for oral and skin complaints. It is a quieter garden relative of the more common T. cordifolia.

Tiarella wherryi is native to the southern Appalachians in Tennessee, North Carolina, Alabama, and Georgia. It was long treated as a variety or subspecies of T. cordifolia before being recognized as a distinct clumping species.

Tibouchina urvilleana is native to the Atlantic Forest of southeastern Brazil, growing as a small shrub or shrub-tree 2-5 meters tall in open coastal and montane forest. The species epithet honors Jules Dumont d'Urville.

American basswood is native to eastern North America. Described by Linnaeus in 1753, its fragrant summer flowers produce exceptional linden honey, and Indigenous peoples used the strong fibrous inner bark for cordage, rope, and woven mats.

Little-leaf linden is native to Europe and has immense cultural weight in Germany ("die Linde,") where village linden trees served as meeting points, courts, and dance venues. Linden flowers produce the finest European honey and a traditional calmative tea.

Silver linden is native to southeastern Europe and western Asia. Named by Conrad Moench in 1785, its silvery-white leaf undersides produce a distinctive flashing effect in wind. Nectar from its flowers can cause fatal narcosis in bumblebees, a documented ecological phenomenon.

A tiny air plant native to Central America, famous for turning bright red during its once-in-a-lifetime blooming event. Grows rootlessly on tree branches, absorbing water and nutrients entirely through specialized leaf scales (trichomes).

Tillandsia usneoides has one of the largest native ranges of any bromeliad, extending from Virginia south through the Gulf Coast and the Caribbean to northern Argentina, where it drapes from trees in humid subtropical and tropical forest.

Mexican sunflower is a tall Mexican and Central American annual whose fiery orange daisies fuel migrating monarchs bound for Michoacán wintering grounds. It has become a cornerstone pollinator plant for US meadow and cutting gardens.

Piggyback plant, or youth-on-age, is a Pacific Northwest shade perennial whose new plantlets sprout at the junction of leaf and petiole. The genus honors William Fraser Tolmie, a 19th-century surgeon-botanist of the Hudson's Bay Company.

Wishbone flower is a Southeast Asian annual bearing tubular flowers with wishbone-shaped stamens. French botanist Eugene Fournier lent his name to the species, which remains a go-to shade bedding plant for long hot summers.

California nutmeg is endemic to California. Torrey described it in 1854, named in his own honor by Arnott. The aromatic seeds were roasted and eaten by indigenous peoples. Unrelated to true nutmeg (Myristica), the species is a relict of a once-widespread Miocene genus now reduced to six surviving species worldwide.

Toxicodendron diversilobum, Pacific or western poison oak, is a deciduous shrub or climbing vine native to western North America from southern British Columbia through the California coast ranges and Sierra Nevada to northern Baja California.

Eastern poison ivy is a climbing or creeping perennial native to North America whose leaves and sap contain urushiol, an oily allergen causing severe contact dermatitis in most humans. Indigenous peoples used it cautiously for dye and medicine; today it is a known hazard in gardens and woodlands.

Star jasmine (also called Confederate jasmine) is native to East and Southeast Asia, despite the regional American "Confederate" name. Its intensely sweet-scented white pinwheel flowers and glossy evergreen foliage make it one of the most popular fragrant evergreen climbers in warm-temperate gardens worldwide.

Native to southeastern Brazil and Argentina, Small-leaf Spiderwort has become one of the world's worst shade-tolerant invasive weeds, smothering native forest floor in New Zealand, Australia, and parts of the US Southeast.

Wandering Jew (now often called Inch Plant to avoid the offensive folk name) is native to Mexico and Central America. A fast-growing trailing houseplant, it is an invasive weed in many frost-free climates worldwide.

Salsify, or oyster plant, is a Mediterranean biennial cultivated since antiquity for its edible taproot. Romans valued it, and it spread through medieval European gardens, reaching colonial America by the 1700s. Its delicate oyster-like flavor once made it a fashionable winter vegetable before falling into obscurity.

Lesser yellow trefoil, native to Europe and western Asia, was described by Sibthorp in 1794. With its small three-lobed leaves, it is widely considered the 'true' shamrock of Ireland and is the species most often displayed on St. Patrick's Day.

Red clover, native to Europe and western Asia, is the state flower of Vermont (1894) and the national flower of Denmark. Long used as fodder and a traditional herbal medicine for coughs and menopausal complaints, it is now a major subject of phytoestrogen research.

White clover, native to Europe and western Asia, was deliberately introduced worldwide as a pasture legume. Celtic and later Christian traditions made its three leaflets a symbol of the Trinity; rare four-leaved sports became symbols of good luck across Western cultures.

Trillium cuneatum is the most common sessile trillium of the southeastern United States, ranging primarily through Tennessee, northern Georgia, Alabama, Kentucky, and the Carolinas. French-Sicilian-American polymath Constantine Samuel Rafinesque-Schmaltz named it in 1840; his prolific but.

Yellow trillium is a sessile trillium native to the southern Appalachians with lemon-scented yellow flowers. Described by Muhlenberg in 1813, its fragrance and golden color set it apart from its mostly maroon sessile cousins, making it a choice woodland garden plant.

Garden nasturtium is native to the Andes (Peru, Ecuador, Colombia, Bolivia). Brought to Spain in the 16th century, it spread through European kitchen gardens as a peppery edible flower and leaf, and was a favorite of Claude Monet's Giverny garden. The whole plant has a watercress-like taste.

Dwarf nasturtium is a low compact ancestor of the familiar garden nasturtium, introduced to Spain from Peru by 16th-century explorers. Its peppery edible flowers and leaves have garnished European tables since the first accounts of Monardes in 1569.

Canary creeper is a tendril-climbing Peruvian vine with fringed yellow canary-like flowers. Introduced to European gardens in the 18th century, it remains a charming climbing annual for trellises, screens, and cottage-garden-style plantings.

Mountain hemlock is native to the coastal mountains of western North America. Bongard described it in 1832 based on specimens from Sitka, Alaska, collected by Karl Heinrich Mertens. Distinguished from other hemlocks by needles arranged around the shoot rather than flat, it is the dominant subalpine tree of the Cascades and coastal Alaska.

Tulipa 'Queen of Night' was bred in the Netherlands in 1944 and became the defining near-black tulip of Western horticulture. It remains the standard reference for 'black tulip' and is still one of the most widely distributed tulips in commerce.

Lady tulip is native to Iran, Afghanistan, and Kashmir. Named for Carolus Clusius who helped introduce tulips to Europe in the 1590s, it naturalized in Renaissance Italian and Iberian gardens and became a classic species tulip for warm-climate gardens.

The Garden Tulip triggered the world's first documented speculative asset bubble — 'Tulip Mania' in 1636–37 Netherlands, when single bulbs sold for the price of a canal house. Descended from Ottoman Turkish cultivated stock.

Tulipa greigii grows wild on stony slopes of the Tien Shan mountains in Kazakhstan and Kyrgyzstan. Russian botanist Eduard Regel described it in 1873 in honor of Samuel Alexeievich Greig, then president of the Imperial Russian Horticultural Society in St.

Tulipa kaufmanniana grows wild on rocky slopes of the Tien Shan mountains in Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, and Uzbekistan. Russian botanist Eduard Regel described it in 1877, naming it for General Konstantin Petrovich von Kaufmann, Russian governor-general of Turkestan and sponsor of botanical.

Tulipa tarda grows wild in rocky montane slopes of Kazakhstan, Tajikistan, and adjacent Central Asia. Russian botanist Alexei Stapf described it in 1933 based on collections from Central Asian expeditions.

Coltsfoot is a creeping perennial native to Europe and western Asia whose yellow dandelion-like flowers appear in early spring before the leaves. Its Latin name derives from tussis (cough), for ancient respiratory uses. Modern regulators caution against internal use due to liver-toxic alkaloids.

Common cattail (also called broadleaf cattail or bulrush) is a circumboreal wetland plant, native across North America, Europe, and Asia. Described as "the supermarket of the swamp," virtually every part is edible or utile — pollen for flour, young shoots as vegetables, rhizomes as starch, and leaves and seed-fluff for weaving and stuffing.

American elm was the defining shade tree of early 20th-century American streets, its characteristic vase-shaped arching branches forming cathedral canopies over countless Main Streets. Dutch elm disease, arriving in North America in 1928, destroyed roughly 75% of mature American elms by the 1980s.

Chinese elm or lacebark elm is native to China, Korea, and Japan. Described by Jacquin in 1798, it became increasingly important in American horticulture following the Dutch elm disease catastrophe, as it is highly resistant to the disease and features exfoliating mottled bark.

Slippery elm is native to eastern North America. Described by Frederick Pursh in 1814, its mucilaginous inner bark was a critical Indigenous medicinal plant and remains widely used herbally. Revolutionary War soldiers chewed bark for sustenance at Valley Forge.

California bay laurel (also called Oregon myrtle, pepperwood, or "myrtlewood") is native to California and southwestern Oregon. Produces pungent aromatic leaves used as substitute for Mediterranean bay in cooking, though stronger and camphor-scented. The beautifully figured wood is the exclusive raw material for Oregon Coast "myrtlewood" tourist-craft products.

Namaqua parachute daisy is a South African annual whose spring daisies carpet the west coast flower season alongside Namaqualand daisies. Introduced to European gardens in the 18th century, it remains a cool-season cottage garden and Mediterranean bedding annual.

Urtica dioica, stinging nettle, is a perennial native to Eurasia with stinging hairs that inject histamine and formic acid. Despite the sting, it has been gathered for thousands of years as spring greens, fibre for cloth, and herbal medicine. Bronze Age textiles made from nettle fibre survive in European burials.

The highbush blueberry is native to eastern North America and was the first blueberry domesticated — by USDA botanist Frederick Coville working with Elizabeth White of New Jersey, releasing the first commercial cultivar in 1916. Nearly all commercial blueberries worldwide derive from their work.

Vaccinium macrocarpon is native to acidic bogs and wetlands of northeastern North America, from Nova Scotia to North Carolina. Indigenous Eastern Woodlands peoples — particularly Wampanoag in Massachusetts, Lenape in New Jersey.

Valerian is a European perennial whose root has been used as a sedative since Greek and Roman antiquity. Modern clinical trials support its traditional use for insomnia, and valerian root extract is among the most widely sold OTC sleep-aid supplements worldwide.

White inside-out flower is a Pacific Northwest woodland perennial whose nodding flowers have strongly reflexed petals, appearing turned inside out. American botanist Joseph Decaisne honored Captain George Vancouver with the genus name in 1839.

Blue vanda is native to northeastern India, Myanmar, and southwest China. Famous for one of the purest true-blue flowers in the orchid world — rare among flowers of any kind. Discovered by William Griffith in 1837 in the Khasi Hills of Assam, it became a Victorian horticultural sensation and today is a CITES Appendix I protected species due to wild collection pressure.

Verbascum thapsus, common mullein, is a biennial native to Europe and western Asia with a rosette of soft, woolly leaves and a tall yellow flower spike. Old World herbalists used the leaves for coughs and the dried stalks dipped in tallow as torches. It is now a familiar weed of disturbed ground across North America.

Verbena bonariensis, commonly called tall verbena, Argentinian vervain, or purpletop vervain, is an erect perennial native to tropical South America, with the epithet bonariensis honoring Buenos Aires, Argentina.

Rose verbena, now Glandularia canadensis, is a sprawling native of the eastern and central United States producing mounds of fragrant rose-pink flower clusters. Colonial and 19th-century gardens welcomed it, and cultivars remain staples of southern landscapes.

Common vervain is a Eurasian herb sacred to Druids, Romans, and early Christians. Called "herb of the cross" for its reputed role at the Crucifixion and used in folk-magic rituals across Europe. Modern research has identified calming glycosides that support its long use as a nervine tea.

Slender vervain, or sandpaper vervain, is a low spreading South American perennial that colonizes dry sandy soil with mats of purple flowers on short stems. It has naturalized widely in the southern United States and is used in meadow and prairie plantings.

Garden verbena is a hybrid complex developed since the early 1800s from several South American species. English and European breeders crossed Verbena peruviana, V. phlogiflora, V. incisa, and V. platensis to create the hundreds of bedding cultivars grown today.

Vernonia noveboracensis is native to moist meadows, open wetland edges, and riparian habitats throughout the eastern and central United States, from Massachusetts to Alabama. It is one of the tallest native perennial wildflowers of eastern North America.

Slender speedwell, native to Turkey and the Caucasus, was introduced to British gardens in 1808 and to the US in the 1920s as a rock garden ornamental. Its creeping habit let it escape gardens and become a troublesome sterile-spreading lawn weed across the northern US.

Persian speedwell, native to Iran and the Caucasus, was described by Poiret in 1808 and has spread globally as one of the most common cool-season annual lawn weeds. Its small sky-blue flowers with white throats are among the earliest garden blooms of spring.

Prostrate speedwell is a European native in the plantain family. Linnaeus described it in 1762. Forms 5-15 cm mats with profuse blue flower spikes in late spring. 'Trehane', 'Heavenly Blue', and 'Mrs. Holt' are widely cultivated selections for rock gardens.

Creeping speedwell is a Caucasus native. M. Bieberstein described it in 1808. 'Georgia Blue' (also sold as 'Oxford Blue') was discovered by Roy Lancaster in the Republic of Georgia in 1979 and has become one of the most popular groundcover veronicas with intense gentian-blue flowers.

Sweet viburnum or Japanese cheese wood is native to southern Japan, Korea, and Taiwan. K. Koch formalized the name. Large glossy corrugated leaves and enormous fragrant white flower panicles make it one of the most dramatic of all viburnums—widely planted as a screening evergreen across the southeastern United States.

Koreanspice viburnum is native to Korea and Japan's Tsushima Islands. Hemsley described it in 1888 based on specimens collected by William Richard Carles. Its tight clusters of pink-budded white flowers emit a powerfully sweet clove-spice fragrance in mid-spring that has made it a cornerstone of fragrant spring gardens worldwide.

Arrowwood viburnum is an eastern North American native described by Linnaeus in 1753. Indigenous peoples used its straight stems for arrow shafts, giving the plant its common name. Modern breeding has produced cultivars prized for blue fruit and fall color.

Fragrant winter viburnum is native to northern China. Stearn honored plant explorer Reginald Farrer in the name (1966), correcting earlier V. fragrans which was a later homonym. Blooming from November through March with richly fragrant pink flowers, it brings winter perfume to cold-climate gardens.

Japanese viburnum is native to Japan and southern Korea. C.P. Thunberg's material was formalized by Sprengel in 1825. Thick, leathery, glossy evergreen leaves and rounded clusters of fragrant white spring flowers followed by red-to-black berries make it a robust structural evergreen for mild-winter landscapes.

Possumhaw viburnum, native to the US southeast, was described by Linnaeus in 1753. Its glossy leaves and pink-to-blue fruit transitions made it a horticultural breakthrough after the 1991 introduction of Donald Egolf's cultivar 'Winterthur' at the US National Arboretum.

Guelder rose (European cranberry bush) is native across Europe and northern Asia. Its red autumn berries and the "snowball" cultivar (V. opulus 'Roseum') have been a fixture of European gardens for centuries. In Ukrainian culture, kalyna is a national symbol of homeland, motherhood, and resilience.

Doublefile viburnum is native to Japan and China. Thunberg described it in 1784. The epithet plicatum (folded, pleated) refers to the deeply corrugated leaves. Horizontal branching and lacecap or snowball flower clusters held above the branches create one of the most architectural flowering shrubs in horticulture.

Leatherleaf viburnum is native to western and central China. Hemsley described it in 1906 based on E.H. Wilson's collections. Long, narrow, deeply corrugated leathery leaves with densely felted undersides—perhaps the most leathery foliage of any viburnum—give it bold year-round presence in shaded gardens.

Laurustinus is an evergreen Mediterranean viburnum flowering in winter and early spring, producing pink-budded white flower clusters followed by metallic blue berries. Cultivated in European gardens since antiquity, it has been a staple of Mediterranean and mild-winter Atlantic-climate landscapes for centuries.

American cranberry bush, a native of northern North America, was described by Thomas Walter in 1788 as Viburnum trilobum and is now often treated as V. opulus var. americanum. Its tart red fruit was used by Indigenous peoples as a jelly substitute for true cranberries.

Bodnant viburnum is a hybrid of V. farreri and V. grandiflorum, raised at Bodnant Garden in North Wales in 1935 by head gardener Charles Puddle. The cultivar 'Dawn' combines the winter-blooming fragrance of V. farreri with the larger, more richly pink flowers of V. grandiflorum and is a worldwide garden classic.

The fava or broad bean is one of the oldest cultivated plants, with remains at Neolithic Near Eastern sites dated 8000 BCE. Pythagoras famously forbade his followers from eating it — possibly due to favism, a genetic G6PD deficiency that causes severe hemolytic anemia after fava consumption.

Giant Amazon water lily is native to the backwater channels of the Amazon basin. Its enormous 2-3 meter diameter leaves, strong enough to support a small child, were a sensation in Victorian horticulture after first successful cultivation at Chatsworth in 1849; the leaf's structural rib pattern inspired the Crystal Palace roof design of 1851.

Greater periwinkle is native to the Mediterranean and southwestern Europe. Larger-leaved and more vigorous than V. minor, it became a popular warm-climate groundcover in 19th-century British and American horticulture. In California, Oregon, and Australia it is now a serious invasive of riparian and woodland understory habitats.

Vinca minor is native to central and southern Europe, from Portugal to Ukraine and south into Turkey and the Caucasus. It was cultivated in Roman villa gardens (Pliny the Elder describes it as "vinca pervinca") and became one of the foundational evergreen groundcovers of medieval European monastery.

Viola 'Molly Sanderson' was selected by British gardener Molly Sanderson at her Northern Ireland garden in the 1980s and introduced commercially in the 1990s. It remains the standard reference black-flowered viola and a modern shade-garden design classic.

Horned violet is a Pyrenean perennial whose small-flowered crosses with garden pansies produced the modern viola bedding type. Pastel tufted violas now dominate spring and fall bedding, a legacy of Scottish and English 19th-century breeding work.

Sweet violet has been grown for its fragrance since classical antiquity. Napoleon adopted it as his personal emblem (becoming "Papa la Violette" to loyalists after Elba), and 19th-century Parma and Toulouse built entire perfume industries on violet-flower production.

Common blue violet, the state flower of Illinois, New Jersey, Rhode Island, and Wisconsin, is a familiar spring perennial of eastern North American lawns and woodlands. Indigenous peoples and early settlers used its leaves and flowers as food and medicine.

Heartsease is a European annual and progenitor of the garden pansy. Shakespeare's love-potion flower in A Midsummer Night's Dream ("love-in-idleness"), it was crossed with V. lutea and V. altaica in early 19th-century England to create the modern pansy.

Garden pansy is a hybrid developed in English horticulture in the 1810s-1830s by crossing Viola tricolor with V. lutea and V. altaica. Named for Swedish botanist Veit Brecher Wittrock. The "face" pattern of contrasting colors — bred into selections by Admiral William Gambier's gardener from 1812 — is the signature ornamental feature.

Chaste tree is native to the Mediterranean and central Asia. Named from Latin agnus (lamb) and castus (chaste), it was used in ancient Greek medicine and by medieval monks to suppress libido. Its summer purple flower spikes attract pollinators in hot dry climates.

Crimson glory vine is native to Japan, Korea, Sakhalin, and southern Kuril Islands. Pulliat published the name in 1883, honoring Mme. Coignet who brought seed to France. Its enormous heart-shaped leaves turn spectacular shades of scarlet and crimson in autumn—likely the most brilliant fall color of any climbing vine.

Fox grape is native to eastern North America. Linnaeus described it in 1753. The ancestor of the Concord grape—selected in 1849 by Ephraim Wales Bull in Concord, Massachusetts—and of all the classic American slip-skin grapes (Niagara, Catawba) whose foxy flavor defines American grape juice, jelly, and Welch's Concord welcome.

Vitis vinifera was domesticated in the South Caucasus — modern Georgia, Armenia, and eastern Turkey — approximately 8,000-10,000 years ago from wild Vitis vinifera subsp. sylvestris. A 2017 archaeological study of pottery residues at Gadachrili Gora in Georgia dated the earliest wine-making to 6000.

Siberian barren strawberry is a strawberry-like groundcover of Eurasia. Stephan described it as Dalibarda ternata in 1800; Fritsch transferred it to Waldsteinia. Forms evergreen mats with yellow spring flowers. Genus named for Austrian botanist Franz von Waldstein.

Weigela is native to northern China, Korea, and Japan, introduced to Western horticulture in the 1840s by plant hunter Robert Fortune. Its arching branches of pink, rose, or white trumpet flowers in late spring, and later-developed dark-foliage cultivars like "Wine & Roses," have made it a staple mid-size flowering shrub.

Middendorff's weigela, from the Russian Far East, Korea, and Japan, was described by Carrière in 1866 from collections made by Russian explorer Alexander von Middendorff. Notable for yellow bell-shaped flowers, rare in the genus, it reached Western gardens in the 1850s.

Early weigela is native to Korea and the Russian Far East. Bailey formalized the name. Blooming two to three weeks earlier than the common W. florida, its pink tubular flowers appear in mid to late spring and are a preferred early-season nectar source for newly returned hummingbirds in northern gardens.

Korean weigela is endemic to Korea, described by Japanese botanist Nakai in 1909. Long cultivated in Korean gardens as 'byeongggot-namu', it was introduced to Western horticulture via Japanese and US collecting trips in the 20th century.

Japanese wisteria is native to Honshu and was introduced to the West via Philipp Franz von Siebold in the 1830s. The legendary 1870s Ashikaga Flower Park specimen in Tochigi is one of the most celebrated wisterias on Earth, drawing hundreds of thousands to see it in bloom each May.

Chinese wisteria is native to central and eastern China. Introduced to Western horticulture in 1816 by John Reeves, an East India Company tea inspector, it became the defining pergola and veranda vine of Victorian and Edwardian English and American estates. Now considered a serious invasive species in the southeastern United States, where it strangles native forest trees.

Xanthium strumarium, common cocklebur, is an annual native to the Americas whose spiny burs cling to fur and clothing. Its seeds and seedlings contain toxic carboxyatractyloside, historically poisoning livestock. The burs famously inspired the design of Velcro when Swiss engineer George de Mestral studied their hooks in 1941.

Xanthosoma sagittifolium is native to northern South America and the Caribbean, where it was cultivated by pre-Columbian peoples for its edible starchy corms. Unlike the Old World taro (Colocasia esculenta, called "old cocoyam" in West Africa), Xanthosoma is a New World aroid — a distinct.

Strawflower is the classic Australian everlasting whose papery bracts retain color for years after drying. Victorian dried arrangements popularized the species, and modern breeding gave rise to the container and cutting-garden Granvia and Dreamtime series.

Beargrass is a western North American perennial whose tough leaves Indigenous peoples wove into watertight baskets traded across the continent. Lewis and Clark documented exceptional Chinook and Salish basketry, and the plant remains central to Pacific Northwest cultural arts.

The Spineless Yucca is native to Mexico and Guatemala. Unlike other yuccas, it has soft leaves without the dangerous sharp tips that give the genus its reputation. Its flowers and young flower stalks are a traditional Mexican food (flor de izote).

The ZZ Plant is native to eastern Africa from Kenya to Zanzibar. Nearly unknown as a houseplant before Dutch nurseries began mass propagation around 1996, it rose rapidly to become one of the defining low-light office plants of the 2000s and 2010s.

Calla lily (also called arum lily) is native to southern Africa. Despite its English common name it is neither a true lily nor a true calla (both of which are different plant families) — it is a South African aroid. The national flower of Ethiopia by some accounts and one of the most universally grown cut flowers worldwide, signature of Art Nouveau and Art Deco floral aesthetics.

Yellow calla lily was described by Engler in 1915 but is known only in cultivation — all attempts to find wild populations in South Africa have failed. Its clear yellow spathes and silver-spotted leaves made it the critical yellow parent of all modern colored hybrid callas.

Pink calla lily, native to KwaZulu-Natal and Swaziland in South Africa, was described by John Gilbert Baker in 1891, honoring the Moravian explorer Anton Rehmann. Its slender pink spathes broke the white-only calla tradition and became a parent of all colored hybrid callas.

Maize was domesticated from teosinte (Z. mays subsp. parviglumis) in the Balsas River valley of central Mexico around 9,000 years ago. Transformed from a tiny-kernelled grass into the world's largest cereal crop, it is the engineering keystone of the Americas.

Chinese zelkova is native to central and northern China. Described by Hemsley in 1894, it is rarer in Western cultivation than the Japanese Z. serrata. The flaking orange-cinnamon bark on mature trees is highly ornamental, and the species shares resistance to Dutch elm disease with its congeners.

Ginger is native to maritime Southeast Asia, though wild populations have never been identified — suggesting a very ancient domestication. Traded as a spice between India, China, and the Mediterranean since at least 500 BCE, ginger was one of the earliest spices to reach Europe and remains one of the most important global spices, flavorings, and medicinal herbs.

Zinnia angustifolia is native to the deserts and semi-arid grasslands of northern Mexico (particularly Chihuahua, Sonora, and Durango) and adjacent southwestern United States (southern Arizona and New Mexico).

Zinnias are native to the semi-arid grasslands of Mexico. German botanist Johann Gottfried Zinn first described them in 1753, giving the genus its name. Zinnias are the first flower to bloom in space, aboard the International Space Station in 2016.

Prairie zinnia, or Rocky Mountain zinnia, is a low-growing perennial of the US Great Plains and Mexico. Indigenous peoples used it medicinally, and it now anchors native meadow and xeriscape plantings from New Mexico to Nebraska.

Mexican zinnia (Persian carpet zinnia) is native to Mexico. Named for 19th-century German nurseryman Friedrich Adolph Haage. Distinctive for its small bicolored flowers in intense combinations of red, orange, yellow, and mahogany — unlike any other zinnia — often with dark markings suggesting Persian carpet patterns.

Zahara zinnia, marketed as Zinnia marylandica, is a disease-resistant hybrid developed by USDA breeders at Beltsville, Maryland. The hybrid crosses Zinnia angustifolia with Z. violacea and has transformed US bedding zinnia production since its 2004 Zahara release.

Zinnia peruviana, the Peruvian zinnia, is an annual herb native to a broad range from the southwestern United States through Mexico, Central America, and into the Andes of South America. Linnaeus originally described it in 1753 as Chrysogonum peruvianum before recognizing its true placement in.
